Both specs conflate testable performance contracts (keep normative) with platform-specific implementation details (move to implementation notes appendix).

**runtime-kernel/spec.md — move to implementation notes:**
- Line 33: Platform thread-priority enum names (SCHED_RR, QOS_CLASS_USER_INTERACTIVE, THREAD_PRIORITY_TIME_CRITICAL) — keep 'elevated to high priority', drop enum names
- Lines 212-218: Named software backends (llvmpipe, WARP), specific env var (HEADLESS_FORCE_SOFTWARE) — keep 'software fallback when available', drop backend names
- Lines 220-235: Exact degradation ratios (2x coalesce, 50% texture scale, 512x512 threshold) — keep the 5-level ladder and level semantics, move exact ratios to reference implementation
- Line 331: 100ms post-revocation delay — make configurable, keep 'bounded cleanup delay'
- Line 334: 500ms drain, 200ms flush — make configurable, keep ordered shutdown sequence

**runtime-kernel/spec.md — KEEP normative (ChatGPT is wrong here):**
- Frame budget < 16.6ms p99 — this IS the spec
- 14ms trigger / 12ms recovery — testable degradation contract
- Channel capacity 256/512 — debatable, but bounded channels are load-bearing
- Session memory < 64KB — testable resource contract
- Resource envelope defaults — testable, configurable anyway

**policy-arbitration/spec.md — move to implementation notes:**
- Line 275: Sub-10us lookup budgets (< 5us capability check, < 10us attention, < 20us zone contention) — these are cache-line-dependent and not portable. Keep the relative ordering (capability < attention < zone < per-mutation < per-frame)
- Line 253: 5-minute freeze default — UX tuning, not standard

**policy-arbitration/spec.md — KEEP normative:**
- Per-mutation < 50us, per-frame < 200us — these protect frame budget
- Human override < 1 frame, privacy transition < 2 frames — user-visible contracts

The goal is not to remove precision but to distinguish between 'what the system must achieve' (normative) and 'how a reference implementation achieves it' (appendix).
title: Separate normative budgets from implementation mechanisms in runtime-kernel and policy-arbitration

close_reason: PR #128 merged after PR review. Attention budget and quiet hours implementation complete with review feedback incorporated.
description:
# Attention Budget and Quiet Hours

Implement attention management per policy-arbitration/spec.md.

## Spec Requirements

### Requirement: Level 4 Attention Management (spec lines 143-166)
Five interruption classes matching the InterruptionClass enum (RFC 0010 §3.1):
- SILENT: always passes, never counted against budget
- LOW: discarded during quiet hours (too stale by quiet hours exit to be useful)
- NORMAL: queued during quiet hours; delivered when quiet hours end (FIFO order)
- HIGH: passes through quiet hours unless pass_through_class raises threshold; subject to attention budget
- CRITICAL: always passes, bypasses both quiet hours and attention budget; only the runtime may emit CRITICAL

Attention budget MUST track rolling per-agent and per-zone counters over the last 60 seconds with configurable limits:
- Default per-agent: 20 per minute
- Default per-zone: 10 per minute (30 per minute for Stack-policy zones per RFC 0010 §7)

When budget exhausted: mutations MUST be coalesced (latest-wins within agent+zone key).

Attention budget check MUST complete in < 10us.

Note: RFC 0009 §11.5 uses older doctrine names (Gentle/Normal/Urgent) and older defaults (6/12); RFC 0010 §3.1 is authoritative for both enum names and default budgets.

### Requirement: Policy Evaluation Latency Budgets (spec lines 294-312, attention sub-check)
- Attention check < 10us

## WHEN/THEN Scenarios (from spec)

- WHEN quiet hours are active and a mutation with NORMAL interruption class arrives THEN the mutation is queued until quiet hours end and then delivered in FIFO order (spec lines 148-150)
- WHEN quiet hours are active and a mutation with LOW interruption class arrives THEN the mutation is discarded (not queued) because LOW content is too stale by quiet hours exit (spec lines 152-154)
- WHEN quiet hours are active and a mutation with HIGH interruption class arrives THEN the mutation passes through quiet hours (unless pass_through_class is set higher than HIGH) (spec lines 156-158)
- WHEN a CRITICAL interruption arrives during quiet hours with an exhausted attention budget THEN the mutation passes through both quiet hours and attention budget (spec lines 160-162)
- WHEN an agent exceeds 20 interruptions per minute THEN subsequent mutations are coalesced (latest-wins) until the budget refills (spec lines 164-166)
- WHEN Level 1 (Safety) enters safe mode while Level 4 (Attention) has queued notifications THEN the queued notifications MUST be discarded; safe mode overrides the delivery queue (spec lines 36-38, cross-level scenario)

## Secondary Spec Reference
- scene-events/spec.md Requirement: Interruption Classification — defines the InterruptionClass enum (SILENT/LOW/NORMAL/HIGH/CRITICAL)
- configuration/spec.md — quiet hours configuration (Epic 7, rig-nolr)

## Test Scenes
- policy_matrix_basic: attention budget enforcement with various interruption classes
- policy_arbitration_collision: attention budget + quiet hours + degradation collision

## Acceptance Criteria
- InterruptionClass enum: SILENT, LOW, NORMAL, HIGH, CRITICAL
- Quiet hours filtering: CRITICAL/HIGH pass through, NORMAL queued (FIFO), LOW discarded, SILENT always passes
- pass_through_class threshold correctly raises the quiet hours pass-through bar
- Rolling 60-second per-agent counter with configurable limit (default 20/min)
- Rolling 60-second per-zone counter with configurable limit (default 10/min, 30/min for Stack-policy zones)
- Budget exhaustion triggers latest-wins coalescing within agent+zone key
- Attention budget check < 10us
- CRITICAL can only be emitted by runtime (not agents)
- Safe mode discards queued notifications (cross-level interaction with bead #1 / bead #3)
- Budget tests pass

## Purity Constraint
Policy evaluation MUST be a pure function over a typed PolicyContext — no side effects. The attention budget is read as input state (current counters, quiet hours active flag, pass_through_class); the pipeline produces Queue/Commit/coalesce decisions. Counter increment is a side effect owned by the caller (frame pipeline), not the policy evaluator.

## Crate/File Location
crates/tze_hud_policy/src/attention.rs — attention budget evaluation, quiet hours logic
crates/tze_hud_policy/src/attention_budget.rs — rolling counter data structure (circular buffer, 60s window)
crates/tze_hud_policy/src/interruption.rs — InterruptionClass enum and classification

## v1-mandatory requirements
Level 4 Attention Management, Policy Evaluation Latency Budgets (attention sub-check < 10us)

## v1-reserved exclusions
- Per-Agent Arbitration Diagnostics RPC (spec lines 376-383) — attention stats via telemetry only in v1

## post-v1 exclusions
- Dynamic Policy Rules (spec lines 367-374) — quiet hours schedule and budget limits are static config in v1
- Viewer Identification Pipeline (configuration/spec.md) — quiet hours triggered by config, not dynamic presence detection

## What this does NOT include
- Arbitration stack data structures (bead #1)
- Per-mutation evaluation pipeline integration (bead #2)
- Per-event / per-frame evaluation pipelines (bead #3)
- Scene-events InterruptionClass protobuf definition (events epic)
- Quiet hours configuration loading (Epic 7, rig-nolr)
- System-shell freeze/safe-mode state machine (Epic 11)
title: Attention budget and quiet hours

description:
Lease governance is the state machine that governs every agent's presence on screen. No agent can mutate the scene without a valid lease; leases have TTL, priority, capability scope, and revocation semantics. Spec reviewers rated this subsystem 9.2/10.

description:
## Problem

The AllOrDefer sync group commit policy has a force-commit escape hatch mentioned in Section 2.4 prose and hinted at in the proto (SyncGroupConfig.max_defer_frames, SyncGroupForceCommitEvent), but the force-commit semantics are not normatively specified. Key gaps:

1. The prose in Section 2.4 says 'the group transitions to AvailableMembers policy for that commit cycle only' on force-commit, but this transitional behavior is not reflected in the proto or Rust data model. There is no field tracking whether a group is in a forced-commit state.

2. max_defer_frames is in the SyncGroupConfig proto (field 4, default 3, 0 = use default) but is never referenced normatively in Section 2.3 (Semantics) or Section 2.4 (Timing Contract) — it only appears in Section 2.4 as an implicit concept ('Maximum deferral: configurable, default 3 frames').

3. The force-commit escape hatch is the only mechanism preventing AllOrDefer from conceptually deadlocking when a member agent is slow or disconnected. Without normative specification, implementors may omit it or implement it inconsistently.

4. What happens to the deferred mutations that were waiting? Are they all applied, or only the available ones? The prose says 'available members mutations are force-applied' but does not specify what happens to the absent members' deferred state — are they cleared, or do they carry over to the next cycle?

## Affected Sections

- Section 2.3 (Semantics) — needs normative reference to max_defer_frames
- Section 2.4 (Timing Contract) — needs detailed force-commit state machine
- Section 7.1 (timing.proto) — SyncGroupConfig.max_defer_frames needs normative documentation

## Doctrine Reference

presence.md: sync-group membership is a tile property; architecture.md: sync groups are first-class timing objects. The atomicity guarantee is a core selling point — its failure mode (force-commit) must be equally well-specified.

## Concrete Change

1. Add a normative subsection '2.4.1 Force-Commit Semantics' that specifies: trigger condition, what happens to pending mutations of present vs absent members, the temporary AvailableMembers behavior, when the group returns to AllOrDefer, and the telemetry event.
2. Reference max_defer_frames by name in Section 2.3 and 2.4 prose.
3. Add a 'deferred_frames_count' tracking field to the Rust SyncGroup struct to support the state machine.

## Rationale

Without normative force-commit semantics, AllOrDefer is a conceptual deadlock risk. This is the single most important sync group edge case.
title: RFC 0003: Normative max_defer_frames / force-commit semantics for AllOrDefer sync groups

description:
## Problem

RFC 0007 §4.3 (Freeze Scene) specifies that agents are not informed when the scene is frozen:
> 'Agents are not informed that the scene is frozen; they continue submitting mutations normally.'

The design rationale is clean sovereignty — the viewer's freeze action is none of the agent's business. However, the feedback identifies a practical concern: under high-throughput conditions, agents will continue generating and submitting mutations that are silently queued (up to 1000 per session) and then dropped when the queue overflows. This wastes agent compute, network bandwidth, and runtime resources on work that will never be applied.

The tradeoff:
- **Silent freeze** (current spec): Sovereignty-pure. Agents cannot detect freeze. But wasteful — agents spend resources generating mutations for a frozen scene.
- **Advisory freeze**: Agents receive a non-binding advisory that the scene is frozen. They can choose to reduce update frequency. The advisory is informational, not a command — agents that ignore it continue to work normally. The freeze still happens regardless.

## Affected Sections
- RFC 0007 §4.3 (Freeze Scene)
- RFC 0005 (Session Protocol — would need a new advisory message type if advisory model is chosen)

## Relevant Doctrine
- architecture.md §Policy arbitration: Human override 'always wins' and 'cannot be intercepted by any policy layer below.' An advisory does not violate this — the freeze still executes unconditionally.
- security.md §Human override: Overrides 'cannot be intercepted, delayed, or vetoed.' An advisory that says 'scene is frozen, consider reducing updates' does not allow interception or veto.
- privacy.md §Agent Isolation: Viewer state is not exposed to agents. However, freeze is an override action, not viewer identity — it is more analogous to lease revocation (which agents are already notified about) than to viewer class.

## Proposed Resolution
Adopt a middle ground: **silent freeze with backpressure signal**. Instead of an explicit 'scene is frozen' advisory (which leaks viewer action intent), use the existing mutation response path:

1. While frozen, mutations are queued as currently specified.
2. When the queue reaches 80% capacity (800/1000 default), the runtime sends a generic MUTATION_QUEUE_PRESSURE warning to the agent via the session stream.
3. If the queue overflows, the runtime sends MUTATION_DROPPED for each shed mutation.
4. Agents that receive MUTATION_QUEUE_PRESSURE can voluntarily reduce submission rate.

This gives agents a natural backpressure signal without revealing that the viewer activated freeze. The same backpressure mechanism would apply in any other scenario where mutations cannot be applied (slow compositor, degradation, etc.), making it a general-purpose facility rather than a freeze-specific leak.

Add this to §4.3 and note the interaction with RFC 0005 session events.
title: RFC 0007: Decide freeze advisory model (silent vs advisory to agents)

description:
Implement canonical capability enforcement per configuration/spec.md Requirement: Capability Vocabulary.

## Spec References
- Requirement: Capability Vocabulary (spec.md lines 149-164, v1-mandatory)

## Canonical v1 Capabilities (17 total, exact snake_case wire-format names)
create_tiles, modify_own_tiles, manage_tabs, manage_sync_groups, upload_resource, read_scene_topology, subscribe_scene_events, overlay_privileges, access_input_events, high_priority_z_order, exceed_default_budgets, read_telemetry, publish_zone:<zone_name>, publish_zone:*, emit_scene_event:<event_name>, resident_mcp, lease:priority:<N>

## WHEN/THEN Acceptance Scenarios
- WHEN agent capabilities include ["create_tiles", "publish_zone:subtitle", "emit_scene_event:doorbell.ring"] THEN accepted (lines 155-156)
- WHEN agent capabilities include "emit_scene_event:system.shutdown" THEN startup fails with CONFIG_RESERVED_EVENT_PREFIX (lines 159-160)
- WHEN agent capabilities include a non-canonical name such as "createTiles", "create-tiles", "tile_create", or any other name not in canonical v1 vocabulary THEN startup fails with CONFIG_UNKNOWN_CAPABILITY identifying the unrecognized name and providing a hint with closest canonical match (e.g., {"unknown": "createTiles", "hint": "did you mean create_tiles?"}) (lines 162-164)

## Legacy Name Rejection
RFC 0009 §8.1 contains older names (read_scene, receive_input, zone_publish) superseded by RFC 0005 Round 14 (rig-b2s). RFC 0006 §6.3 is the canonical authority. Legacy names MUST be rejected with CONFIG_UNKNOWN_CAPABILITY and a hint pointing to the canonical replacement.

## Implementation Location
crates/tze_hud_config/src/capability.rs — canonical vocabulary registry, validation, hint generation

## Immutability Contract
Capability vocabulary is frozen at startup. The set of canonical capabilities is a compile-time constant. Agent capability grants validated at config load time are immutable. No hot-reload of capability definitions.

## v1-mandatory requirements
Capability Vocabulary (all 17 canonical capabilities recognized; non-canonical names rejected with structured error and hint; parameterized capabilities parsed correctly; reserved event prefixes system.* and scene.* rejected)

## v1-reserved exclusions
None for this bead specifically.

## post-v1 exclusions
- Additional capability names beyond the 17 canonical v1 set
- Dynamic capability registration at runtime

## Does NOT include
- TOML parsing/loading (bead #1)
- Display profile resolution (bead #2)
- Per-agent capability grants or agent registration (bead #4 — this bead only validates individual capability names against the vocabulary; bead #4 wires grants to agents)

description:
## Problem

RFC 0002 §2.2 states the main thread calls surface.present() and §2.3 states the compositor thread owns the wgpu Device/Queue and does GPU submit. The current text says: 'The compositor thread owns the wgpu::Device and wgpu::Queue. No other thread touches the device. The main thread holds only the surface handle, which is safe to present() from the main thread on platforms where winit requires it.'

This split is architecturally significant but under-justified. wgpu's threading model has specific constraints:
- wgpu::Queue::submit() and wgpu::Surface::get_current_texture() have specific thread-safety properties
- On some platforms (particularly macOS with Metal), surface acquisition and presentation must happen on the main thread
- The 'read-only surface handle' claim in §1.1's architecture diagram needs substantiation

This is not a bug — the current design is likely correct — but it lacks the architectural decision record that would prevent future implementors from accidentally breaking the invariant.

## Affected Sections

- RFC 0002 §1.1 — 'read-only surface handle for present()'
- RFC 0002 §2.2 — main thread calls present()
- RFC 0002 §2.3 — compositor thread owns Device/Queue
- RFC 0002 §7.3 — surface lifecycle (resize, recreation)
- Appendix A — CompositorSurface trait

## Doctrine Reference

- architecture.md §Rendering: native GPU pipeline — wgpu for cross-platform GPU rendering
- architecture.md §Language: Rust for the core — explicit resource ownership

## Required Change

Add an ADR-style note (can be a subsection of §2 or a note in §10) that:

1. Documents WHY the split exists: winit requires the event loop (and thus present()) on the main thread on macOS/iOS. GPU command encoding is CPU-intensive and must not block input drain.

2. Documents the safety boundary: which wgpu calls are safe from which thread, and how the CompositorSurface trait enforces this.

3. Documents the synchronization mechanism: FrameReadySignal is the handoff point. The main thread must not call present() until the compositor thread signals completion. Race conditions (double-present, present-before-submit) must be structurally impossible.

4. Documents platform-specific constraints: macOS requires main-thread presentation. Other platforms may allow compositor-thread presentation — note this as a future optimization opportunity.
title: RFC 0002: add thread-ownership ADR for surface present() vs GPU submit

description:
session_server.rs lines 2220-2224 explicitly note: 'SceneGraph::grant_lease does not currently persist granted_priority in the lease record (the scene-graph API predates the priority system). The granted_priority value is returned to the agent in LeaseResponse and honored at the session layer, but arbitration based on stored priority is a follow-up task.'

The runtime-kernel spec Requirement: Tile Shedding Order requires sorting by (lease_priority ASC, z_order DESC). The lease-governance spec Requirement: Priority Sort Semantics defines the ordering. Without persisted priority, the degradation ladder cannot correctly shed lowest-priority tiles first.

**What must change:**
1. Add priority field to SceneGraph lease record (LeaseRecord or equivalent)
2. SceneGraph::grant_lease accepts and stores granted_priority
3. Degradation ladder reads stored priority for shedding order
4. Lease renewal preserves priority

**Spec refs:**
- lease-governance/spec.md Requirement: Priority Assignment (lines 49-60, v1-mandatory)
- lease-governance/spec.md Requirement: Priority Sort Semantics (lines 62-69, v1-mandatory)
- runtime-kernel/spec.md Requirement: Tile Shedding Order (degradation ladder uses lease priority)

**v1-mandatory. Files: tze_hud_scene/src/graph.rs (lease record), session_server.rs (grant_lease call)**

close_reason: PR #148 merged after PR review (rig-ty0e). 6-level degradation ladder is now on main.
description:
Implement the 6-level degradation system per runtime-kernel/spec.md Requirement: Degradation Ladder (line 220), Requirement: Degradation Trigger (line 237), Requirement: Degradation Hysteresis (line 250), and Requirement: Tile Shedding Order (line 263).

## Spec Requirements (all v1-mandatory)

### Requirement: Degradation Ladder (line 220)
6-level ladder:
- Level 0 Normal — full quality rendering
- Level 1 Coalesce — reduce outbound SceneEvent frequency for state-stream tiles by configurable ratio (suggested 2x per Implementation Notes line 415)
- Level 2 ReduceTextureQuality — scale down textures whose linear dimensions exceed 512px by configurable factor (suggested 50% per Implementation Notes line 417)
- Level 3 DisableTransparency — force semi-transparent tiles to opaque, skip alpha-blend render passes entirely (Implementation Notes line 418)
- Level 4 ShedTiles — remove lowest-priority tiles from render pass sorted by (lease_priority ASC, z_order DESC)
- Level 5 Emergency — render only chrome layer + highest-priority single tile

### Requirement: Degradation Trigger (line 237)
Evaluation after every frame. Trigger: frame_time_p95 > 14ms measured over rolling 10-frame window (166ms at 60fps). The 10-frame window absorbs transient spikes.

### Requirement: Degradation Hysteresis (line 250)
Recovery requires frame_time_p95 < 12ms sustained over 30-frame window (500ms at 60fps). Recovery one level at a time. 2ms hysteresis band (14ms trigger vs 12ms recovery) prevents oscillation.

### Requirement: Tile Shedding Order (line 263)
Sort tiles by (lease_priority ASC, z_order DESC). Lower lease_priority values (0 = highest priority) preserved first. Within same priority class, higher z_order wins. Highest lease_priority + lowest z_order shed first.

## WHEN/THEN Scenarios from Spec

- WHEN frame_time_p95 > 14ms over 10 frames and runtime is at Normal THEN runtime MUST transition to Level 1 and reduce outbound state-stream notification frequency (line 226)
- WHEN the runtime reaches Level 4 THEN tiles MUST be sorted by (lease_priority ASC, z_order DESC) and lowest-priority tiles removed from render pass while remaining in scene graph (line 230)
- WHEN the runtime reaches Level 5 THEN only chrome layer and single highest-priority tile MUST be rendered; all other tiles visually suppressed (line 234)
- WHEN a single frame exceeds 14ms but 10-frame p95 remains below 14ms THEN runtime MUST NOT trigger degradation (line 243)
- WHEN the 10-frame rolling p95 exceeds 14ms THEN runtime MUST advance one degradation level within 1 frame (line 247)
- WHEN runtime is at Level 2 and frame_time_p95 < 12ms sustained over 30-frame rolling window THEN runtime MUST recover one level to Level 1 (line 256)
- WHEN runtime is at Level 5 THEN reaching Normal MUST require 5 successive 30-frame windows of clean performance (frame_time_p95 < 12ms per window), recovering one level per window (~2.5 seconds total at 60fps) (line 260)
- WHEN runtime enters Level 4 with tiles at priority 0, 1, and 2 THEN priority-2 tiles MUST be shed first, then priority-1, preserving priority-0 tiles (line 269)

## Performance Gates
- Degradation evaluation latency: must not add measurable overhead to frame pipeline (< 100us)
- Rolling window computation: O(1) per frame (ring buffer of frame times)
- Shedding sort: O(n log n) where n = tile count; must complete within Stage 5 Layout Resolve budget (< 1ms p99)

## v1-mandatory
- Requirement: Degradation Ladder (line 220)
- Requirement: Degradation Trigger (line 237)
- Requirement: Degradation Hysteresis (line 250)
- Requirement: Tile Shedding Order (line 263)

## v1-reserved exclusions
None for this bead.

## post-v1 exclusions
- Media Worker Pool (line 373) — no media-related degradation in v1
- Parallel Render Encoding (line 382) — no parallel encoding to degrade to in v1

## Acceptance Criteria
- Budget enforcement tests from budget.rs (991 lines, 23 tests) all pass
- Degradation trigger threshold verified: frame_time_p95 > 14ms over 10-frame window triggers level advance
- Recovery threshold verified: frame_time_p95 < 12ms over 30-frame window triggers one-level recovery
- Transient spike tolerance: single-frame spike does NOT trigger degradation
- Shedding order matches spec: (lease_priority ASC, z_order DESC)
- Level 5 emergency: only chrome + highest-priority single tile rendered
- Level transitions emit structured DegradationEvent for telemetry
- All 6 levels implemented with configurable ratios (coalesce ratio, texture scale factor)

## Relevant Epic 0 test scenes
- max_tiles_stress: degradation trigger under high tile count
- three_agents_contention: priority-based shedding with multiple agents
- coalesced_dashboard: Level 1 coalescing behavior
- overlay_transparency: Level 3 DisableTransparency effect
- mobile_degraded: degradation under constrained profile
- chatty_dashboard_touch: sustained load triggering degradation

## Crate/File Location
- crates/tze_hud_runtime/src/degradation.rs (new — degradation state machine, trigger, hysteresis, shedding)
- crates/tze_hud_runtime/src/budget.rs (existing — 991 lines, 23 tests; frame-time rolling windows)
- crates/tze_hud_runtime/src/pipeline.rs (integration — degradation evaluation after each frame)

## What This Does NOT Include
- Frame pipeline stages (bead #1 — this bead depends on pipeline existing)
- Bounded channels (bead #3)
- Headless mode (bead #4)
- Admission control / per-agent budgets (bead #5 — depends on this bead)
- Window mode implementation
title: 6-level degradation ladder with trigger/hysteresis/shedding

close_reason: Capability vocabulary unified: read_scene->read_scene_topology, receive_input->access_input_events, zone_publish->publish_zone in both RFC 0005 and RFC 0006
description:
## Problem

The capability identifier names used in RFC 0005 (Session/Protocol) §7.1 do not match those defined in RFC 0006 (Configuration) §6.3:

| Subscription Table (RFC 0005 §7.1) | Config RFC (RFC 0006 §6.3) |
|-------------------------------------|----------------------------|
| `read_scene` | `read_scene_topology` |
| `receive_input` | `access_input_events` |
| `zone_publish:<zone>` | `publish_zone:<zone>` |

These are not just naming variants — they are the wire-level identifiers that agents will send in `requested_capabilities` and receive in `granted_capabilities`. If the session RFC and config RFC use different names, agents configured with config-RFC identifiers will fail capability checks at session time, or vice versa.

## Affected Sections

- §7.1 (Subscription Categories table — 'Minimum Capability' column)
- §7.2 (Initial Subscriptions — references capability filtering)
- Any prose referencing these capability names

## Doctrine Reference

security.md §'Capability scopes' defines capabilities as granular, additive grants. architecture.md §'Versioning' requires that capability names are stable identifiers. Having two different identifier vocabularies for the same concept violates both.

## Concrete Change

Adopt the RFC 0006 (Configuration) names as canonical, since config is where capabilities are declared and persisted:
- `read_scene` → `read_scene_topology`
- `receive_input` → `access_input_events`
- `zone_publish:<zone>` → `publish_zone:<zone>`

Update the §7.1 table and all prose references in RFC 0005. Add a normative note that capability identifiers are defined in RFC 0006 §6.3 and must match exactly.

## Rationale

Capability names are API surface — they flow over the wire in `requested_capabilities` and `granted_capabilities` string fields. A mismatch is not a cosmetic issue; it is a protocol incompatibility. The config RFC is the source of truth for capability definitions since that is where administrators declare them.
title: RFC 0005: unify capability vocabulary with config RFC 0006

description:
Implement the four-level zone model: zone types, zone instances, zone publication, zone occupancy, contention policies, media types, and layer attachment per scene-graph/spec.md.

## Spec References

### Requirement: Zone Registry (scene-graph/spec.md line 185)
Runtime-owned zone registry loaded from configuration at startup. Agents MUST NOT create zones in v1. Four-level ontology: zone type (schema), zone instance (type bound to tab), publication (publish event), occupancy (resolved state). Zone instances are static in v1 (from config, one instance per tab per zone type).
Source: RFC 0001 §2.5 | Scope: v1-mandatory

### Requirement: Zone Publishing (scene-graph/spec.md line 198)
PublishToZoneMutation with zone_name (type name), content, publish_token, optional expires_at_wall_us, optional publish_key (for MergeByKey), optional content_classification. Runtime resolves zone_name to ZoneInstance for agent's active tab. Requires publish_zone:<zone_name> capability and valid ZonePublishToken. ClearZone clears all publications by agent in specified zone.
Source: RFC 0001 §3.1, §3.3 | Scope: v1-mandatory

### Requirement: Zone Contention Policies (scene-graph/spec.md line 215)
Four policies: LatestWins (most recent replaces previous), Stack (accumulate, auto-dismiss, configurable max_depth), MergeByKey (same key replaces, different keys coexist, configurable max_keys), Replace (single occupant, new evicts current).
Source: RFC 0001 §2.5 | Scope: v1-mandatory

### Requirement: Zone Media Types V1 (scene-graph/spec.md line 232)
Six media types: five v1-mandatory (StreamText, ShortTextWithIcon, KeyValuePairs, StaticImage, SolidColor) and one post-v1 (VideoSurfaceRef). Zone publish content MUST match one of the zone type's accepted_media_types.
Source: RFC 0001 §2.5 | Scope: v1-mandatory (five types), post-v1 (VideoSurfaceRef)

### Requirement: Zone Layer Attachment (scene-graph/spec.md line 241)
Three attachment types: Background (behind all agent tiles), Content (within content layer z-order space at z_order >= ZONE_TILE_Z_MIN = 0x8000_0000), Chrome (above all agent content, rendered by runtime). Content-layer zone tiles participate in same z-order traversal as agent tiles but in reserved upper band.
Source: RFC 0001 §2.5 | Scope: v1-mandatory

### Requirement: Zone Occupancy Query API (scene-graph/spec.md line 360)
effective_geometry and occupancy query API deferred to post-v1. In v1, runtime maintains occupancy state internally; snapshots include active publications per instance but do not expose effective_geometry.
Source: RFC 0001 §2.5 | Scope: post-v1

## WHEN/THEN Scenarios from Spec

- Scenario: Zone type loaded from config (line 190)
  WHEN the runtime starts with config defining zone type "subtitle"
  THEN the zone registry MUST contain a ZoneType "subtitle" with its accepted_media_types, contention_policy, and rendering_policy

- Scenario: Agent cannot create zones (line 194)
  WHEN an agent attempts to create a new zone type or zone instance
  THEN the runtime MUST reject the operation (no mutation exists for zone creation in v1)

- Scenario: Publish to subtitle zone (line 203)
  WHEN an agent with publish_zone:subtitle capability and valid ZonePublishToken submits PublishToZoneMutation with zone_name="subtitle"
  THEN the runtime MUST resolve to subtitle ZoneInstance in agent's active tab and publish

- Scenario: Zone not found (line 207)
  WHEN an agent publishes to zone_name="nonexistent"
  THEN reject with ZoneNotFound

- Scenario: Invalid publish token (line 211)
  WHEN PublishToZoneMutation with invalid or expired ZonePublishToken
  THEN reject with ZonePublishTokenInvalid

- Scenario: LatestWins contention (line 220)
  WHEN two agents publish to a LatestWins zone
  THEN only the most recent publication MUST be visible

- Scenario: MergeByKey contention (line 224)
  WHEN agent A publishes with publish_key="temp" and agent B with publish_key="humidity" to MergeByKey zone
  THEN both publications MUST coexist

- Scenario: Stack max depth (line 228)
  WHEN publications exceed a Stack zone's max_depth
  THEN the oldest publication MUST be evicted

- Scenario: Media type mismatch (line 237)
  WHEN KeyValuePairs published to a zone that only accepts StreamText
  THEN reject with ZoneMediaTypeMismatch

- Scenario: Content zone tile z-order (line 246)
  WHEN a Content-layer zone instance is active
  THEN the runtime MUST create a zone tile with z_order >= 0x8000_0000 above all agent-owned tiles

- Scenario: Effective geometry not in v1 snapshot (line 365)
  WHEN a scene snapshot is serialized in v1
  THEN it MUST include active zone publications but MUST NOT include effective_geometry data

## Implementation Details

Zone types defined in configuration (v1 set: subtitle, notification, status-bar, pip, ambient-background, alert-banner). Each zone type defines accepted_media_types, contention_policy, rendering_policy, and layer_attachment. Zone instances bind a type to a specific tab with geometry policy. ZonePublishToken is opaque, runtime-issued, validated on each publish. Occupancy is the runtime's resolved view after applying contention policy to all active publications.

## Acceptance Criteria

- Zone test scenes from Epic 0 pass: zone_publish_subtitle, zone_reject_wrong_type, zone_conflict_two_publishers, zone_orchestrate_then_publish, zone_geometry_adapts_profile, zone_disconnect_cleanup
- Contention policy property tests pass (proptest: random publish/clear sequences, verify occupancy invariants)
- All six v1 zone types loadable from config
- All four contention policies implemented and tested (LatestWins, Stack, MergeByKey, Replace)
- Five v1 media types validated; VideoSurfaceRef accepted in config but not renderable
- Layer attachment routing: Background, Content (z_order >= 0x8000_0000), Chrome
- ZonePublishToken validation rejects expired/invalid tokens

## Epic 0 Test Gates

- assert_layer0_invariants() zone-related checks
- Zone test scenes: zone_publish_subtitle, zone_reject_wrong_type, zone_conflict_two_publishers, zone_orchestrate_then_publish, zone_geometry_adapts_profile, zone_disconnect_cleanup
- Zone contention proptest suite

## Crate/File Location

- Primary: crates/tze_hud_scene/src/types.rs (ZoneDefinition, ZoneMediaType, ContentionPolicy, RenderingPolicy, GeometryPolicy already partially defined)
- Zone registry: crates/tze_hud_scene/src/graph.rs (zone registry within SceneGraph)
- Zone publication: crates/tze_hud_scene/src/mutation.rs (PublishToZone, ClearZone mutations)
- Tests: crates/tze_hud_scene/tests/zone_ontology.rs (integration tests)

## v1 Scope Tags

- v1-mandatory: Zone Registry (line 185), Zone Publishing (line 198), Zone Contention Policies (line 215), Zone Media Types V1 (line 232, five types), Zone Layer Attachment (line 241)
- v1-reserved: VideoSurfaceRef media type (schema defined, rendering deferred per line 351)
- post-v1: Zone Occupancy Query API (line 360), effective_geometry, VideoSurfaceRef rendering, agent-created zones

## What This Does NOT Include

- Identity types (bead #1) — SceneId, ResourceId consumed
- Scene hierarchy (bead #2) — Tab/Tile consumed for zone instance binding
- Atomic batch pipeline (bead #3) — PublishToZone/ClearZone are mutation types processed by the batch pipeline; this bead provides the zone resolution logic called during validation
- Hit-test interaction with zones (bead #5) — Chrome-layer zones win hit-test, but traversal logic is bead #5
- Zone rendering (compositor epic) — this bead is pure data model, no GPU rendering
- Zone occupancy query API (post-v1)
title: Zone ontology: type, instance, publication, occupancy

description:
Analyze and refine 3 foundation-layer specs against their source RFCs and heart-and-soul doctrine:

**Specs:** scene-graph/spec.md (RFC 0001), runtime-kernel/spec.md (RFC 0002), timing-model/spec.md (RFC 0003)

**Refinement criteria:**
1. **Law-and-lore adherence** — Verify every requirement traces correctly to its RFC section. Flag requirements that contradict or drift from the RFC. Ensure quantitative budgets match RFC values exactly. Check cross-RFC integration contracts (clock domain naming, identity types, frame pipeline budgets).
2. **Heart-and-soul spirit** — Verify specs embody: screen sovereignty (LLMs never in frame loop), atomic mutations, namespace isolation, clock domain typing (_wall_us/_mono_us), performance as product (latency targets are normative, not aspirational).
3. **Conciseness and clarity** — Tighten vague SHALL/MUST statements into testable assertions. Remove redundant scenarios. Clarify ambiguous boundary conditions. Ensure v1/post-v1 scope tags are consistent and justified.

**These 3 specs are the foundation layer — all other specs depend on their types, contracts, and budgets being precise.**
title: Refine foundation specs: scene-graph, runtime-kernel, timing-model

close_reason: Duplicate resume paths resolved in Round 2: SessionInit fields 9-10 reserved, SessionResume is the sole resume path
description:
## Problem

RFC 0005 has two distinct paths for session resumption, creating ambiguity:

1. **SessionInit fields** (§1.2): `resume_session_token` (field 9) and `resume_last_seen_server_seq` (field 10) allow resume as part of a fresh SessionInit message.
2. **SessionResume message** (§6.2): A dedicated `SessionResume` message that agents send 'as the first message instead of SessionInit.'

Both paths carry the same information (agent_id, session_token, last_seen_server_sequence, auth_credential) but diverge in how they are framed on the wire and what response they receive (`SessionEstablished` vs `SessionResumeResult`). This creates implementer confusion and protocol ambiguity — which path should agents use?

## Affected Sections

- §1.2 (SessionInit proto definition, fields 9-10)
- §6.2 (SessionResume message definition)
- §6.3 (SessionResumeResult)
- §6.4 (State Delta on Resume)
- §9 (Proto schema — both messages defined)

## Doctrine Reference

architecture.md §'Session model' requires one primary bidirectional stream per agent. Having two resume paths undermines protocol clarity. v1.md §'Advanced protocol' explicitly defers resumable state sync, further arguing for simplicity.

## Concrete Change

Pick ONE resume path for v1:
- **Recommendation**: Remove `resume_session_token` and `resume_last_seen_server_seq` from `SessionInit`. Keep `SessionResume` as the dedicated resume message. This cleanly separates 'new session' from 'resume session' at the wire level — the first message's type determines the intent unambiguously.
- Update §1.2, §6.2-6.4, and the proto schema in §9 accordingly.
- Add a note that the two-path approach was considered and rejected for v1 clarity.

## Rationale

Duplicate resume paths are the #1 cross-document drift issue flagged by review. Implementers will inevitably pick different paths, creating interop bugs. Clean separation (SessionInit = new, SessionResume = resume) is idiomatic for protobuf oneof-based dispatch.
title: RFC 0005: resolve duplicate resume paths (SessionInit resume fields vs SessionResume message)

description:
Implement scene snapshot serialization: deterministic, complete, performant serialization of the full scene graph at a point in time per scene-graph/spec.md.

## Spec References

### Requirement: Scene Snapshot Serialization (scene-graph/spec.md line 276)
Full scene snapshot is a complete, deterministic serialization at a specific sequence number. MUST include: sequence, snapshot_wall_us (UTC wall-clock), snapshot_mono_us (monotonic), all tabs (ordered by display_order), all tiles, all nodes, zone_registry (types, instances, active publishes), active_tab, and a BLAKE3 checksum. Fields serialized in deterministic order (BTreeMap, not HashMap). Serialization < 1ms for 100-tile scene with 1000 nodes.
Source: RFC 0001 §4.1, §10 | Scope: v1-mandatory

### Requirement: V1 Reconnect via Full Snapshot (scene-graph/spec.md line 333)
When an agent reconnects, runtime SHALL always send a full SceneSnapshot. Agent discards prior state, applies snapshot, resumes from snapshot's sequence number.
Source: RFC 0001 §4.2 | Scope: v1-mandatory

### Requirement: Ephemeral Scene State (scene-graph/spec.md line 324)
Resources are ephemeral in v1 — snapshots reference ResourceIds but don't guarantee backing data survives restart.
Source: RFC 0001 §6; RFC 0011 §4.1 | Scope: v1-mandatory

### Requirement: Incremental Diff (scene-graph/spec.md line 342)
Incremental diff (WAL-backed delta sync) deferred to post-v1. V1 ships snapshot-only reconnection.
Source: RFC 0001 §4.2 | Scope: post-v1

### Requirement: Zone Occupancy Query API (scene-graph/spec.md line 360)
Snapshots include active publications per instance but MUST NOT include effective_geometry data in v1.
Source: RFC 0001 §2.5 | Scope: post-v1

## WHEN/THEN Scenarios from Spec

- Scenario: Snapshot determinism (line 281)
  WHEN two snapshots are taken of identical scene state
  THEN both MUST produce identical serialization bytes and identical BLAKE3 checksums

- Scenario: Snapshot performance (line 285)
  WHEN a snapshot is serialized for a scene with 100 tiles and 1000 nodes
  THEN serialization MUST complete in < 1ms (protobuf encode, single core, reference hardware)

- Scenario: Reconnecting agent receives snapshot (line 338)
  WHEN an agent reconnects after a disconnection
  THEN the runtime MUST send a full SceneSnapshot; incremental diff is not available in v1

- Scenario: Scene lost on restart (line 329)
  WHEN the runtime process restarts
  THEN the scene graph MUST be empty; agents MUST reconnect and re-create

- Scenario: Incremental diff not available (line 347)
  WHEN an agent requests incremental diff in v1
  THEN the runtime MUST provide a full snapshot instead

- Scenario: Effective geometry not in v1 snapshot (line 365)
  WHEN a scene snapshot is serialized in v1
  THEN it MUST include active zone publications but MUST NOT include effective_geometry data

## Implementation Details

SceneSnapshot struct containing: sequence (u64), snapshot_wall_us (i64), snapshot_mono_us (u64), tabs (BTreeMap<SceneId, Tab> ordered by display_order), tiles, nodes, zone_registry (zone types, instances, active publications), active_tab (SceneId), checksum (BLAKE3 of serialized content). All internal maps use BTreeMap for deterministic iteration order. Serialization target is protobuf (SceneSnapshot message in session.proto). Checksum computed over the serialized protobuf bytes before the checksum field itself.

Round-trip invariant: serialize → deserialize → re-serialize produces byte-identical output.

## Acceptance Criteria

- Snapshot determinism: serialize same scene twice, assert byte equality and BLAKE3 checksum equality
- Round-trip: deserialize snapshot → re-serialize → assert byte equality with original
- All 25 test scenes produce valid snapshots (no panics, all fields populated, checksum verifiable)
- Performance: serialization < 1ms for 100 tiles with 1000 nodes (benchmarked, hardware-normalized)
- Snapshot includes zone publications but NOT effective_geometry
- Snapshot references ResourceIds but does not embed resource data
- BTreeMap used for all map types (verified by code inspection / compilation — HashMap would not satisfy determinism)

## Epic 0 Test Gates

- assert_layer0_invariants() passes on all 25 scenes — snapshots must serialize graphs that pass invariants
- Snapshot determinism property test: proptest generates random valid scenes, serializes twice, asserts equality
- Test scenes most relevant: all 25 (snapshot must work for every scene), particularly zone_publish_subtitle and zone_conflict_two_publishers (zone publications in snapshot), max_tiles_stress (performance), three_agents_contention (multi-namespace snapshot)

## Crate/File Location

- Primary: crates/tze_hud_scene/src/diff.rs (snapshot serialization — file already exists)
- Types: crates/tze_hud_scene/src/types.rs (SceneSnapshot struct)
- Protobuf: crates/tze_hud_protocol/proto/session.proto (SceneSnapshot message)
- Benchmarks: crates/tze_hud_scene/benches/snapshot.rs (criterion benchmark for < 1ms)
- Tests: crates/tze_hud_scene/tests/snapshot.rs (integration tests)

## v1 Scope Tags

- v1-mandatory: Scene Snapshot Serialization (line 276), V1 Reconnect via Full Snapshot (line 333), Ephemeral Scene State (line 324)
- v1-reserved: None for this bead
- post-v1: Incremental Diff (line 342, WAL-backed delta sync), Zone Occupancy Query API (line 360, effective_geometry), persistent resource store (resource-store/spec.md, resources survive restart)

## What This Does NOT Include

- Scene hierarchy types (bead #2) — consumed
- Zone ontology (bead #4) — zone registry and publications consumed for snapshot content
- Incremental diff / SceneDiff / DiffOp (post-v1, spec line 342)
- Resource persistence (post-v1, resource-store/spec.md)
- gRPC transport for sending snapshots to agents (session protocol epic)
- Session management / reconnection logic (session protocol epic) — this bead provides the snapshot, transport delivers it
- Zone occupancy effective_geometry (post-v1, spec line 360)
title: Deterministic scene snapshots

description:
Interleaved spec consolidation and code convergence phase. The RFC set is now strong (8.8/10) but implementation lags the contracts (7.4/10). This epic pairs spec fixes with code implementation to close the gap simultaneously.

## Workstreams

### WS1: Protocol unification (highest priority)
Fix RFC 0005 (weakest RFC, 9 P1 beads) and simultaneously cut over from scene_service.proto to the RFC 0005 session model. This is the spine — everything else builds on it.

### WS2: Lease governance (spec + code)
Write RFC 0008 while implementing the lease state machine. The safe-mode contradiction (rig-tew), undefined lease_priority (rig-kig), and suspension semantics all need both spec authority and working code.

### WS3: Policy arbitration (spec + code)
Write RFC 0009 while implementing the arbitration stack. Resolves GPU failure path conflict (rig-nev + rig-81i), redaction ownership (rig-zeb), freeze advisory model (rig-9v1).

### WS4: Cross-RFC unification passes
ID wire types (rig-6k5), capability vocabulary (rig-vbi). Update both RFC protos and code protos simultaneously.

### WS5: Per-RFC fixes
Remaining 35 beads from the ChatGPT review, prioritized by RFC score (0005 first, then 0003/0004, then rest).

### WS6: Scene events + resource store (lower priority)
Write RFCs 0010-0011. Less urgent — can follow once WS1-WS3 stabilize.

## Sequencing
- WS1 + WS2 + WS3 can start in parallel (independent)
- WS4 depends on WS1 (ID types in session proto must settle first)
- WS5 can be interleaved throughout
- WS6 follows WS1-WS3

## Success criteria
- scene_service.proto replaced by RFC 0005 session model
- Lease state machine implemented and tested
- Policy arbitration stack implemented
- All 47 improvement beads closed or superseded
- Vertical slice wired through new contracts end-to-end
title: Consolidation + convergence: specs and code in parallel

description:
## Problem

RFC 0007 §7.3 defines OverrideEvent types that are logged to the 'audit stream', but the RFC never specifies what that audit stream is, what format shell-state events take, or how they are consumed. The most important human interventions in the system happen at the shell level (dismiss tile, dismiss all, safe mode entry/exit, freeze, viewer context changes), yet there is no defined audit trail for these actions.

Without a defined audit event schema:
- Operators cannot review what overrides were triggered and when.
- Compliance/safety analysis of viewer-agent interaction patterns is impossible.
- Debugging 'why did my agent get disconnected?' requires guessing.

## Affected Sections
- RFC 0007 §7.3 (Override Event Types — mentions 'audit stream' without defining it)
- RFC 0007 §6.2 (Viewer Context Transitions — no audit event for viewer class changes)
- RFC 0002 §2.5 (Telemetry Thread — the likely consumer of audit events)

## Relevant Doctrine
- architecture.md §Error model: 'Every error the runtime returns must be structured, machine-readable, and diagnostic.' Audit events should follow the same structured principle.
- security.md §Capability scopes: 'Every capability grant and revocation is logged with timestamp, agent identity, and reason.' Override events should have the same auditability.

## Proposed Resolution
Add a §7.8 (Shell Audit Events) that defines:

1. **Audit event envelope**: timestamp_us (monotonic), event_type enum, trigger source (keyboard/gesture/auto), optional affected_tile_ids, optional affected_session_ids.
2. **Defined audit event types**: TILE_DISMISSED, ALL_DISMISSED, SAFE_MODE_ENTERED, SAFE_MODE_EXITED, FREEZE_ACTIVATED, FREEZE_DEACTIVATED, VIEWER_CLASS_CHANGED (old_class, new_class), VIEWER_PROMPT_SHOWN, VIEWER_PROMPT_RESOLVED.
3. **Emission path**: Audit events are emitted to the telemetry thread (RFC 0002 §2.5) as a distinct event category. They are never sent to agents.
4. **Privacy constraint**: Audit events must NOT contain viewer identity details (name, face recognition confidence, etc.) — only the resulting viewer class. This prevents the audit log from becoming a surveillance tool.

Cross-reference the existing OverrideEvent types in §7.3 and note that they are the source of audit events — the audit trail is derived from OverrideEvents plus viewer-context-change events.
title: RFC 0007: Add shell-state audit events for human interventions

description:
The event type naming convention in scene-events/spec.md (lines 35-47) has structural defects:

**Defect 1: Missing scene prefix**
- Rule defines scene events as '<object>.<action>' with no prefix
- Agent events get 'agent.<namespace>.<event_name>' prefix
- System events get 'system.<action>' prefix
- Rule then reserves 'scene.' prefix but never mandates its use for scene events
- Creates ambiguity: are scene events scene.tile.created or just tile.created?

**Defect 2: Agent event example contradicts grammar**
- Rule says agent events are 'agent.<namespace>.<event_name>' (3 segments)
- Example shows 'agent.doorbell_agent.doorbell.ring' (4 segments)
- Is it agent.<namespace>.<category>.<action> or agent.<namespace>.<event_name>?

**Defect 3: Subscription category naming mismatch**
- Line 157 uses SHOUTY_SNAKE_CASE for subscription categories (SCENE_TOPOLOGY, INPUT_EVENTS, etc.)
- But event types use dotted.lowercase format
- No mapping between subscription categories and event type prefixes

**Resolution:**
- Mandate 'scene.' prefix for scene events: scene.<object>.<action>
- Fix agent event grammar to match example: agent.<namespace>.<category>.<action>
- Add explicit mapping table: subscription category → event type prefix pattern
- Example: SCENE_TOPOLOGY subscribes to scene.tile.*, scene.tab.*, scene.node.*
title: Fix malformed event type naming convention in scene-events spec

close_reason: PR #149 merged after PR review. Implemented bounded channels, thread architecture, window mode abstraction, and GPU adapter selection per runtime-kernel/spec.md.
description:
Implement inter-thread communication and thread model per runtime-kernel/spec.md Requirement: Thread Model (line 19), Requirement: Main Thread Responsibilities (line 33), Requirement: Compositor Thread Ownership (line 46), Requirement: Channel Topology (line 272), Requirement: Graceful Shutdown (line 333), Requirement: Window Modes (line 172), and Requirement: Platform GPU Backends (line 189).

## Spec Requirements (all v1-mandatory)

### Requirement: Thread Model (line 19)
Fixed, small set of threads with explicit responsibilities:
- Main thread: winit event loop, input drain, local feedback, frame presentation
- Compositor thread: scene commit, render encode, GPU submit
- Network thread(s): Tokio multi-thread runtime for gRPC server, MCP bridge, session management
- Telemetry thread: async structured emission
Thread count MUST be determined at startup; no dynamic thread spawning during normal operation.

### Requirement: Main Thread Responsibilities (line 33)
Main thread runs winit event loop. Drains OS input events, applies press/hover local feedback, calls surface.present() when signaled, handles resize, initiates shutdown. MUST NOT encode render commands or submit GPU work. Elevated to high priority at startup using platform-appropriate mechanisms:
- Linux: SCHED_RR real-time scheduling (Implementation Notes line 401)
- macOS: QOS_CLASS_USER_INTERACTIVE (Implementation Notes line 402)
- Windows: THREAD_PRIORITY_TIME_CRITICAL (Implementation Notes line 403)
Failure to elevate MUST NOT fail startup — log warning and continue at normal priority.

### Requirement: Compositor Thread Ownership (line 46)
Compositor thread owns wgpu Device and Queue exclusively. No other thread touches the device. Main thread holds surface handle, only thread calling surface.present(). Split driven by macOS/Metal requirements.

### Requirement: Channel Topology (line 272)
All inter-thread communication via bounded channels. No unbounded queues.
- InputEvent: ring buffer, drop-oldest semantics
- SceneLocalPatch: ring buffer, drop-oldest
- SceneEventEphemeral: ring buffer, drop-oldest
- TelemetryRecord: ring buffer, drop-oldest
- SceneEventTransactional: backpressure channel, capacity 256, NEVER dropped
- SceneEventStateStream: coalesce-key channel, capacity 512, intermediate states skipped (not dropped)
- FrameReadySignal: tokio::sync::watch (latest value wins)

### Requirement: Window Modes (line 172)
Two modes configured at startup:
- Fullscreen: compositor owns entire display, all input captured, all platforms
- Overlay/HUD: transparent borderless always-on-top window, per-region input passthrough, platform-specific
Runtime mode switching supported but disruptive (requires surface recreation).

### Requirement: Platform GPU Backends (line 189)
GPU backends per platform: Vulkan on Linux, D3D12 and Vulkan on Windows, Metal on macOS. GPU device initialization MUST be fatal if no suitable adapter exists.

### Requirement: Graceful Shutdown (line 333)
Ordered sequence:
1. Stop accepting new connections
2. Drain active mutations (wait up to configurable drain timeout [0ms, 60000ms]; suggested default 500ms per Implementation Notes line 429)
3. Revoke all leases without waiting for acknowledgement
4. Flush telemetry with configurable grace period [0ms, 10000ms] (suggested default 200ms per Implementation Notes line 430)
5. Terminate agent sessions
6. GPU drain via device.poll(Wait)
7. Release resources with reference counts reaching zero
8. Exit process (code 0 clean, non-zero error)

### Requirement: Single-Process Model (line 10)
Single OS process. Agents are external gRPC clients that do not share compositor's address space.

## WHEN/THEN Scenarios from Spec

Thread Model:
- WHEN the runtime is running THEN main thread handles input drain, local feedback, surface.present(); compositor thread handles scene commit, render encode, GPU submit; network threads MUST NOT touch scene graph or GPU state directly (line 26)
- WHEN the runtime is in normal operation THEN no new OS threads SHALL be spawned beyond startup threads (line 30)

Main Thread:
- WHEN the compositor thread is encoding render commands THEN main thread MUST continue processing input events without waiting (line 38)
- WHEN the runtime lacks privileges for elevated thread priority THEN main thread MUST log warning and continue at normal priority (line 43)

Compositor Thread:
- WHEN any code path attempts to call wgpu Device or Queue methods THEN only compositor thread SHALL execute those calls (line 51)
- WHEN a frame is ready for presentation THEN compositor thread signals main thread via FrameReadySignal, only main thread calls surface.present() (line 55)

Channel Topology:
- WHEN the SceneEventTransactional channel is full THEN compositor thread MUST block (apply backpressure) rather than dropping the event (line 278)
- WHEN the SceneEventStateStream channel is full THEN a new state-stream event for same (tile_id, event_kind) MUST replace pending entry rather than being dropped or blocking (line 282)
- WHEN the SceneEventEphemeral ring buffer is full THEN oldest event MUST be dropped and overflow counted in telemetry (line 286)

Window Modes:
- WHEN runtime starts in fullscreen mode THEN compositor MUST own entire display with opaque background and all input captured (line 178)
- WHEN runtime is in overlay mode and pointer event lands outside any active hit-region THEN event MUST pass through to underlying desktop (line 182)
- WHEN runtime starts in overlay mode on GNOME Wayland (no layer-shell) THEN runtime MUST fall back to fullscreen silently with startup warning logged (line 186)

GPU:
- WHEN no suitable GPU adapter is found during initialization THEN runtime MUST fail with fatal error and structured error message (line 195)
- WHEN an agent connects to the runtime THEN agent MUST communicate via gRPC or MCP over IPC/network; MUST NOT share compositor's address space (line 16)

Shutdown:
- WHEN runtime receives SIGTERM THEN stop accepting connections, drain compositor, revoke leases, flush telemetry, exit cleanly with code 0 (line 339)
- WHEN GPU device is lost THEN flush telemetry, attempt safe mode overlay, trigger graceful shutdown with non-zero exit code (line 343)

## v1-mandatory
All requirements listed above.

## v1-reserved exclusions
None for this bead.

## post-v1 exclusions
- Media Worker Pool (line 373) — DecodedFrameReady channel slot remains empty in v1
- Parallel Render Encoding (line 382) — no parallel CommandEncoder instances in v1

## Acceptance Criteria
- No unbounded allocations under sustained load
- Backpressure channels (SceneEventTransactional, capacity 256) never drop transactional messages
- Coalesce-key channels (SceneEventStateStream, capacity 512) correctly replace pending entries by (tile_id, event_kind) key
- Ring buffers (InputEvent, SceneLocalPatch, SceneEventEphemeral, TelemetryRecord) correctly drop oldest on overflow
- FrameReadySignal uses tokio::sync::watch (latest value wins)
- Channel capacity assertions pass for all channel types
- Thread startup: main, compositor, telemetry, network threads all created at startup
- No dynamic thread spawning during normal operation
- Main thread priority elevation attempted with graceful fallback
- Graceful shutdown sequence completes in order with configurable timeouts
- Fullscreen and overlay window modes functional
- GPU adapter fatal error produces structured error message

## Relevant Epic 0 test scenes
- chatty_dashboard_touch: high-frequency input through InputEvent ring buffer
- coalesced_dashboard: state-stream coalescing under mutation storm
- max_tiles_stress: channel capacity under high tile count
- three_agents_contention: multi-agent channel contention
- disconnect_reclaim_multiagent: graceful shutdown / session teardown path
- overlay_passthrough_regions: overlay mode input passthrough

## Crate/File Location
- crates/tze_hud_runtime/src/channels.rs (new — bounded channel types: ring buffer, backpressure, coalesce-key)
- crates/tze_hud_runtime/src/threads.rs (new — thread spawning, priority elevation, shutdown coordination)
- crates/tze_hud_runtime/src/lib.rs (runtime startup orchestration)
- crates/tze_hud_runtime/src/window.rs (new — window mode abstraction, fullscreen/overlay)
- crates/tze_hud_compositor/src/lib.rs (GPU adapter selection, device initialization)

## What This Does NOT Include
- Frame pipeline stage logic (bead #1 — uses these channels)
- Degradation ladder (bead #2)
- Headless mode specifics (bead #4 — depends on this bead for thread architecture)
- Admission control / per-agent budgets (bead #5)
- Session protocol implementation (Epic 5 — uses network threads from this bead)
title: Bounded channels and thread architecture

description:
Implement the SceneEvent envelope structure and event type naming convention per scene-events/spec.md.

## Spec Requirements

### Requirement: SceneEvent Envelope Structure (spec lines 20-32)
Every scene event carries a common envelope containing:
- event_id: UUID v7, 16 bytes
- event_type: namespaced dotted string (e.g., "scene.tile.created", "agent.doorbell_agent.doorbell.ring")
- interruption_class: effective class after ceiling enforcement
- timestamp_wall_us: wall clock microseconds
- timestamp_mono_us: monotonic microseconds
- source_lease_id: empty for system events
- source_namespace: empty for runtime events
- sequence: monotonic scene sequence number for gap detection
- payload: oneof of zone, tile, tab, agent, system, or sync_group payloads

### Requirement: Event Type Naming Convention (spec lines 35-47)
Dotted namespace hierarchy:
- Scene events: scene.<object>.<action> (e.g., "scene.tile.created", "scene.zone.occupancy_changed", "scene.focus.changed")
- Agent events: agent.<namespace>.<category>.<action> (e.g., "agent.doorbell_agent.doorbell.ring", "agent.alarm_agent.fire.detected")
- System events: system.<action> (e.g., "system.degradation_changed", "system.lease_revoked")
All segments: lowercase letters, digits, underscores only.
Reserved prefixes "system." and "scene." rejected for agent-emitted events.

### Requirement: Three-Category Event Taxonomy (spec lines 5-17)
All events classified into exactly three categories: input, scene, system. Each SceneEvent variant belongs to exactly one subscription category.

### Requirement: Event Ordering and Sequence Numbers (spec lines 269-277)
Events within a session delivered in generation order via SceneEvent.sequence field. Agents detect gaps (e.g., receiving 10, 11, 14) indicating reconnect+snapshot cycle needed. No replay of missed events.

## WHEN/THEN Scenarios (from spec)

- WHEN the runtime generates a TileCreated scene event THEN the SceneEvent envelope MUST contain a non-zero UUID v7 event_id, event_type "scene.tile.created", a valid InterruptionClass, both wall and monotonic timestamps, the source lease_id, the source agent namespace, and a monotonically increasing sequence number (spec line 27)
- WHEN a system event (e.g., degradation_changed) is generated THEN source_lease_id MUST be empty and source_namespace MUST be empty (spec line 31)
- WHEN an agent with namespace "doorbell_agent" emits event "doorbell.ring" THEN the delivered event_type MUST be "agent.doorbell_agent.doorbell.ring" (spec line 42)
- WHEN an agent attempts to emit an event with name starting with "system." or "scene." THEN the runtime MUST reject the emission (spec line 46)
- WHEN any event is generated by the runtime, an agent, or a system subsystem THEN it MUST belong to exactly one of the three categories (input, scene, system) and map to exactly one subscription category (spec line 12)
- WHEN an agent receives events with sequence numbers 10, 11, 14 (gap at 12-13) THEN the agent MUST detect the gap and request a SceneSnapshot for reconnect (spec line 276)

## Acceptance Criteria

- EventRouter trait tests from Epic 0 pass with the envelope implementation
- Naming convention validated for all event types: scene.tile.created, scene.zone.occupancy_changed, scene.focus.changed, agent.doorbell_agent.doorbell.ring, system.degradation_changed, system.lease_revoked
- Reserved prefix rejection verified: agent emitting "system.foo" or "scene.bar" is rejected
- UUID v7 event_ids are monotonically time-ordered
- Sequence numbers are monotonically increasing per session
- System events have empty source_lease_id and source_namespace
- Agent events have populated source_lease_id and source_namespace

## Relevant test scenes
- chatty_dashboard_touch (high-frequency event envelope creation)
- three_agents_contention (multi-source event envelopes)

## Crate/file location
- Event envelope type: crates/tze_hud_scene/src/events/envelope.rs
- Naming convention: crates/tze_hud_scene/src/events/naming.rs
- Event taxonomy enum: crates/tze_hud_scene/src/events/taxonomy.rs
- Module root: crates/tze_hud_scene/src/events/mod.rs

## v1-mandatory
- SceneEvent Envelope Structure (spec lines 20-32)
- Event Type Naming Convention (spec lines 35-47)
- Three-Category Event Taxonomy (spec lines 5-17)
- Event Ordering and Sequence Numbers (spec lines 269-277)

## v1-reserved exclusions
None for this bead.

## post-v1 exclusions
- Inter-Agent Event Bus (spec lines 328-335)
- Advanced Event Routing (spec lines 339-346)

## What this bead does NOT include
- Subscription filtering (bead #2)
- Interruption classification and quiet hours (bead #3)
- Agent event emission protocol and rate limiting (bead #4)
- tab_switch_on_event (bead #4)
- Event bus pipeline stages 2-4 (other beads)
title: Event envelope and naming convention

description:
Final integration epic — verifies all subsystems work together, realizes validation Layers 2-4, and proves the v1 thesis.

## Context
Runs after all subsystem epics (1-11) are substantially complete. Focuses on cross-subsystem integration contracts, higher validation layers (Layer 2 SSIM, Layer 3 performance, Layer 4 artifacts), and the v1 success criteria from heart-and-soul/v1.md.

## Primary spec
openspec/changes/v1-mvp-standards/specs/validation-framework/spec.md — Layers 2-4, Hardware-Normalized Calibration, Test Scene Registry

## Secondary specs
All 12 subsystem specs (cross-subsystem integration contracts)

## V1 success criteria coverage (all 7 thesis points)
1. An LLM can hold a tile and have it render at 60fps
2. The lease model works (auth, capabilities, TTL, revocation)
3. Multiple agents coexist without interference
4. Performance is real (p99 latencies measured and tested)
5. The validation architecture works (5 layers operational)
6. Zones work as LLM-first surface (single MCP call, no scene context needed)
7. Headless mode fully functional (no display server, CI on software GPU)

## V1 scope tags
- v1-mandatory: All 6 sub-beads address v1-mandatory requirements
- v1-reserved: None — no reserved scope items in this epic
- post-v1: None — all work is v1 scope

## Sub-beads (6)
1. Multi-agent integration test
2. Session lifecycle integration
3. Layer 2: SSIM visual regression
4. Layer 3: Performance validation with calibration
5. Layer 4: Developer visibility artifacts
6. V1 thesis proof

## Dependency chain
Epics 1-11 --> #1 Multi-Agent Integration
           --> #2 Session Lifecycle Integration
           --> #3 Layer 2 SSIM --> #5 Layer 4 Artifacts --> #6 V1 Thesis Proof
           --> #4 Layer 3 Perf --> #5

description:
Sub-bead #3 of Epic 3 (Timing Model). Implement sync group membership, lifecycle, commit policies, force-commit, owner disconnect, resource governance, and sync drift budget.

Parent epic: rig-zyeo (Epic 3: Timing Model)
Depends on: sub-bead #2 (rig-mdpo, TimingHints and presentation scheduling)

## Spec requirements (v1-mandatory)

### Requirement: Sync Group Membership and Lifecycle (spec lines 124-139)
Sync groups MUST be named scene-graph objects with SceneId (UUIDv7) identity, explicit lifecycle (create, join, leave, destroy), and owner_namespace. Creating requires manage_sync_groups capability. A tile MUST belong to at most one sync group. Joining via UpdateTileSyncGroup; leaving via setting sync_group to None. Destruction on explicit delete or when last member leaves.

WHEN/THEN scenarios:
- WHEN an agent with manage_sync_groups capability submits CreateSyncGroup, THEN the group MUST be created with specified id, name, and commit_policy (spec lines 129-131)
- WHEN a tile already belongs to sync group A and is assigned to sync group B, THEN the tile MUST leave group A and join group B (spec lines 133-135)
- WHEN the last member tile leaves a sync group, THEN the sync group MUST be destroyed and removed from scene graph (spec lines 137-139)

### Requirement: Sync Group Commit Policies (spec lines 141-156)
Two commit policies: AllOrDefer (all members must have pending mutations before any applied; incomplete at frame close defers) and AvailableMembers (apply whatever subset has pending mutations).

WHEN/THEN scenarios:
- WHEN all members of AllOrDefer group have pending mutations in frame N's intake window, THEN all mutations MUST be applied atomically in frame N's Stage 4 (spec lines 146-148)
- WHEN only some members of AllOrDefer group have pending mutations, THEN entire group MUST be deferred to next frame (spec lines 150-152)
- WHEN only some members of AvailableMembers group have pending mutations, THEN available members' mutations MUST be applied; absent members remain unchanged (spec lines 154-156)

### Requirement: AllOrDefer Force-Commit (spec lines 158-173)
AllOrDefer deferred for max_defer_frames (default 3, configurable) consecutive frames MUST trigger force-commit: partition into present-and-ready vs absent, apply present-and-ready atomically, discard absent-member deferred mutations, reset deferred_frames_count to 0, emit SyncGroupForceCommitEvent. deferred_frames_count only increments when at least one member has pending and at least one is absent.

WHEN/THEN scenarios:
- WHEN AllOrDefer group incomplete for 3 consecutive frames (default), THEN force-commit: apply present members, discard absent, emit SyncGroupForceCommitEvent (spec lines 163-165)
- WHEN AllOrDefer group has no pending mutations for any member, THEN deferred_frames_count MUST NOT increment (spec lines 167-169)
- WHEN force-commit has fired, THEN group MUST resume AllOrDefer from deferred_frames_count = 0 on next frame (spec lines 171-173)

### Requirement: Sync Group Owner Disconnect (spec lines 175-186)
When owner session closes: emit SyncGroupOrphanedEvent, release all member tiles, destroy group after 5-second grace period. Reconnect within grace cancels destruction.

WHEN/THEN scenarios:
- WHEN agent that created sync group G disconnects, THEN emit SyncGroupOrphanedEvent, release all member tiles, destroy G after 5s (spec lines 180-182)
- WHEN owner reconnects within 5s of disconnect, THEN pending destruction MUST be cancelled (spec lines 184-186)

### Requirement: Sync Group Resource Governance (spec lines 188-195)
Max sync groups per agent namespace: 16. Max tiles per sync group: 64. Agent MUST NOT place another agent's tiles into a sync group (ownership check).

WHEN/THEN scenarios:
- WHEN agent has 16 sync groups and attempts to create 17th, THEN compositor MUST reject (spec lines 193-195)

### Requirement: Sync Drift Budget (spec lines 197-208)
sync_group_max_drift_us in FrameTimingRecord tracks worst mutation-arrival spread within committed sync groups. If exceeds sync_drift_budget_us (default 500us), emit sync_drift_high telemetry alert and activate staleness indicator for affected tiles.

WHEN/THEN scenarios:
- WHEN all sync group members' mutations arrive within 500us of each other, THEN sync_drift_budget_exceeded MUST be false in FrameTimingRecord (spec lines 202-204)
- WHEN sync group members' mutations arrive with 800us spread, THEN sync_drift_budget_exceeded MUST be true and staleness indicator activated for slow member's tiles (spec lines 206-208)

## Crate/file location
- crates/tze_hud_scene/src/timing/sync_group.rs (new: SyncGroup struct, lifecycle, policies)
- crates/tze_hud_scene/src/timing/sync_commit.rs (AllOrDefer, AvailableMembers evaluation, force-commit)
- crates/tze_hud_scene/src/timing/sync_drift.rs (drift budget tracking, telemetry)

## Relevant test scenes
- sync_group_media — primary sync group coordination scene (from validation-framework 25-scene registry)
- three_agents_contention — multi-agent sync behavior
- disconnect_reclaim_multiagent — owner disconnect + orphan lifecycle

## Cross-epic contracts
- Session-protocol (Epic 6) triggers owner disconnect handling when session closes
- Scene-graph (Epic 1) provides SceneId (UUIDv7) for sync group identity
- Lease-governance (Epic 4) governs manage_sync_groups capability grants
- Timing sub-bead #2 provides pending queue and staleness indicator infrastructure that sync groups interact with

## Acceptance criteria
- sync_group_media test scene passes
- AllOrDefer: all-ready atomic commit, incomplete deferral, force-commit after max_defer_frames
- AvailableMembers: partial commit applies available, absent unchanged
- Force-commit: SyncGroupForceCommitEvent emitted, deferred_frames_count reset, absent mutations discarded
- Owner disconnect: SyncGroupOrphanedEvent, 5s grace period, reconnect cancels
- Resource governance: 16 groups per namespace, 64 tiles per group, ownership check
- Sync drift budget: 500us threshold, telemetry alert, staleness indicator
- deferred_frames_count not incremented when group idle
- All tested with SimulatedClock for deterministic behavior

## v1-mandatory
All requirements listed above.

## v1-reserved exclusions
- None

## post-v1 exclusions
- Media clock binding on sync groups (spec lines 404-411) — sync groups are scene-level coordination only in v1
- media_clock_binding field on SyncGroup — defined but not processed

## Scope boundary (what this bead does NOT include)
- Clock domain types (sub-bead #1)
- TimingHints struct, presentation scheduling, pending queue, expiration, timestamp validation (sub-bead #2)
- Relative scheduling primitives (sub-bead #4)
- Media-clock-bound sync groups (post-v1)
title: Sync group coordination

description:
## Problem

The ChatGPT 5.4 Pro external review (scored 8.1/10) identified that RFC 0001's zone model is thinner than what presence.md defines. Presence.md specifies four distinct layers of zone definition:

1. **Zone type** — the schema (accepted media, contention policy, rendering policy, etc.)
2. **Zone instance** — a zone type bound into a specific tab with geometry and layer attachment
3. **Publication** — one publish event with content, TTL, key, priority, privacy classification
4. **Occupancy** — the runtime's resolved current state (what content is visible, which publications are active, effective geometry)

The RFC currently has `ZoneDefinition` (which conflates type and instance) plus `ZonePublishRecord` (publication). Zone instances and occupancy are not modeled as first-class concepts.

## Why This Matters

As presence.md explicitly warns: 'This four-level structure prevents the common confusion of is a zone the schema or the instance or the content?' Without it:
- Same zone type appearing in multiple tabs cannot be cleanly modeled (e.g., a 'notification' zone in both Morning and Work tabs with different geometry)
- Zone occupancy queries have no formal data structure
- The RFC loses expressiveness that will be painful to retrofit when multi-tab zone instances are needed

## Sections Affected

- §2.5 Zone Registry — `ZoneDefinition` needs to be split into ZoneType + ZoneInstance
- §4.1 `ZoneRegistrySnapshot` — needs to include occupancy state per zone instance
- §7.1 protobuf — `ZoneDefinitionProto` needs restructuring; add `ZoneInstanceProto` and `ZoneOccupancyProto`
- The zone-to-tile mapping note (after §2.5) should reference instances, not definitions

## Doctrine Reference

presence.md §'Zone anatomy' — explicitly defines the four-level structure and explains why it exists.

## Concrete Changes Needed

1. Split `ZoneDefinition` into `ZoneType` (schema: accepted media, contention policy, rendering policy defaults) and `ZoneInstance` (type ref + tab binding + geometry policy + layer attachment)
2. Add `ZoneOccupancy` struct capturing resolved state per instance (visible content, active publications, effective geometry)
3. Update `ZoneRegistrySnapshot` to include zone types, zone instances, and occupancy
4. Update protobuf schema to match
5. In v1, zone instances are static (loaded from config per presence.md), but the data model must support the full ontology
title: RFC 0001: Align zone model with doctrine's four-level ontology (type/instance/publication/occupancy)
