description:
# Freeze Semantics

Implement the freeze protocol that queues agent mutations (does not reject them), pauses resource budgets and degradation ladder advancement, defers attention signals, and auto-unfreezes on configurable timeout.

## Ownership rule
The shell is the SOLE owner of freeze state transitions. Only the shell state machine writes OverrideState.freeze_active. No other subsystem (including policy arbitration) may modify this field. The shell enforces the state invariant: safe_mode=true implies freeze_active=false. Freeze attempted during safe mode is ignored.

## Spec references (system-shell/spec.md)

### Requirement: Freeze Scene (line 141)
Ctrl+Shift+F freezes current scene state. Agent mutations queued in bounded per-session queue (default 1000). Queue overflow is traffic-class-aware:
- Transactional mutations: NEVER evicted (gRPC backpressure applied instead)
- State-stream mutations: coalesced (latest-wins) before eviction
- Ephemeral mutations: dropped oldest-first
Unfreeze applies queued mutations in submission order.
- Scenario: Freeze queues mutations (line 146) — WHEN viewer presses Ctrl+Shift+F and agent submits mutations THEN mutations queued, tile content does not update, badges continue to render
- Scenario: Transactional mutations never dropped (line 150) — WHEN freeze queue full and transactional mutation (e.g. CreateTile) submitted THEN mutation not evicted; gRPC backpressure applied instead
- Scenario: Unfreeze applies queued mutations (line 154) — WHEN viewer unfreezes scene THEN all queued mutations applied in submission order in next available frame batch

### Requirement: Freeze Backpressure Signal (line 158)
Agents NOT informed that scene is frozen. At 80% queue capacity (800/1000 default), runtime sends MUTATION_QUEUE_PRESSURE via RuntimeError in MutationResult. On overflow for non-transactional mutations, runtime sends MUTATION_DROPPED. Signals fire for ANY queue-pressure scenario (not specifically freeze) to avoid leaking viewer state.
- Scenario: Queue pressure signal (line 163) — WHEN per-session freeze queue reaches 80% capacity THEN runtime sends MUTATION_QUEUE_PRESSURE in MutationResult response
- Scenario: Mutation dropped signal (line 167) — WHEN queue full and state-stream mutation evicted THEN runtime sends MUTATION_DROPPED for evicted mutation

### Requirement: Safe Mode and Freeze Interaction (Shell State Invariant) (line 124)
(Cross-reference with bead #2) safe_mode=true implies freeze_active=false. On safe mode activation: freeze cancelled, freeze queue discarded, freeze_active=false before other steps. Freeze attempted during safe mode ignored. After safe mode exit, freeze inactive.
- Scenario: Freeze ignored during safe mode (line 137) — WHEN viewer presses Ctrl+Shift+F while safe mode active THEN no effect

## Freeze behavior details (from prompt)
- Freeze queues agent mutations (does NOT reject them)
- Pauses resource budgets and degradation ladder advancement
- Defers attention signals
- Auto-unfreeze on configurable timeout (default 5 minutes)
- Freeze attempted during safe mode is ignored

## Acceptance criteria
- Mutations queued during freeze (not rejected, not applied)
- Tile content does not update while frozen; badges continue to render
- Queued mutations applied in submission order on unfreeze
- Traffic-class-aware overflow: transactional never evicted (backpressure), state-stream coalesced, ephemeral dropped oldest-first
- MUTATION_QUEUE_PRESSURE sent at 80% capacity (800/1000)
- MUTATION_DROPPED sent on non-transactional eviction
- Agents never told scene is frozen (signals are generic queue pressure, not freeze-specific)
- Budget timers paused during freeze
- Degradation ladder advancement paused during freeze
- Attention signals deferred during freeze
- Auto-unfreeze after configurable timeout (default 5 minutes)
- Freeze ignored during safe mode
- Freeze cancelled on safe mode entry (freeze queue discarded)
- After safe mode exit, freeze inactive

## v1-mandatory requirements
- Freeze Scene (line 141)
- Freeze Backpressure Signal (line 158)
- Safe Mode and Freeze Interaction (Shell State Invariant) (line 124) — cross-ref with bead #2

## v1-reserved exclusions
None for this bead.

## post-v1 exclusions
None for this bead.

## Crate/file location
crates/tze_hud_runtime/src/shell/freeze.rs — freeze state machine, per-session mutation queue, traffic-class-aware overflow, backpressure signals, auto-unfreeze timer

## What this does NOT include
- Chrome layer rendering (bead #1)
- Safe mode state machine and overlay (bead #2) — this bead implements the freeze queue and freeze state; bead #2 implements safe mode cancelling freeze
- Privacy redaction (bead #4)
- Disconnection badges (bead #5)
title: Shell #3: Freeze semantics

close_reason: Resolved by PR #43 (rig-6k5) — SceneId unified across all 7 RFCs as canonical wire type
description:
## Problem

The ChatGPT 5.4 Pro external review identified a cross-RFC issue: IDs are defined as binary in the Scene Contract (SceneId = 16-byte UUIDv7, ResourceId = 32-byte BLAKE3), but other RFCs may use string representations for the same concepts. Without a single canonical wire type definition that all RFCs import, each RFC risks defining its own ID encoding.

The RFC already defines the wire types well in §7.1 (protobuf messages SceneId and ResourceId with `bytes` fields), but this definition lives embedded within scene.proto rather than being factored into a shared types proto that other RFCs can import.

## Why This Matters

- RFC 0004 (Input) references SceneId for tile/node identification in input events
- RFC 0005 (Session/Protocol) imports scene types for session state
- RFC 0006 (Configuration) will need IDs for zone definitions loaded from config
- If each RFC defines or re-interprets IDs independently, wire format mismatches will surface at integration time
- The external review explicitly recommended: 'Define shared ID wire type and force every other RFC to reuse it'

## Sections Affected

- §1.1 Scheme Overview — add an explicit 'normative wire type' designation
- §7.1 protobuf — factor SceneId and ResourceId into a shared `tze_hud.common.v1` proto package
- §13 Related RFCs — add a note that all RFCs MUST import IDs from this common package

## Doctrine Reference

architecture.md §'Versioning and protocol compatibility' — 'MCP tools are versioned alongside the gRPC protocol' implies a single source of truth for shared types.

## Concrete Changes Needed

1. Create a `common.proto` or `ids.proto` package (`tze_hud.common.v1`) containing SceneId, ResourceId, Rect, Point2D, Rgba — the shared geometric and identity types
2. Update `scene.proto` to import from common.proto instead of defining these types inline
3. Add a normative statement in §1.1: 'SceneId and ResourceId are the canonical ID wire types for all tze_hud protos. Other RFCs MUST import these definitions from `tze_hud.common.v1`, not redefine them.'
4. Add SceneId encoding rules: always 16 bytes LE for UUIDv7, display as standard UUID string format (8-4-4-4-12 hex) in logs/errors/JSON contexts
5. Add ResourceId encoding rules: always 32 bytes for BLAKE3, display as 64-char lowercase hex in logs/errors/JSON contexts
title: RFC 0001: Define canonical shared ID wire type for cross-RFC reuse

description:
Implement agent event emission protocol, rate limiting, bare name regex validation, and tab_switch_on_event contract per scene-events/spec.md.

## Spec Requirements

### Requirement: Agent Event Emission Protocol (spec lines 107-123)
Agents emit scene events via EmitSceneEvent message on the session stream.
Emission requires emit_scene_event:<event_name> capability, where <event_name> is the agent-supplied bare name (e.g., emit_scene_event:doorbell.ring), not the fully-prefixed delivered event_type.
Runtime namespace-prefixes agent events as agent.<namespace>.<category>.<action> before delivery.
Agent event payload limited to 4KB maximum.
Reserved prefixes "system." and "scene." rejected (from bead #1 naming, enforced here at emission boundary).

### Requirement: Agent Event Rate Limiting (spec lines 126-133)
Rate limited to configurable maximum: default 10 events/second per agent session.
Sliding window: 1-second window, max count.
Events exceeding rate limit rejected with RuntimeError code AGENT_EVENT_RATE_EXCEEDED.

### Requirement: tab_switch_on_event Contract (spec lines 235-251)
Per-tab tab_switch_on_event configuration field names a scene-level event triggering automatic tab activation.
Agent events match against bare event name (before namespace prefixing) for agent-independence. E.g., tab configured with tab_switch_on_event = "doorbell.ring" fires for ANY agent emitting "doorbell.ring", regardless of which agent.
System events (system.* prefix) excluded from tab_switch_on_event matching.
Triggered tab switch subject to attention filtering (quiet hours, attention budget from bead #3).
Successful switch generates ActiveTabChangedEvent (event_type "scene.tab.active_changed").

### Bare Name Regex Validation
Bare names must match: [a-z][a-z0-9_]*(\.[a-z][a-z0-9_]*)+
Valid examples: doorbell.ring, fire.detected, weather.update, status.heartbeat.alive
Invalid examples: Doorbell.Ring, doorbell, .ring, doorbell., system.fake, scene.impersonate

## WHEN/THEN Scenarios (from spec)

- WHEN an agent without emit_scene_event:doorbell.ring capability attempts to emit "doorbell.ring" THEN the runtime MUST reject the emission with an error in EmitSceneEventResult (spec line 113)
- WHEN an agent with namespace "alarm_agent" and emit_scene_event:fire.detected capability emits "fire.detected" THEN the event MUST be delivered to subscribers with event_type "agent.alarm_agent.fire.detected" (spec line 118)
- WHEN an agent emits an event with a payload exceeding 4KB THEN the runtime MUST reject the emission (spec line 122)
- WHEN an agent emits 11 events within a 1-second sliding window (default limit: 10/s) THEN the 11th event MUST be rejected with AGENT_EVENT_RATE_EXCEEDED (spec line 133)
- WHEN a tab is configured with tab_switch_on_event = "doorbell.ring" and any agent with the capability emits "doorbell.ring" THEN the runtime MUST switch to that tab (subject to attention filtering) regardless of which agent emitted the event (spec line 242)
- WHEN quiet hours are active and a tab_switch_on_event fires with NORMAL interruption class THEN the tab switch MUST be deferred until quiet hours end (spec line 246)
- WHEN a tab is configured with tab_switch_on_event = "system.degradation_changed" THEN the runtime MUST NOT trigger a tab switch from system events; the system.* prefix is excluded from tab_switch_on_event matching (spec line 250)

## Acceptance Criteria

- Capability-gated emission: emit_scene_event:doorbell.ring required to emit "doorbell.ring"
- Emission without capability rejected with structured error in EmitSceneEventResult
- Namespace prefixing: "doorbell.ring" from namespace "doorbell_agent" becomes "agent.doorbell_agent.doorbell.ring"
- Payload size limit: >4KB rejected
- Rate limiting: 10 events/second default, sliding 1-second window, AGENT_EVENT_RATE_EXCEEDED on violation
- Bare name regex: [a-z][a-z0-9_]*(\.[a-z][a-z0-9_]*)+
  - Valid: doorbell.ring, fire.detected, weather.update.summary
  - Invalid: Doorbell.Ring (uppercase), doorbell (no dot), .ring (leading dot), 9invalid.start (digit start)
- tab_switch_on_event matches bare name (before prefix): "doorbell.ring" matches regardless of emitting agent namespace
- tab_switch_on_event fires tab switch generating ActiveTabChangedEvent (event_type "scene.tab.active_changed")
- System events excluded: tab_switch_on_event = "system.degradation_changed" never triggers
- tab_switch_on_event subject to attention filtering (quiet hours defer, budget coalesce)

## Relevant test scenes
- tab_switch (tab_switch_on_event triggering tab activation)
- chatty_dashboard_touch (rate limiting under high-frequency emission)
- policy_arbitration_collision (tab switch deferred by quiet hours)
- three_agents_contention (multiple agents emitting, agent-independent tab switch)

## Crate/file location
- Agent emission handler: crates/tze_hud_runtime/src/agent_events.rs
- Rate limiter: crates/tze_hud_runtime/src/agent_events/rate_limiter.rs
- Bare name validation: crates/tze_hud_scene/src/events/naming.rs (extends bead #1)
- tab_switch_on_event: crates/tze_hud_runtime/src/tab_switch_trigger.rs
- EmitSceneEvent/EmitSceneEventResult messages: crates/tze_hud_protocol/proto/session.proto

## v1-mandatory
- Agent Event Emission Protocol (spec lines 107-123)
- Agent Event Rate Limiting (spec lines 126-133)
- tab_switch_on_event Contract (spec lines 235-251)

## v1-reserved exclusions
None for this bead.

## post-v1 exclusions
- Inter-Agent Event Bus (spec lines 328-335) — direct agent-to-agent routing deferred
- Advanced Event Routing (spec lines 339-346) — content-based routing, event transformations deferred

## What this bead does NOT include
- Event envelope structure (bead #1)
- Naming convention types and taxonomy enum (bead #1 — this bead calls into naming validation)
- Subscription filtering and delivery mechanics (bead #2)
- Self-event suppression (bead #2)
- Interruption classification definitions (bead #3 — this bead uses the InterruptionClass enum)
- Quiet hours enforcement logic (bead #3 — tab_switch_on_event delegates to quiet hours gate)
- Attention budget enforcement (bead #3 — tab_switch_on_event delegates to budget check)
title: tab_switch_on_event and agent event emission

description:
The session-protocol spec's outline section and later requirement sections use different message names than each other, creating multiple vocabularies in one file. This will cause codegen, adapter naming, and test fixture forks.

**Session lifecycle:**
- Outline (line 61): SessionAccepted, SessionDenied
- Requirements (13+ locations): SessionEstablished, SessionError
- Fix: update outline to SessionEstablished, SessionError

**Session resume:**
- SessionResumeResult (lines 401-419) is used in requirements but NOT listed in the outline
- Fix: add SessionResumeResult to the outline

**Lease messages:**
- Outline (line 62): LeaseGrant, LeaseDenial  
- Requirements (lines 279, 285, 456): LeaseResponse (unified grant/deny/revoke)
- LeaseDenial appears only in the outline — orphaned, never used
- Requirements also use LeaseRequest with action=ACQUIRE/RENEW/RELEASE pattern (line 279/284) but outline lists separate LeaseRenew, LeaseRelease messages
- Fix: pick one pattern. Either unified LeaseRequest(action=X) + LeaseResponse, or separate messages

**Subscription messages:**
- Outline (line 61): SubscriptionAck
- Requirements (lines 461, 467): SubscriptionChangeResult
- Fix: pick one name. SubscriptionChangeResult matches the *Result naming pattern used elsewhere

**Zone publishing:**
- ZonePublishResult (lines 611-621) used in requirements but NOT listed in the outline
- Fix: add ZonePublishResult to outline

**Mutation messages in types.proto:**
- Outline (line 47): MutationBatchRequest, MutationBatchResponse
- Line 72: marks these as legacy DELETED
- Requirements: MutationBatch, MutationResult
- Fix: remove legacy names from types.proto listing

**Canonical v1 vocabulary (recommended):**
- SessionInit / SessionResume / SessionClose / SessionEstablished / SessionResumeResult / SessionError
- LeaseRequest / LeaseResponse / LeaseStateChange
- MutationBatch / MutationResult
- SubscriptionChange / SubscriptionChangeResult
- ZonePublish / ZonePublishResult
- HudSession (already consistent)
title: Fix session-protocol outline/requirement message name contradictions

description: RFC 0010 §2.2 still uses old 3-segment grammar 'agent.<namespace>.<event_name>' and scene events without 'scene.' prefix. The scene-events spec now mandates 'scene.<object>.<action>' and 4-segment agent events 'agent.<namespace>.<category>.<action>'. RFC should be updated for full alignment. Discovered during rig-bxh.
title: Update RFC 0010 §2.2 event naming grammar to match spec

close_reason: ID types aligned in Rounds 7+9: batch_id, lease_id, created_ids use SceneId; session-level identifiers remain string
description:
## Problem

RFC 0001 (Scene Contract) §8 defines `SceneId` as a typed protobuf message with a `bytes` field (16-byte UUIDv7 binary representation). All scene objects (tabs, tiles, nodes, leases, zones) use `SceneId` for identity.

RFC 0005 uses plain `string` for all IDs in its proto schema:
- `MutationBatch.batch_id` — `string` (described as UUIDv7)
- `MutationBatch.lease_id` — `string`
- `MutationResult.created_ids` — `repeated string`
- `SessionInit.agent_id` — `string`

The `batch_id` is documented as UUIDv7 but encoded as `string`, while RFC 0001's `SceneId` encodes UUIDv7 as `bytes`. The `lease_id` and `created_ids` fields refer to scene objects that are `SceneId` typed in RFC 0001.

This creates a type mismatch at the protobuf boundary: the session proto uses `string` where the scene proto uses `SceneId` (bytes). Implementations will need ad-hoc conversion at every boundary.

## Affected Sections

- §3.3 (MutationBatch — `batch_id`, `lease_id`, `created_ids`)
- §6.1 (`session_token` — this one is correctly `string` since it's opaque/non-scene)
- §9 (Proto schema — all `string` ID fields that refer to scene objects)

## Doctrine Reference

- RFC 0001 §1.1: 'SceneId: UUIDv7 for all live scene objects' — this is the canonical ID type.
- RFC 0001 §8: Proto schema defines `message SceneId { bytes bytes = 1; }`

## Concrete Change

1. Import `scene_service.proto` and use `SceneId` for `lease_id` and `created_ids` in `MutationBatch`/`MutationResult`.
2. For `batch_id`: decide whether it should also be `SceneId` (it's UUIDv7) or remain `string` (it's not a scene object). Recommendation: make it `SceneId` for consistency since it's documented as UUIDv7.
3. Keep `agent_id` and `session_token` as `string` — they are not scene objects and have different identity semantics.
4. Document the distinction: scene-object IDs use `SceneId` (binary UUIDv7), session-level IDs use `string`.

## Rationale

Type drift between the scene proto and session proto creates unnecessary conversion code and potential for subtle bugs (string vs binary UUID representation). Using the canonical `SceneId` type at the proto level makes the type system enforce consistency.
title: RFC 0005: align ID types with RFC 0001 SceneId (bytes, not string)

description:
# Focus Tree and Management

Implement the focus model per input-model/spec.md.

## Spec Requirements (with line numbers from spec.md)
- **Requirement: Focus Tree Structure** (lines 11-13, spec.md) — per-tab focus tree, at most one focus owner per tab, focus owner is one of: None, chrome element, tile, or specific HitRegionNode within a tile. Tab switch preserves focus state without generating events on suspended tab.
- **Requirement: Click-to-Focus Acquisition** (lines 27-29, spec.md) — runtime transfers focus to hit target before forwarding pointer event; focus transfer occurs before PointerDownEvent dispatch. Passthrough tiles do not acquire focus.
- **Requirement: Programmatic Focus Request** (lines 42-44, spec.md) — agents request focus via FocusRequest; runtime denies with DENIED if steal=false and another agent holds focus; denies with INVALID if tile/node doesn't exist or isn't owned by requesting agent.
- **Requirement: Focus Transfer on Destruction** (lines 57-58, spec.md) — focused tile/node destroyed causes fallback to previously focused element or FocusOwner::None.
- **Requirement: Focus Isolation Between Agents** (lines 67-69, spec.md) — agents cannot observe or query focus state of tiles they don't own.
- **Requirement: Focus Cycling** (lines 79-81, spec.md) — NAVIGATE_NEXT/NAVIGATE_PREV traverse in tile z-order, depth-first left-to-right within tiles, Passthrough tiles excluded, wraps at end. Chrome tab bar excluded from content focus cycle.
- **Requirement: Focus Events Dispatch** (lines 93-94, spec.md) — FocusGainedEvent with source (CLICK, TAB_KEY, PROGRAMMATIC, COMMAND_INPUT), FocusLostEvent with reason (CLICK_ELSEWHERE, TAB_KEY, PROGRAMMATIC, TILE_DESTROYED, TAB_SWITCHED, LEASE_REVOKED, AGENT_DISCONNECTED, COMMAND_INPUT).
- **Requirement: Focus Ring Visual Indication** (lines 400-402, spec.md) — 2px solid ring, system accent color, minimum 3:1 contrast ratio, rendered in chrome layer above all agent content, updated within Stage 2.

## WHEN/THEN Acceptance Scenarios
- WHEN a tab has focus owner set to Node(tile_id=T2, node_id=N1) THEN no other element in the same tab SHALL report itself as the focus owner (line 18)
- WHEN Tab A has focus on Node(T2,N1) and user switches to Tab B THEN Tab A's focus state preserved without generating FocusLostEvent; switching back restores without FocusGainedEvent from restoration (line 22)
- WHEN user clicks on HitRegionNode with accepts_focus=true in tile T1 THEN runtime sets focus owner to Node(T1, node_id) and dispatches FocusGainedEvent(source=CLICK) before PointerDownEvent (line 32)
- WHEN user clicks on tile with input_mode=Passthrough THEN focus owner does NOT change (line 37)
- WHEN agent sends FocusRequest(steal=false) and no other agent holds focus THEN FocusResponse(result=GRANTED) and FocusGainedEvent(source=PROGRAMMATIC) (line 48)
- WHEN agent sends FocusRequest(steal=false) while another agent holds focus THEN FocusResponse(result=DENIED) (line 52)
- WHEN focused node N1 destroyed and previous focus was Node(T2,N2) THEN focus transfers to Node(T2,N2) and FocusLostEvent(reason=TILE_DESTROYED) dispatched (line 63)
- WHEN Agent A owns T1 and Agent B owns T2 with focus on T2/N1 THEN Agent A receives no event indicating T2/N1 has focus (line 73)
- WHEN scene has tiles at z=1,3,8 and focus on N2 THEN Tab cycles N2→N3→Tile(z=8)→wrap to N1 (line 84)
- WHEN focus cycling encounters Passthrough tile THEN tile and all its nodes skipped (line 88)
- WHEN user presses Tab and focus moves to N1 in T1 THEN agent receives FocusGainedEvent(source=TAB_KEY) and previous owner receives FocusLostEvent(reason=TAB_KEY) (line 99)
- WHEN focus transfers to HitRegionNode THEN focus ring rendered at node bounds in same frame (line 406)

## Latency budgets
- Focus ring update: within Stage 2 of frame pipeline (< 500µs p99)
- Local feedback for focus change: < 4ms p99 input_to_local_ack

## Test scenes
- input_highlight — validates focus ring rendering
- Property testing: focus uniqueness invariant holds under random transitions

## Crate/file location
crates/tze_hud_input/src/ (focus.rs, focus_tree.rs)

## Depends on
- Epic 1 hit regions (HitRegionNode, scene graph)

## v1-mandatory
Focus Tree Structure, Click-to-Focus Acquisition, Programmatic Focus Request, Focus Transfer on Destruction, Focus Isolation Between Agents, Focus Cycling, Focus Events Dispatch, Focus Ring Visual Indication

## v1-reserved exclusions
- Configurable Tab Order (agent tab_index) — post-v1, uses z-order only in v1
- Platform Accessibility Bridge — a11y tree data structures shipped but platform API deferred
- Full IME focus integration — IME composition cancelled on focus loss is v1-reserved

## post-v1 exclusions
- Configurable Tab Order (tab_index field reserved in proto but ignored in v1)

## Does NOT include
- Pointer event dispatch (bead #2)
- Keyboard event types or command input model (bead #3)
- Pointer capture protocol (bead #5)
- Local feedback rendering pipeline (bead #6, though focus ring rendering is co-owned)
title: Input: Focus tree and management

description:
Epic 1: Scene Graph Core — the foundation data model that all other v1 subsystems build on.

## Dependencies
- Epic 0 (rig-ge28): test scenes, Layer 0 assertions, trait contracts
- Depended on by: Epics 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 10 (nearly everything builds on scene types)

## Primary spec
openspec/changes/v1-mvp-standards/specs/scene-graph/spec.md

## Secondary specs
- resource-store/spec.md (ResourceId)
- validation-framework/spec.md (Layer 0 assertions, test scene registry)
- input-model/spec.md (HitRegionNode Primitive, lines 248-276)
- session-protocol/spec.md (MutationBatch Processing, line 294)

## Context
The scene graph is the pure, renderer-independent data model at the center of tze_hud. It owns identity types, the scene hierarchy, atomic mutations, namespace isolation, zone ontology, and hit-test data. The existing crate crates/tze_hud_scene/ has a partial implementation in src/graph.rs with tiles, nodes, leases, zones, sync groups, and hit regions. Epic 0 provides test scenes and Layer 0 invariant assertions that this epic's implementation must satisfy.

## Sub-beads (6 implementation tasks)
1. SceneId and ResourceId identity types (no internal deps)
2. Scene hierarchy: Scene→Tab→Tile→Node (depends on #1)
3. Atomic batch mutations (depends on #2)
4. Zone ontology: type, instance, publication, occupancy (depends on #2)
5. Hit-test data model (depends on #2)
6. Deterministic scene snapshots (depends on #2, #4)

## Dependency chain
#1 Identity Types → #2 Scene Hierarchy → #3 Atomic Batches
                                       → #4 Zone Ontology → #6 Snapshots
                                       → #5 Hit-Test Data

## v1 scope
- v1-mandatory: All 6 sub-beads implement v1-mandatory requirements from scene-graph/spec.md
- v1-reserved: Mobile profile negotiation (one scene model, two profiles — profile differences are config, not separate code paths)
- post-v1: Incremental Diff (line 342), VideoSurfaceRef (line 351), Zone Occupancy Query API (line 360), persistent resource store (resource-store/spec.md)

## Doctrine guardrails
- LLMs must never sit in the frame loop — scene graph is pure data, no rendering
- Arrival time ≠ presentation time — timing semantics carried in payloads
- Screen is sovereign — runtime owns composition, agents request via leases
- Errors are teaching surfaces — BatchRejected has structured errors with correction hints
- Presence requires governance — namespace isolation, lease-gated mutations, capability checks

description: The attention.rs module (PR #128) introduces a correct pure evaluate_attention() function that properly handles LOW as Discard (not Shed). Migrate stack.rs to delegate to evaluate_attention() to eliminate the duplicate implementations and ensure the authoritative behavior is used everywhere.

description: Subscription struct has pub fields (category, filter_prefix) that allow constructing invalid states without going through the validated constructors. A caller can set filter_prefix to a prefix outside the category's default prefix namespace, bypassing capability gating. Fields should be made private with accessor methods; constructors/change application should validate that filter_prefix.starts_with(category_prefix(category)). This was identified in PR #117 review as a hardening/encapsulation task — current callers are controlled so no v1 correctness risk.

close_reason: RFC 0009 rewritten with formal 7-level arbitration stack, conflict resolution rules, three evaluation pipelines, failure arbitration tiers, capability registry, freeze semantics, override taxonomy, quantitative requirements. Resolves rig-nev, rig-81i, rig-tew, rig-zeb, rig-9v1, rig-vbi.
description:
## Problem

The doctrine (architecture.md, security.md) defines a priority order across human override, capabilities, privacy, interruption, attention budget, zone contention, and degradation. This arbitration order is currently spread across 4+ RFCs with conflicting implementations:

- **RFC 0001** §2.5: zone contention policies (latest-wins, stack, merge-by-key, replace) — but no cross-zone arbitration
- **RFC 0002** §4-5: degradation ladder and budget enforcement — uses lease_priority as sort key but doesn't define it
- **RFC 0006** §5/§7: capability configuration and privacy redaction — redaction_style owned by both [privacy] and [chrome]
- **RFC 0007** §4-5: human override controls, safe mode, freeze — contradicts RFC 0002 on GPU failure response

Concrete conflicts caused by missing arbitration authority:
- Safe mode revokes vs suspends leases (rig-tew)
- GPU failure exits vs enters safe mode (rig-nev + rig-81i)
- Redaction owned by privacy and chrome (rig-zeb)
- Freeze: silent vs advisory to agents (rig-9v1)

## Proposed RFC 0009 Structure

1. **Arbitration Stack** — Formal priority order with precedence rules:
   - Level 0: Human override (always wins, local, instant)
   - Level 1: Safety (safe mode, critical errors)
   - Level 2: Privacy (viewer context, redaction)
   - Level 3: Security (capability enforcement, lease validity)
   - Level 4: Attention (interruption classes, quiet hours)
   - Level 5: Resource (budget enforcement, degradation)
   - Level 6: Content (zone contention, agent priority)
2. **Conflict Resolution Rules** — When two levels conflict, how to resolve. Explicit tie-breaking.
3. **Policy Evaluation Pipeline** — How the runtime evaluates the stack per-frame and per-event.
4. **Override Semantics** — Formal definition of what 'override' means at each level (suppress, redirect, transform, block).
5. **Failure Arbitration** — Tiered response to failures: recoverable → safe mode → exit. Resolves RFC 0002 vs 0007 conflict.
6. **Redaction Ownership** — Single authority for redaction decisions. Resolves rig-zeb.

## Doctrine References

- security.md §Human override: 'Cannot be intercepted, delayed, or vetoed'
- privacy.md §Viewer context: viewer class determines visibility
- failure.md §Degradation ladder: ordered severity levels
- attention.md §Interruption classes: quiet hours, priority classification

## Related Beads

- rig-tew: safe-mode contradiction (RFC 0007)
- rig-nev + rig-81i: GPU failure path conflict (RFC 0002 vs 0007)
- rig-zeb: redaction_style dual ownership (RFC 0006)
- rig-9v1: freeze advisory model (RFC 0007)

## Acceptance Criteria

1. Complete arbitration stack with formal precedence rules
2. Every policy decision in RFCs 0001-0007 traceable to a stack level
3. All identified cross-RFC conflicts resolvable by referencing this RFC
4. Failure arbitration tiered model defined (resolves rig-nev/rig-81i)
5. Redaction ownership assigned to single authority (resolves rig-zeb)
title: Write Policy & Arbitration RFC (new RFC 0009)

description:
## Problem

RFC 0004 makes focus, capture, gestures, IME, and full platform accessibility all normative at once. The RFC does not clearly distinguish between behavior that must ship in v1 and behavior that is defined now for future-proofing but can be deferred.

v1.md §V1 ships → Interaction is explicit about what v1 requires:
- Mouse/pointer input with hit testing
- Touch input on supported platforms
- Local-first feedback (press states, focus rings, hover highlights)
- Input events forwarded to owning agent
- At least one retained interactive primitive (hit_region node)

v1.md does NOT list IME, full platform accessibility bridging (AT-SPI2/UIA/NSAccessibility), or gesture recognition as v1 requirements. These are doctrinal commitments (presence.md §Interaction, §IME, §Accessibility) but the v1 scope boundary is silent on them.

Mixing v1-mandatory and reserved behavior in a single normative RFC creates implementation risk: implementers may try to build everything at once, or may not know what to prioritize.

## Affected Sections
- §3 Gesture Model — v1 needs basic click/drag; full gesture pipeline with arbiter may be deferred
- §4 IME — CJK/emoji/voice input is important but complex; may be v1+1
- §5 Accessibility — full AT-SPI2/UIA/NSAccessibility bridge is a major platform integration; presence.md requires it doctrinally but v1.md does not list it
- §13 Non-Goals already defers some items; this issue is about adding a 'v1 deferred' tier between 'normative' and 'non-goal'

## Concrete Change
1. Add a '## V1 Scope' section (or a 'V1 / Post-V1' column to the DR table) that explicitly marks each capability:
   - **V1-mandatory:** Focus model (§1), Capture model (§2), Local feedback (§6), HitRegionNode primitives (§7), Event dispatch (§8), basic pointer events (§7.3), keyboard events (§7.4), basic hit-testing
   - **V1-reserved (defined but may defer):** Gesture pipeline with full arbiter (§3) — v1 could ship with simple tap/click recognition only; IME composition (§4); Full platform a11y bridge (§5) — v1 could ship with a11y tree structure but defer platform API integration
   - **Post-v1 (already in §13):** DnD, scroll, gamepad, stylus, multi-pointer
2. Mark each section header with its tier, e.g., '## 3. Gesture Model [V1-reserved]'
3. For reserved sections, state the minimal v1 fallback (e.g., 'v1 may ship with tap-only recognition; full gesture pipeline activates post-v1')

## Doctrine Reference
- v1.md: explicit v1 scope boundary defines what must ship
- presence.md §Interaction, §IME, §Accessibility: doctrinal commitments that establish the direction but not the v1 deadline
- architecture.md §Node type extensibility: 'The set of node types is fixed per compositor version' — same philosophy applies to input capabilities

## Rationale
Clearly separating v1-mandatory from reserved behavior helps implementers prioritize correctly and reduces the risk of v1 scope creep. The reserved definitions remain in the RFC for contract completeness but are flagged as not blocking v1 ship.
title: RFC 0004: Split v1-mandatory vs reserved/deferred input behavior

description: RFC 0009 §11.5 uses outdated attention budget defaults (6/12 per minute) and doctrine class names (Gentle/Normal/Urgent) superseded by RFC 0010 §3.1 (20/10 per minute; SILENT/LOW/NORMAL/HIGH/CRITICAL). Discovered during rig-9d9.
title: Align RFC 0009 §11.5 with RFC 0010 attention budget and enum vocabulary

description:
Implement upload and content-addressed deduplication for the resource store. Agent sends raw bytes over session stream; runtime computes BLAKE3 hash to produce ResourceId (raw 32-byte digest, NOT hex-encoded). If ResourceId already exists, return existing reference with was_deduplicated = true (no re-upload, no additional storage consumed).

## Parent epic
rig-tylk (Epic 10: Resource Store)

## Dependencies
- Epic 0: ResourceStore trait contract
- Epic 1: ResourceId type definition
- Epic 6: session upload stream transport

## Spec requirements (resource-store/spec.md)

### Requirement: Content-Addressed Resource Identity (lines 5-8, v1-mandatory)
All resources identified by BLAKE3 hash of raw input bytes, stored as 32 bytes. Two uploads of identical bytes MUST produce the same ResourceId.

### Requirement: Resource Immutability (lines 20-24, v1-mandatory)
Once stored, content at a given ResourceId never changes. Dedup hit returns existing content unchanged with was_deduplicated = true.

### Requirement: V1 Resource Type Enumeration (lines 31-34, v1-mandatory)
V1 supports exactly five types: IMAGE_RGBA8, IMAGE_PNG, IMAGE_JPEG, FONT_TTF, FONT_OTF. Unsupported types rejected with RESOURCE_UNSUPPORTED_TYPE.

### Requirement: Upload Protocol via Session Stream (lines 46-49, v1-mandatory)
Resources uploaded via session stream (RFC 0005). Multiplexed over agent's existing bidirectional gRPC session stream. No separate upload RPC.

### Requirement: Chunked Upload Flow (lines 57-60, v1-mandatory)
Resources > 64 KiB: three-phase flow (ResourceUploadStart, sequential ResourceUploadChunk max 64 KiB, ResourceUploadComplete). Runtime validates BLAKE3 hash against expected_hash.

### Requirement: Small Resource Fast Path (lines 72-74, v1-mandatory)
Resources <= 64 KiB: single ResourceUploadStart with inline_data. No chunk/complete messages required.

### Requirement: Upload Validation (lines 83-87, v1-mandatory)
Six-step validation: (1) upload_resource capability, (2) BLAKE3 hash integrity, (3) per-resource size limit, (4) texture_bytes_total budget, (5) v1-supported type, (6) content decodes successfully. Each failure produces a specific error code.

### Requirement: Content-Addressed Deduplication (lines 102-104, v1-mandatory)
Dedup check on ResourceUploadStart via expected_hash. If found, return ResourceStored immediately with was_deduplicated = true, skip chunk sequence. Dedup check latency < 100 microseconds.

### Requirement: Concurrent Upload Limits (lines 117-119, v1-mandatory)
Max 4 concurrent uploads per agent. 5th rejected with RESOURCE_TOO_MANY_UPLOADS.

### Requirement: Upload Capability Gate (lines 181-183, v1-mandatory)
upload_resource capability required (in default resident set). Guest agents rejected with RESOURCE_CAPABILITY_DENIED.

### Requirement: Per-Resource Size Limits (lines 281-283, v1-mandatory)
Max input size 16 MiB (configurable). Max decoded texture 64 MiB (configurable). Max texture dimension 8192px. Rejected with RESOURCE_SIZE_EXCEEDED.

### Requirement: Per-Runtime Total Limits (lines 296-298, v1-mandatory)
Max total texture memory 512 MiB (configurable). Max font cache 64 MiB (configurable). Max concurrent resources 4096 (configurable). Rejected with RESOURCE_BUDGET_EXCEEDED.

### Requirement: Upload Rate Limiting (lines 307-309, v1-mandatory)
1 MiB/s per agent (configurable), sliding 1s window. Back-pressure via gRPC flow control.

### Requirement: BLAKE3 Hash Performance (lines 329-331, v1-mandatory)
Hash computation < 1ms for 1 MiB resource.

## WHEN/THEN acceptance scenarios

WHEN Agent A uploads a 500KB PNG and Agent B uploads the same 500KB PNG
THEN both MUST receive the same ResourceId (BLAKE3 hash of the raw bytes) [line 11-12]

WHEN a ResourceId is computed for any resource
THEN it MUST be exactly 32 bytes (256-bit BLAKE3 digest) [line 14-16]

WHEN a resource with ResourceId X is stored and a subsequent upload produces ResourceId X
THEN the store MUST return the original content unchanged and report was_deduplicated = true [line 26-27]

WHEN an agent uploads a resource with type IMAGE_PNG
THEN the upload MUST be accepted (assuming all other validation passes) [line 37-38]

WHEN an agent uploads a resource with type VIDEO_H264
THEN the upload MUST be rejected with RESOURCE_UNSUPPORTED_TYPE error [line 41-42]

WHEN an agent uploads a 200 KiB image via ResourceUploadStart followed by 4 chunks and ResourceUploadComplete
THEN the runtime MUST compute BLAKE3, verify against expected_hash, decode, return ResourceStored [line 63-64]

WHEN computed BLAKE3 hash does not match expected_hash
THEN runtime MUST reject with RESOURCE_HASH_MISMATCH [line 67-68]

WHEN an agent uploads a 32 KiB icon with inline_data in ResourceUploadStart
THEN runtime MUST accept without requiring chunk or complete messages [line 78-79]

WHEN a guest agent (without upload_resource capability) attempts upload
THEN MUST be rejected with RESOURCE_CAPABILITY_DENIED [line 89-90]

WHEN an agent uploads bytes claiming IMAGE_PNG but containing corrupted data
THEN MUST be rejected with RESOURCE_DECODE_ERROR [line 93-94]

WHEN an agent whose texture_bytes_total is at lease limit attempts another upload
THEN MUST be rejected with RESOURCE_BUDGET_EXCEEDED [line 97-98]

WHEN an agent starts upload with expected_hash matching an already-stored resource
THEN runtime MUST return ResourceStored immediately with was_deduplicated = true [line 108-109]

WHEN runtime performs a dedup lookup
THEN lookup MUST complete within 100 microseconds [line 112-113]

WHEN an agent has 4 uploads in flight and sends a 5th ResourceUploadStart
THEN runtime MUST reject with RESOURCE_TOO_MANY_UPLOADS [line 123-124]

WHEN an agent uploads a resource of 20 MiB (exceeding 16 MiB limit)
THEN MUST be rejected with RESOURCE_SIZE_EXCEEDED [line 287-288]

WHEN a 1 MiB PNG decodes to a texture exceeding 64 MiB
THEN decode MUST be aborted with RESOURCE_SIZE_EXCEEDED [line 291-292]

WHEN total texture memory across all agents reaches 512 MiB and a new upload arrives
THEN MUST be rejected with RESOURCE_BUDGET_EXCEEDED [line 301-303]

WHEN an agent attempts to upload at 2 MiB/s
THEN runtime MUST back-pressure via gRPC flow control [line 313-314]

WHEN a 1 MiB resource is uploaded and BLAKE3 hash is computed
THEN hash computation MUST complete within 1 millisecond [line 335-336]

## Relevant test scenes
Upload-centric scenes from the validation-framework's 25-scene registry (resource upload, dedup verification, type validation)

## Crate/file location
New crate: crates/tze_hud_resource/ — upload.rs, dedup.rs, validation.rs, types.rs

## Wire vs debug format
ResourceId is raw 32 bytes on wire (protobuf bytes field), hex-encoded lowercase string for logging/CLI.

## What this bead does NOT include
- Reference counting (bead #2)
- Garbage collection (bead #2)
- Cross-agent sharing semantics (bead #3)
- V1 ephemerality enforcement (bead #4)
- Font asset management (covered by bead #2 for holds, separate font subsystem epic)
- GPU texture compression BC7/ASTC (post-v1, spec lines 362-365)
- Persistent resource store (post-v1, spec lines 373-376)
- Post-v1 resource types: VIDEO_H264, VIDEO_VP9, AUDIO_OPUS, AUDIO_AAC, IMAGE_SVG, WASM_MODULE (post-v1, spec lines 384-386)

## v1-mandatory
All requirements listed above.

## v1-reserved exclusions
None for this bead.

## post-v1 exclusions
- GPU Texture Compression BC7/ASTC (spec lines 362-365)
- Persistent Resource Store (spec lines 373-376)
- Post-V1 Resource Types (spec lines 384-386)
title: RS-1: Content-addressed upload and deduplication

description:
The runtime-kernel/spec.md Requirement: Window Modes (line 172-173) is v1-mandatory and currently unimplemented. WindowSurface in crates/tze_hud_compositor/src/surface.rs has acquire_frame() as unimplemented!().

**What must be built:**

1. **WindowSurface::acquire_frame()** — replace panic with real swapchain acquisition:
   - Call surface.get_current_texture()
   - Create TextureView from swapchain texture
   - Return CompositorFrame with SurfaceTexture as the guard

2. **Compositor::new_windowed()** — factory method alongside existing new_headless():
   - Accept winit Window handle
   - Create wgpu::Surface from window
   - Configure surface with window dimensions and present mode

3. **WindowedRuntime** — analogous to HeadlessRuntime:
   - Wire winit::event_loop on main thread (per Requirement: Main Thread Responsibilities, line 33)
   - Main thread runs winit event loop, input drain, local feedback, frame presentation
   - Compositor thread handles scene commit, render encode, GPU submit (per Requirement: Thread Model, line 20)
   - Cross-thread signaling via FrameReadySignal (tokio::sync::watch)
   - On macOS/Metal: only main thread calls surface.present()

4. **Input integration** — feed winit WindowEvent into InputProcessor:
   - PointerEvent from CursorMoved, MouseInput
   - KeyboardEvent from KeyboardInput
   - Map to existing InputProcessor.process() pipeline

**Spec refs:**
- runtime-kernel/spec.md Requirement: Window Modes (lines 172-188)
- runtime-kernel/spec.md Requirement: Main Thread Responsibilities (lines 33-44)
- runtime-kernel/spec.md Requirement: Thread Model (lines 19-30)
- runtime-kernel/spec.md Requirement: Headless Mode (line 199: 'same process, code path, and pipeline as windowed mode')

**Acceptance criteria:**
- cargo run -p vertical_slice (without --headless) opens a window and renders
- WindowSurface::acquire_frame() returns real swapchain textures
- Frame pipeline runs at target_fps (60fps default)
- Input events from winit flow through InputProcessor
- Window resize handled (surface reconfigured)

**v1-mandatory. Crate: crates/tze_hud_runtime/, crates/tze_hud_compositor/src/surface.rs**

description:
Input-model/spec.md defines its own proto file and timestamp convention that contradict two sibling specs:

**Proto layout contradiction (vs session-protocol lines 41-99):**
- Input-model line 368 defines 'input.proto (package tze_hud.input.v1)' with InputEnvelope (22-variant oneof), EventBatch
- Session-protocol says input events belong in events.proto (package tze_hud.protocol.v1) as InputEvent, InputEventKind
- No separate input.proto exists in the three-file layout

**Timestamp convention contradiction (vs timing-model lines 23-30):**
- Input-model uses '_hw_us' at lines 279, 285, 290, 331, 337, 363, 379
- Timing-model mandates only '_wall_us' or '_mono_us' suffixes
- '_hw_us' is described as 'OS hardware event timestamp, monotonic domain' — if it's monotonic, it should be '_mono_us'

**Resolution:**
1. Replace all '_hw_us' with '_mono_us' (it IS monotonic per line 279's own description). If hardware timestamp precision needs to be distinguished from other monotonic timestamps, add a note in timing-model blessing a '_hw_mono_us' suffix or simply use '_mono_us' with a field-level comment.
2. Remove the input.proto / tze_hud.input.v1 package definition. Input event types that session-protocol expects in events.proto (InputEvent, InputEventKind) stay there. The richer 22-variant InputEnvelope and EventBatch either:
   a) Replace InputEvent/InputEventKind in events.proto (if they're the v1 target), or
   b) Are acknowledged as a spec-level mismatch that needs reconciliation — decide which schema is canonical.
3. Remove 'scene.proto' import reference (SceneId lives in types.proto per session-protocol).
title: Align input-model with session-protocol proto layout and timing-model clock domains

description:
## Problem

RFC 0006 §5.4 defines `tab_switch_on_event` as a string field that names a "scene-level event" (e.g., `"doorbell.ring"`, `"alert.fire"`). The field cross-references RFC 0004 (Input) for event names. However, the examples reveal a broader scene-event taxonomy that goes beyond input events:

- `"doorbell.ring"` is not an input event (mouse/keyboard/touch) — it is a device/sensor event.
- `"alert.fire"` is a system-level event, not an input.
- The RFC assigns `emit_scene_event:<event_name>` as an agent capability (§6.3), meaning agents can fire arbitrary named events.

This is a scene-event bus, not input. RFC 0004 (Input) is about pointer, touch, keyboard, focus, and gesture routing — not about named semantic events like "doorbell.ring". Conflating these creates an implicit dependency: RFC 0006 requires a scene-event namespace and dispatch contract that RFC 0004 does not define and should not own.

## Affected Sections

- RFC 0006 §2.4 (`tab_switch_on_event` field and its cross-reference to RFC 0004)
- RFC 0006 §5.4 (Tab Switching Policy — event name semantics)
- RFC 0006 §6.3 (capability identifier `emit_scene_event:<event_name>`)

## Doctrine Reference

- architecture.md §"Policy arbitration": references "scene-level events" in the interruption evaluation chain
- presence.md §"Inter-agent events": "Agents can subscribe to a shared event bus for coarse-grained coordination signals"
- v1.md §"V1 explicitly defers": "No inter-agent event bus beyond basic scene topology changes" — this constrains the v1 scope of scene events

## Required Change

1. Acknowledge that `tab_switch_on_event` and `emit_scene_event` define a scene-event contract distinct from input events.
2. Define the v1 scene-event namespace explicitly in RFC 0006 or in a companion section:
   - Reserved event prefix: system events (`system.degradation`, `system.agent_connected`, etc.)
   - Agent-emittable events: require `emit_scene_event:<name>` capability
   - Event naming convention (dotted hierarchy: `<source>.<action>`)
   - Event payload: at minimum, event name + timestamp + source agent ID
3. Remove the cross-reference to RFC 0004 for event names. RFC 0004 owns input routing; scene events are a separate contract. Cross-reference presence.md §"Inter-agent events" instead.
4. Add an Open Question about whether scene events should be their own RFC (possibly a lightweight one) or remain embedded in RFC 0006 as a subsection.
5. Scope the v1 event taxonomy to what v1.md permits: tab switches, agent join/depart, and explicitly-emitted named events (like doorbell.ring). No inter-agent message passing.
title: RFC 0006: extract scene-event taxonomy from tab_switch_on_event

description:
PR #103 fixed the spec outline to match requirements, but the implementation in crates/tze_hud_protocol/proto/session.proto still uses old names:

1. SessionResumeResult: not defined. Server currently sends SessionEstablished on resume. Need to add SessionResumeResult message and use it in session_server.rs on resume.

2. SubscriptionAck -> SubscriptionChangeResult: proto still defines SubscriptionAck; need to rename message and update ServerMessage.payload field (subscription_ack -> subscription_change_result) and all server-side code.

3. ZonePublish / ZonePublishResult: not defined in session.proto. Need to add these as distinct session-stream messages (separate from mutation-based PublishToZoneMutation path). Durable zone publishes get ZonePublishResult; ephemeral are fire-and-forget.

Discovered during review of PR #103.
title: Align session.proto implementation with spec: SessionResumeResult, SubscriptionChangeResult, ZonePublish/ZonePublishResult

description:
## Problem

Section 3.3 defines frame quantization as: a present_at_us timestamp T is 'in scope' for frame F if T <= frame_F_vsync_us + frame_budget_us / 2. This means a mutation with present_at_us = V + 8ms (where V is frame F's vsync time) would be applied at frame F, whose presentation timestamp is V.

Section 5.3 states: 'The compositor guarantees that a mutation with present_at_us = T will be applied no earlier than T.'

These are contradictory. The half-frame rule allows early presentation: if T = V + 8ms and the mutation is applied at frame F (vsync at V), the content appears at time V, which is 8ms BEFORE T. This violates the 'no earlier than T' invariant.

## Affected Sections

- Section 3.3 (Timestamp Resolution / Frame quantization)
- Section 5.3 (Presentation Deadline)
- Section 5.3 pending queue drain logic

## Doctrine Reference

architecture.md 'Time is a first-class API concept': 'Arrival time is not presentation time.' Presenting content before its declared present_at violates this invariant.

## Concrete Change

Two options:
1. Remove the half-frame tolerance from the quantization rule. Make the rule: T is in scope for frame F if T <= frame_F_vsync_us. This preserves the 'no earlier than T' guarantee but may cause off-by-one frame delays for timestamps that fall just after a vsync.
2. Keep the half-frame tolerance but weaken the guarantee in Section 5.3 to: 'applied no earlier than T minus half a frame period.' This is honest but less clean.

Recommendation: Option 1. The 'no earlier than T' guarantee is more valuable than avoiding occasional one-frame delays. The pending queue drain condition in Section 5.3 should also be updated to match.

## Rationale

This is a correctness bug that could cause visible timing errors in synchronized content (e.g., subtitles appearing slightly before their audio cue).
title: RFC 0003: Fix frame-quantization correctness bug (half-frame vs no-earlier-than contradiction)

description:
# Epic 11: System Shell

The system shell owns the topmost render layer (chrome) and all human override state. It is the exclusive owner of freeze/safe-mode state transitions — policy-arbitration evaluates but does not transition shell state. The existing compositor has a layered surface stack (background, content, chrome) but the shell behavior logic is not yet implemented.

## Dependencies
- Epic 0 (test infrastructure) — rig-ge28
- Epic 2 (compositor/chrome layer) — not yet created
- Epic 4 (lease suspension) — not yet created
- Epic 8 (policy stack) — not yet created

## Depended on by
- Epic 12 (integration tests)

## Primary spec
openspec/changes/v1-mvp-standards/specs/system-shell/spec.md

## Secondary specs
- lease-governance/spec.md (suspension: lines 92-130)
- policy-arbitration/spec.md (Level 0 overrides: line 48)
- configuration/spec.md (privacy config: line 215)

## Sub-beads (5)
1. Chrome layer sovereignty
2. Safe mode: suspend and resume
3. Freeze semantics
4. Privacy redaction
5. Disconnection badges and backpressure signals

## Dependency chain
```
Epics 0+2+4+8 --> #1 Chrome Sovereignty --> #2 Safe Mode
                                         --> #3 Freeze
                                         --> #4 Privacy Redaction
                                         --> #5 Badges/Backpressure
```

## v1-mandatory requirements covered
- Chrome Layer Always On Top (spec line 10)
- Agent Exclusion From Chrome (spec line 23)
- Chrome Rendering Independence (spec line 36)
- ChromeState Synchronization (spec line 45)
- Tab Bar Rendering (spec line 54)
- Tab Keyboard Shortcuts (spec line 67)
- Dismiss Tile Override (spec line 76)
- Safe Mode Entry Protocol (spec line 89)
- Safe Mode Overlay (spec line 102)
- Safe Mode Exit (spec line 115)
- Safe Mode and Freeze Interaction (Shell State Invariant) (spec line 124)
- Freeze Scene (spec line 141)
- Freeze Backpressure Signal (spec line 158)
- Disconnection Badge (spec line 171)
- Redaction Placeholder (spec line 184)
- Budget Warning Badge (spec line 197)
- Override Control Guarantees (spec line 206)
- Viewer Class Indicator (spec line 219)
- Agent Isolation for Viewer State (spec line 232)
- Shell Audit Events (spec line 245)
- Audit Privacy Constraint (spec line 258)
- System Status Indicator (spec line 267)
- Capture-Safe Redaction Architecture (spec line 294)
- V1 Diagnostic Surface (spec line 285)

## v1-reserved exclusions
- Mute Control Surface (spec line 276) — rendered as disabled/greyed, accepts input but noops

## post-v1 exclusions
- Full TypeScript Inspector (spec line 303) — v1 diagnostic access is CLI-only
- Render-skip redaction (capture_surface_active always false in v1)
- Operator diagnostics overlay (GUI)

## Crate location
crates/tze_hud_runtime/src/shell/ (shell state machine module)

## Doctrine guardrails
- LLMs never sit in the frame loop — shell is runtime-owned chrome, not agent-driven
- Human override always wins — dismiss, revoke, safe mode, freeze are local, instant, cannot be intercepted
- Screen is sovereign — the runtime owns pixels, timing, composition, permissions, arbitration
- Presence requires governance — leases with TTL, capability scopes, and revocation semantics
title: Epic 11: System Shell — chrome sovereignty, safe mode, freeze, privacy redaction, badges

close_reason: RFC 0010 Scene Events & Interruptions written: three-category taxonomy, SceneEvent envelope, interruption classification, quiet hours, agent emission, subscription model, event bus architecture, tab_switch_on_event contract, quantitative requirements, audit event relationship
description:
## Problem

Multiple RFCs reference a scene-event model that does not yet have its own contract:
- **RFC 0006** §2.4/§5.4/§6.3: tab_switch_on_event with examples like 'doorbell.ring' — implies a full scene-event taxonomy but defines none
- **RFC 0004** §2-4: routes input events through focus/capture model — but input events and scene events are distinct categories
- **RFC 0007** §4-5: handles interruption classes for override controls — but interruption classification is ad-hoc
- **Doctrine (attention.md)**: defines quiet hours, interruption priority classes, and attention governance — none formalized in any RFC

The ad-hoc event references create ambiguity: is 'doorbell.ring' an input event, a scene event, or a system event? How does quiet-hours filtering interact with the event bus? What subscription model governs event delivery to agents?

## Proposed RFC 0010 Structure

1. **Event Taxonomy** — Three categories with clear boundaries:
   - Input events (pointer, keyboard, touch — owned by RFC 0004)
   - Scene events (zone publish, tile state change, tab switch, agent-emitted semantic events like doorbell.ring)
   - System events (safe mode, freeze, viewer context change, degradation — runtime-only, not agent-visible)
2. **Event Bus Architecture** — How events flow: emission → classification → filtering → delivery. Whether events are broadcast, topic-subscribed, or targeted.
3. **Interruption Classification** — Per attention.md: critical (always delivered), high (delivered unless quiet hours), normal (filtered by attention budget), low (batched/deferred). Each scene event has an interruption class.
4. **Quiet Hours Enforcement** — How quiet-hours configuration (RFC 0006) interacts with event delivery. Which events are suppressed, deferred, or always delivered.
5. **Agent Event Emission** — How agents emit scene events (e.g., doorbell.ring). Capability requirements, rate limiting, naming conventions.
6. **Event Subscription Model** — How agents subscribe to scene events. Relationship to RFC 0005 SubscriptionChange.
7. **Wire Protocol** — Protobuf messages for scene events. Distinct from input events (RFC 0004) and session messages (RFC 0005).

## Doctrine References

- attention.md §Interruption classes: priority classification, quiet hours
- presence.md §Zone contention: zone publish events
- architecture.md §Message classes: four traffic types with different delivery semantics

## Related Beads

- rig-f52: extract scene-event taxonomy from tab_switch_on_event (RFC 0006)
- rig-9v1: freeze advisory model — freeze is a system event (RFC 0007)
- rig-bqj: shell-state audit events — audit events are system events (RFC 0007)

## Acceptance Criteria

1. Clear taxonomy distinguishing input, scene, and system events
2. Interruption classification per attention.md with quiet-hours rules
3. Agent event emission contract with capability requirements
4. Event subscription model compatible with RFC 0005 session stream
5. tab_switch_on_event (RFC 0006) expressible within the taxonomy
6. Protobuf schema for scene event messages
title: Write Scene Events & Interruptions RFC (new RFC 0010)

description:
Epic: prerequisite test layer that must exist before implementation workers build v1 subsystems against openspec/changes/v1-mvp-standards.

The v1-mvp-standards spec set contains 378 normative requirements across 12 subsystem specs, with 623 WHEN/THEN scenarios mapping to test cases. Doctrine (heart-and-soul/validation.md) treats validation as architecture. Existing infrastructure includes TestSceneRegistry (5 scenes), assert_layer0_invariants (15 checks), HeadlessRuntime with pixel readback, LatencyBucket with assert_p99_under, Clock trait with TestClock, and proptest in dev-dependencies.

This epic produces 5 independently-assignable work items forming a partial dependency DAG:
  #1 Scene Registry Expansion (no deps) — 5→25 named scenes
  #2 Trait Contract Test Harnesses (no deps) — 5 spec-encoding traits with test suites
  #3 Layer 0 Assertion Expansion (depends on #1) — comprehensive scene graph/lease/zone invariants
  #4 Protocol Conformance Suite (depends on #2) — wire protocol + session state machine tests
  #5 Layer 1 Pixel Readback Definitions (depends on #1) — expected pixel assertions for all 25 scenes

All beads produce TEST INFRASTRUCTURE ONLY — no implementation code. Implementation beads come later and must make these tests pass.
title: Test-driven validation infrastructure for v1-mvp-standards

description:
# Event Batching and Coalescing

Implement per-frame event batching and backpressure coalescing per input-model/spec.md.

## Spec Requirements (with line numbers from spec.md)
- **Requirement: Event Serialization and Batching** (lines 330-332, spec.md) — Events serialized as protobuf InputEnvelope wrapped in EventBatch. Multiple input events for same agent within single frame batched into single EventBatch delivered as single SessionMessage (field 34). Within batch, events ordered by timestamp_mono_us ascending.
- **Requirement: Event Coalescing Under Backpressure** (lines 341-343, spec.md) — PointerMoveEvent coalesced under backpressure (latest position retained). Hover state changes coalesced to net state. ScrollOffsetChangedEvent coalesced per tile to latest offset. Transactional events (down, up, click, key, focus, capture, IME) NEVER dropped or coalesced. Event queue default depth: 256 events per agent, hard cap: 4096.
- **Requirement: Protobuf Schema for Input Events** (lines 367-369, spec.md) — InputEnvelope (22-variant oneof covering all pointer, keyboard, focus, capture, gesture, IME, scroll, command events), EventBatch (frame_number, batch_ts_us, repeated InputEnvelope). All tile_id/node_id as SceneId. All timestamps use _mono_us suffix.
- **Requirement: Event Dispatch to Agent Latency** (lines 356-358, spec.md) — event dispatch (hit-test + session lookup + serialization + enqueue) < 2ms from Stage 2 completion.

## WHEN/THEN Acceptance Scenarios
- WHEN two pointer events for same agent occur within same frame THEN delivered in single EventBatch with events ordered by timestamp_mono_us (line 336)
- WHEN agent's event queue is full and 10 PointerMoveEvents arrive for same node THEN only final position retained and delivered to agent (line 347)
- WHEN agent's event queue is at hard cap and PointerDownEvent arrives THEN PointerDownEvent enqueued; non-transactional events dropped to make room if necessary (line 351)
- WHEN Stage 2 completes and produces InputEvent for Agent A THEN event serialized and enqueued to Agent A's EventBatch within 2ms (line 362)

## Latency budgets
- Event serialization + enqueue: < 2ms from Stage 2 completion (p99)
- Event queue depth: 256 default, 4096 hard cap per agent
- Transactional events: zero-loss guarantee under backpressure

## Test scenes
- chatty_dashboard_touch — high-rate pointer move coalescing under load
- input_highlight — batching correctness

## Crate/file location
crates/tze_hud_input/src/ (batching.rs, coalescing.rs, event_queue.rs)

## Depends on
- Sub-bead #2 (pointer event dispatch — produces pointer events to batch)
- Sub-bead #3 (keyboard/command input — produces keyboard/command events to batch)
- Epic 3 (timing model — timestamp_mono_us ordering)

## v1-mandatory
Event Serialization and Batching, Event Coalescing Under Backpressure, Protobuf Schema for Input Events, Event Dispatch to Agent Latency

## v1-reserved exclusions
- Session-protocol EventBatch Variant Filtering (referenced in prompt but lives in session-protocol spec, co-dependent)

## post-v1 exclusions
- None specific to this bead

## Does NOT include
- Focus tree (bead #1)
- Pointer event dispatch pipeline (bead #2 — dispatch produces events; this bead batches them)
- Keyboard events / command input (bead #3 — produces events; this bead batches them)
- Pointer capture protocol (bead #5)
- Local feedback rendering (bead #6)
title: Input: Event batching and coalescing

close_reason: PR #143 merged after PR review — resource budget enforcement per lease (three-tier ladder, per-agent budgets) merged to main
description:
Implement per-agent resource governance per lease-governance/spec.md Requirement: Resource Budget Schema (lines 156-167), Requirement: Budget Soft Warning at 80% (lines 169-176), Requirement: Budget Hard Limit at 100% (lines 178-185), Requirement: Three-Tier Budget Enforcement Ladder (lines 187-203), and Requirement: Budget Enforcement Latency (lines 204-211).

## Resource Budget Schema (spec lines 156-167, Requirement: Resource Budget Schema)
Every lease carries a ResourceBudget with dimensions:
- max_nodes_per_tile (range [1, 64])
- update_rate_hz (mutations/second)
- max_tiles (range [1, 64])
- texture_bytes_total
- max_active_leases (range [1, 64])
- max_concurrent_streams (0 in v1)
Default values match platform profile defaults.

## Budget Soft Warning at 80% (spec lines 169-176, Requirement: Budget Soft Warning at 80%)
When any budget dimension reaches 80%:
- Runtime sends BudgetWarning event to agent via LeaseStateChange
- Budget warning badge rendered on affected tiles
- Mutation accepted (soft limits do not reject work)

## Budget Hard Limit at 100% (spec lines 178-185, Requirement: Budget Hard Limit at 100%)
When a mutation would push any budget dimension to or beyond 100%:
- Mutation rejected with BUDGET_EXCEEDED_* error code
- Entire MutationBatch fails atomically

## Three-Tier Budget Enforcement Ladder (spec lines 187-203, Requirement: Three-Tier Budget Enforcement Ladder)
Tier 1 — Warning at >= 80%: send BudgetWarning, render badge
Tier 2 — Throttle after warning unresolved for 5 seconds: reduce effective update_rate_hz by 50%
Tier 3 — Revocation after throttle sustained for 30 seconds or critical limit exceeded: revoke all leases, terminate session

Critical triggers that bypass the ladder:
- CriticalTextureOomAttempt
- RepeatedInvariantViolations (> 10 in session lifetime)
- Protocol violations indicating malicious intent

## Budget Enforcement Latency (spec lines 204-211, Requirement: Budget Enforcement Latency)
Per-mutation budget check overhead MUST be < 50us. Budget checks are all-or-nothing within a batch (atomic pipeline).

## Shared Resources and Anti-Collusion
Shared resources double-counted per agent (anti-collusion).

## Guest MCP Zone Publishing Is Lease-Free
Guest MCP zone publishing does NOT require a lease — guests publish to zones without lease acquisition per session-protocol/spec.md Requirement: MCP Bridge Guest Tools. Budget enforcement for guest publishing follows separate session-level limits.

## Post-Revocation Resource Cleanup (cross-reference with bead #3)
On budget-driven revocation: all session leases to REVOKED, LeaseResponse with revoke_reason = BUDGET_POLICY, tiles marked for removal, grace period bypassed, resources freed after 100ms delay, post-revocation resource footprint = zero. (spec lines 253-260, Requirement: Post-Revocation Resource Cleanup)

## WHEN/THEN Scenarios from Spec

Scenario: Budget dimensions enforced (spec lines 161-163)
- WHEN a lease is granted with max_tiles = 8 and max_nodes_per_tile = 32
- THEN the agent can create up to 8 tiles, each with up to 32 nodes

Scenario: max_concurrent_streams zero in v1 (spec lines 165-167)
- WHEN a lease is granted in v1
- THEN max_concurrent_streams is 0 (media streams deferred to post-v1)

Scenario: Texture budget at 80% (spec lines 174-176)
- WHEN an agent's texture usage reaches 80% of texture_bytes_total
- THEN a BudgetWarning event is sent, an amber border appears on tiles, and the mutation is accepted

Scenario: Tile count at 100% (spec lines 183-185)
- WHEN an agent at max_tiles = 8 attempts to create a 9th tile
- THEN the entire MutationBatch is rejected with BUDGET_EXCEEDED_TILE_COUNT

Scenario: Throttle after 5 seconds (spec lines 192-194)
- WHEN a budget warning persists for 5 seconds without resolution
- THEN the agent's effective update_rate_hz is reduced by 50%

Scenario: Revocation after 30 seconds throttle (spec lines 196-198)
- WHEN throttle persists for 30 seconds
- THEN all agent leases are revoked and the session is terminated

Scenario: Critical bypass (spec lines 200-202)
- WHEN an agent attempts to allocate texture memory exceeding the hard maximum
- THEN all leases are immediately revoked without going through warning/throttle

Scenario: Budget check within latency budget (spec lines 209-211)
- WHEN a MutationBatch with 64 mutations is evaluated
- THEN each per-mutation budget check completes within 50us

## State Machine Diagram Reference
Budget enforcement interacts with the lease state machine at the revocation transition: ACTIVE->REVOKED (budget policy). See lease-governance/spec.md line 11. The three-tier ladder (Warning -> Throttle -> Revocation) is a temporal escalation that culminates in the REVOKED terminal state.

## Acceptance Criteria
- Three-tier ladder transitions verified with time injection (TestClock)
- Budget violations produce correct warnings/throttles/revocations
- Budget tests from budget.rs pass
- Guest zone publishing verified lease-free
- Per-mutation budget check < 50us
- MutationBatch fails atomically at 100% hard limit
- Shared resources double-counted per agent
- Critical triggers bypass ladder to immediate revocation
- max_concurrent_streams = 0 in v1
- LeaseStateMachine trait contract tests from Epic 0 (rig-ho4b) pass for budget scenarios

## Crate/File Location
crates/tze_hud_scene/src/lease/budget.rs (ResourceBudget struct, per-mutation budget checks, soft/hard limits), crates/tze_hud_scene/src/lease/enforcement.rs (three-tier ladder state machine, throttle timer, revocation trigger). Reference: existing partial budget logic in crates/tze_hud_scene/src/graph.rs and examples/vertical_slice/tests/budget_assertions.rs.

## Relevant Test Scenes
- three_agents_contention (validation-framework 25-scene registry — budget contention under multi-agent load)
- max_tiles_stress (existing test scene — stress near budget capacity)

## v1-mandatory Requirements Implemented
- Requirement: Resource Budget Schema (spec lines 156-167)
- Requirement: Budget Soft Warning at 80% (spec lines 169-176)
- Requirement: Budget Hard Limit at 100% (spec lines 178-185)
- Requirement: Three-Tier Budget Enforcement Ladder (spec lines 187-203)
- Requirement: Budget Enforcement Latency (spec lines 204-211)
- Cross-reference: Requirement: Post-Revocation Resource Cleanup (spec lines 253-260)

## v1-reserved exclusions
- Budget Renegotiation (spec lines 317-319): cannot change resource_budget after grant; must release and re-request

## post-v1 exclusions
- max_concurrent_streams (spec line 167): media streams deferred to post-v1
- Multi-Lease Atomic Operations (spec lines 325-332)

## What This Does NOT Include
- Core state machine transitions (bead #1)
- TTL countdown, renewal, or safe mode suspend/resume (bead #2)
- Disconnect grace or orphan handling (bead #3)
- Priority sort semantics or capability vocabulary definition (bead #4)
- Compositor render pipeline or badge rendering (compositor epic responsibility)
title: Resource budget enforcement per lease — three-tier ladder, per-agent budgets

description:
## Problem

RFC 0002 §2.1 defines a fixed thread model: main, compositor, network (Tokio), and telemetry. The thread model explicitly states 'no dynamic thread spawning during normal operation.' However, post-v1 will add GStreamer media pipelines and WebRTC, both of which require dedicated worker threads (GStreamer's internal thread pool, WebRTC ICE/DTLS threads). The current RFC makes no mention of where these threads will live or how they interact with the existing channel topology.

Without a reserved boundary, adding media workers later will require either:
- Breaking the 'fixed thread model' contract (confusing for implementors who built around it)
- Hacking media into the Tokio runtime (wrong — GStreamer has its own threading model)
- A major RFC revision that restructures the thread model

## Affected Sections

- RFC 0002 §2.1 Overview — 'fixed, small set of threads'
- RFC 0002 §2.6 Channel Topology — no media worker channels defined
- RFC 0002 §3.2 Stage 6 — 'Media tiles (deferred to post-v1) will add video surface compositing here'
- RFC 0002 §6.2 — deferred doctrine axes (reduce concurrent streams, audio-first fallback)

## Doctrine Reference

- architecture.md §Media: GStreamer — 'media is not an add-on'
- architecture.md §Multiple video feeds are a compositor problem — decode, compositing, budgets per-stream
- architecture.md §Protocol planes §3 — WebRTC media plane

## Required Change

Add a 'Future: Media Worker Boundary' subsection to §2 (or §10 Open Questions) that:

1. **Names the boundary:** A 'media worker pool' managed by GStreamer's internal scheduler, separate from the Tokio runtime. The pool is not spawned in v1 but the channel interfaces are designed to accommodate it.

2. **Defines the channel interface:** Media workers will communicate with the compositor thread via a bounded channel carrying 'decoded frame ready' signals (GPU texture handle + presentation timestamp). The compositor thread pulls from this channel during Stage 3 (Mutation Intake) or a new sub-stage.

3. **Thread ownership:** GStreamer owns its internal threads. The compositor thread owns the GPU context. Decoded frames are uploaded to GPU textures by the compositor thread (not by media workers) to preserve GPU device ownership invariant.

4. **Does NOT implement anything:** This is explicitly a reservation — a comment block or subsection that says 'this boundary exists, these are the constraints, do not design around it.'

This is low cost now and prevents a painful retrofit later.
notes: Merged PR #86 - Media worker boundary design reserved in RFC 0002 §2.8
title: RFC 0002: reserve worker thread boundary for post-v1 media

description:
In PR #129 (bead rig-d857), two issues were deferred:
1. session_server.rs has a private SessionEventRateLimiter that duplicates AgentEventRateLimiter from tze_hud_runtime. Should reuse AgentEventRateLimiter directly.
2. session_server.rs hardcodes MAX_PAYLOAD=4096 and rate limit text '10/s' instead of using tze_hud_runtime::MAX_PAYLOAD_BYTES and tze_hud_runtime::DEFAULT_MAX_EVENTS_PER_SECOND.
3. handle_emit_scene_event has duplicated rejection-response boilerplate (each check builds its own ServerMessage); should be refactored to use a Result-based pattern.
All three are cleanup/style concerns; the code is correct as-is. Discovered from PR review of rig-d857.

description:
validation-framework/spec.md Requirement: Record/Replay Traces (lines 283-295, v1-mandatory).

The runtime SHALL support recording sequences of scene mutations, agent events, input events, zone publishes, and timing data as structured traces. These traces SHALL be replayable deterministically against the scene graph for debugging and regression testing. Fuzzing discoveries that produce minimal reproducers SHALL become permanent regression tests.

**What must be built:**
1. Trace capture API — record mutation batches, input events, zone publishes, timing data into structured trace format
2. Deterministic replay engine — replay trace against scene graph state, verify identical outcomes
3. Fuzzing-to-regression pipeline — convert minimal reproducers from fuzzer into permanent test cases

**Scenarios from spec:**
- WHEN a timing-sensitive bug is discovered THEN the trace capture API records all relevant mutations and events with timestamps
- WHEN a trace is replayed against the same scene state THEN the outcome is identical to the original
- WHEN fuzzing discovers a minimal reproducer THEN it becomes a permanent regression test

**v1-mandatory. Crate: crates/tze_hud_scene/ (trace format), crates/tze_hud_runtime/ (capture integration)**
notes:
Coordinator: worker blocked in this rig snapshot; issue targets crates/spec files not present in current repository checkout. Needs correct repository/worktree or synchronized source before re-dispatch.
Cleanup: unblocked — source crates now present in main after baseline commit
title: Implement record/replay trace harness for deterministic reproduction

description: RFC 0005 §7.1 prose still uses InputMessage terminology when describing variant filtering; this should be EventBatch to match the RFC's own §3.2 update note. The inconsistency is in the source RFC, not the spec, so it's a separate cleanup task. Discovered during rig-5pk refinement.
title: RFC 0005 §7.1 uses stale InputMessage terminology instead of EventBatch

description:
Implement Layer 2 validation per validation-framework/spec.md Requirement: Layer 2 - Visual Regression via SSIM (lines 54-70).

## Implementation scope
- Generate golden reference images for all 25 test scenes from the scene registry
- SSIM comparison with thresholds: 0.995 for layout tests, 0.99 for media composition tests
- Perceptual hash for fast pre-screening before full SSIM
- On failure, produce: per-region SSIM scores, diff heatmap image, structured JSON failure details
- CI integration: fail build on SSIM drop below threshold
- Golden references committed to repository, named by scene and backend

## Spec references
- validation-framework/spec.md — Requirement: Layer 2 - Visual Regression via SSIM (lines 54-70):
  - Scenario: Layout SSIM threshold (line 59-61): "SSIM score of 0.996 MUST pass and an SSIM score of 0.993 MUST fail"
  - Scenario: Structured failure output (line 63-65): "per-region SSIM scores, a diff heatmap image, and structured JSON failure details"
  - Scenario: Golden references in repository (line 67-69): "committed to the repository, named by scene and backend"
- validation-framework/spec.md — Requirement: Five Validation Layers (lines 5-8): Layer 2 catches visual regressions
- validation-framework/spec.md — Requirement: Test Scene Registry (lines 160-172): all 25 named scenes
- validation-framework/spec.md — Requirement: DR-V5 - Trivial Headless Invocation (lines 227-234): "cargo test --features headless" runs Layers 0-2
- validation-framework/spec.md — Requirement: DR-V6 - No Physical GPU Required for CI (lines 238-249): SSIM thresholds account for software/hardware differences
- validation-framework/spec.md — Requirement: Test Honesty and Stability (lines 368-379): no flaky tests, deterministic

## Cross-epic references
- Depends on Epic 2 (compositor): headless rendering produces images to compare
- Depends on Epic 0/scene registry: test scene definitions for all 25 scenes
- Layer 1 (headless render + pixel readback) must be operational for Layer 2 to generate rendered images

## Acceptance criteria
- Golden images generated for all 25 test scenes in the registry
- SSIM comparison operational: score of 0.996 passes for layout tests, 0.993 fails
- Intentional regression (layout shift, z-order error, missing content) detected and flagged
- Structured failure output: per-region SSIM, diff heatmap, JSON with metric/actual/expected
- Perceptual hash pre-screening reduces SSIM computation for clearly-passing scenes
- Golden references stored in repository as {scene_name}_{backend}.png
- cargo test --features headless includes Layer 2 tests
- Tests are deterministic: same scene + same hardware = same result, always

## Test scene registry
All 25 scenes are relevant — each must have a golden reference generated:
empty_scene, single_tile_solid, three_tiles_no_overlap, overlapping_tiles_zorder, overlay_transparency, tab_switch, lease_expiry, mobile_degraded, sync_group_media, input_highlight, coalesced_dashboard, max_tiles_stress, three_agents_contention, overlay_passthrough_regions, disconnect_reclaim_multiagent, privacy_redaction_mode, chatty_dashboard_touch, zone_publish_subtitle, zone_reject_wrong_type, zone_conflict_two_publishers, zone_orchestrate_then_publish, zone_geometry_adapts_profile, zone_disconnect_cleanup, policy_matrix_basic, policy_arbitration_collision

## Artifact expectations
- Golden reference images for all 25 scenes (per backend)
- On regression: diff heatmap images, per-region SSIM JSON, structured failure report
- SSIM comparison tooling usable by both CI and LLM development loop

## V1 success criteria mapping
- Thesis 5: The validation architecture works (Layer 2 is one of the 5 required layers)
- Thesis 7: Headless mode fully functional (Layer 2 runs on software GPU in CI)

## Crate/file location
- tze_hud/crates/validation/src/layer2_ssim.rs (SSIM comparison logic)
- tze_hud/tests/golden/ (golden reference images)
- tze_hud/tests/layer2/ (Layer 2 test harness)

## V1 scope tags
- v1-mandatory: SSIM comparison, golden references, structured failure output, CI integration
- v1-reserved: None
- post-v1: Video frame SSIM (requires media pipeline), mobile-specific goldens

## Scope boundary — what this bead does NOT include
- Does NOT implement Layer 1 headless rendering (that is a prerequisite from Epic 2)
- Does NOT implement the test scene registry itself (that is a shared infrastructure concern)
- Does NOT implement Layer 3 or Layer 4 (those are separate beads)
