description:
Configuration/spec.md line 325 says v1 config with an 'includes' field 'MUST be silently ignored or produce a warning' — implementer's choice between two behaviors.

For a definitive standards set, this should be one behavior. A v1 config parser that silently drops a field behaves differently from one that warns, and the difference matters for operator experience and debugging.

**Resolution:** Change to a hard error in v1. If someone puts 'includes' in their config, they clearly expect layered composition, which doesn't work yet. A clear error message ('includes is not supported in v1; use a single config file') is better than silent data loss or an easily-missed warning.

Update line 325 to: 'Any includes field present in a v1 config file MUST produce a startup error with a message indicating that layered composition is reserved for post-v1.'

Update scenario line 330-331 accordingly.
title: Make configuration includes behavior deterministic: hard error in v1

description:
Sub-bead #2 of Epic 3 (Timing Model). Implement TimingHints struct, presentation scheduling, expiration enforcement, timestamp validation, pending queue, staleness indicators, message class enum, clock drift detection/enforcement, freeze/safe-mode timing behavior, headless virtual clock, ClockSync RPC, and timing configuration.

Parent epic: rig-zyeo (Epic 3: Timing Model)
Depends on: sub-bead #1 (rig-voo8, clock domain types)

## Spec requirements (v1-mandatory)

### Requirement: Timing Fields on Payloads (spec lines 72-83)
All meaningful realtime payloads MUST carry timing fields: present_at_wall_us (or relative scheduling alternative), expires_at_wall_us, created_at_wall_us, sequence, priority, coalesce_key, sync_group. The oneof schedule fields MUST be mutually exclusive; setting more than one MUST be rejected with RELATIVE_SCHEDULE_CONFLICT.

WHEN/THEN scenarios:
- WHEN an agent submits a MutationBatch, THEN it MAY set present_at_wall_us, expires_at_wall_us, sequence, priority, coalesce_key, sync_group; compositor MUST interpret and enforce each (spec lines 77-79)
- WHEN an agent sets both present_at_wall_us and after_us in the same message, THEN compositor MUST reject with RELATIVE_SCHEDULE_CONFLICT (spec lines 81-83)

### Requirement: Timestamp Precedence (spec lines 85-92)
present_at_wall_us MUST follow precedence: node-level overrides tile-level, tile-level overrides batch-level.

WHEN/THEN scenarios:
- WHEN a TextMarkdownNode has present_at_wall_us = T1 and its parent tile has T2, THEN the node MUST use T1 (spec lines 90-92)

### Requirement: Frame Quantization (spec lines 94-105)
present_at_wall_us timestamp T is "in scope" for frame F iff T <= frame_F_vsync_wall_us. Strict no-earlier-than rule.

WHEN/THEN scenarios:
- WHEN a mutation has present_at_wall_us = V + 1ms (where V is vsync time of frame F), THEN it MUST NOT apply at frame F; MUST wait until frame F+1 (spec lines 99-101)
- WHEN a mutation has present_at_wall_us = T, THEN it MUST be applied no earlier than T and no later than T + one frame period (spec lines 103-105)

### Requirement: Arrival Time Is Not Presentation Time (spec lines 50-61)
Every payload with timing significance MUST carry explicit timestamp fields. Mutations with present_at_wall_us in the future MUST be held in pending queue. Mutations without present_at_wall_us (or = 0) SHALL be applied at earliest available frame.

WHEN/THEN scenarios:
- WHEN agent submits MutationBatch with present_at_wall_us = T+500ms, THEN compositor MUST store and NOT apply until vsync_wall_us >= T+500ms (spec lines 55-57)
- WHEN agent submits with present_at_wall_us = 0, THEN apply at earliest available frame (spec lines 59-61)

### Requirement: Timestamp Validation (spec lines 107-122)
Validation rules for all agent-supplied timestamps:
- present_at_wall_us > 60s in past -> TIMESTAMP_TOO_OLD
- present_at_wall_us > max_future_schedule_us (default 300,000,000us = 5min) in future -> TIMESTAMP_TOO_FUTURE
- expires_at_wall_us <= present_at_wall_us -> TIMESTAMP_EXPIRY_BEFORE_PRESENT
- DROP_IF_LATE with non-EPHEMERAL_REALTIME class -> INVALID_DELIVERY_POLICY
- clock skew > 1s -> CLOCK_SKEW_EXCESSIVE

WHEN/THEN scenarios:
- WHEN present_at_wall_us > 60s before session_open_wall_us, THEN reject with TIMESTAMP_TOO_OLD (spec lines 112-114)
- WHEN present_at_wall_us > 5min in future, THEN reject with TIMESTAMP_TOO_FUTURE (spec lines 116-118)
- WHEN expires_at_wall_us <= present_at_wall_us, THEN reject with TIMESTAMP_EXPIRY_BEFORE_PRESENT (spec lines 120-122)

### Requirement: Presentation Deadline (spec lines 236-251)
Mutations held in per-agent pending queue (sorted by present_at_wall_us ascending, max depth 256 per agent). Late arrivals deferred to next frame. EphemeralRealtime with DROP_IF_LATE MAY be dropped.

WHEN/THEN scenarios:
- WHEN frame's vsync_wall_us >= mutation's present_at_wall_us, THEN extract from pending queue to Stage 4 (spec lines 241-243)
- WHEN pending queue has 256 entries, THEN reject new with PENDING_QUEUE_FULL (spec lines 245-247)
- WHEN EPHEMERAL_REALTIME with DROP_IF_LATE arrives after Stage 3 closes, THEN discard (spec lines 249-251)

### Requirement: Expiration Policy (spec lines 253-268)
Tile with expires_at_wall_us = T auto-removed at first frame where vsync_wall_us >= T. Min-heap O(expired_items). Non-negotiable under load (even degradation Level 4/5). Expiring tile removed from sync group before deletion.

WHEN/THEN scenarios:
- WHEN tile's expires_at_wall_us reached, THEN remove from scene at Stage 4, emit TileExpired event (spec lines 258-260)
- WHEN compositor at degradation Level 5, THEN expiry still runs (spec lines 262-264)
- WHEN 1000 tiles exist but only 2 expired, THEN evaluation touches only 2 (spec lines 266-268)

### Requirement: Session Close Pending Queue Flush (spec lines 291-298)
All entries in closed session's pending queue MUST be discarded. Reconnecting agent starts with empty queue.

WHEN/THEN scenarios:
- WHEN session closes with 10 pending mutations, THEN all 10 discarded, MUST NOT apply after reconnect (spec lines 296-298)

### Requirement: Staleness Indicators (spec lines 300-311)
Two staleness conditions: content staleness (no mutation within tile_stale_threshold_ms default 5000ms) and sync group staleness (arrival spread exceeds sync_drift_budget_us). Chrome-layer visual badge. Cleared on new valid mutation or agent disconnect.

WHEN/THEN scenarios:
- WHEN STATE_STREAM tile receives no mutation for 5s, THEN activate staleness badge (spec lines 305-307)
- WHEN stale tile receives new valid mutation, THEN clear staleness immediately (spec lines 309-311)

### Requirement: Message Class Typed Enum (spec lines 369-380)
Four message classes as typed MessageClass enum: Transactional, StateStream, EphemeralRealtime, ClockedMediaCue. Each has distinct delivery semantics.

WHEN/THEN scenarios:
- WHEN agent submits EPHEMERAL_REALTIME with DROP_IF_LATE, THEN accept (spec lines 374-376)
- WHEN agent submits DROP_IF_LATE with TRANSACTIONAL, THEN reject with INVALID_DELIVERY_POLICY (spec lines 378-380)

### Requirement: Vsync Sync Point (spec lines 32-39)
At each frame start, record (frame_number, vsync_mono_us, vsync_wall_us). Include in TelemetryRecord.

WHEN/THEN scenarios:
- WHEN new frame begins at vsync, THEN record sync point triple in FrameTimingRecord (spec lines 37-39)

### Requirement: Session Clock Sync Point (spec lines 41-48)
During gRPC handshake, record session_open_mono_us and session_open_wall_us. Difference is initial clock-skew estimate.

WHEN/THEN scenarios:
- WHEN agent establishes gRPC session, THEN record both timestamps and compute initial clock-skew estimate (spec lines 46-48)

### Requirement: Clock Drift Detection and Correction (spec lines 210-221)
Sliding window of last 32 agent timestamps for clock-skew estimation (median). Drift within tolerance (<=100ms): apply signed offset correction transparently. Clock jump (consecutive samples differ by > clock_jump_detection_ms default 50ms): reset window.

WHEN/THEN scenarios:
- WHEN agent clock is 50ms ahead, THEN apply -50ms correction transparently (spec lines 215-217)
- WHEN consecutive skew samples differ by > 50ms, THEN reset estimation window (spec lines 219-221)

### Requirement: Clock Drift Enforcement (spec lines 223-234)
Drift > 100ms: CLOCK_SKEW_HIGH warning, continue with correction. Drift > 1s: reject with CLOCK_SKEW_EXCESSIVE. Three consecutive ClockSync failures: terminate session.

WHEN/THEN scenarios:
- WHEN drift is 200ms, THEN emit CLOCK_SKEW_HIGH warning, continue with correction (spec lines 228-230)
- WHEN drift exceeds 1s, THEN reject new mutations with CLOCK_SKEW_EXCESSIVE (spec lines 232-234)

### Requirement: ClockSync RPC (spec lines 382-389)
Unary RPC for agents to align clocks. Request: agent_timestamp_wall_us. Response: compositor_mono_us, compositor_wall_us, estimated_skew_us, skew_within_tolerance, warning.

WHEN/THEN scenarios:
- WHEN agent calls ClockSync after session establishment, THEN compositor returns current timestamps and estimated skew (spec lines 387-389)

### Requirement: Freeze Override Timing Behavior (spec lines 326-341)
During freeze: pending queue drain suspended, expiry heap suspended, sync group deferred_frames_count not incremented, staleness timer suspended, clock-skew estimation continues. On unfreeze: apply past-due mutations, expire past-due tiles, resume staleness from pre-freeze, drop past-due DROP_IF_LATE ephemeral.

WHEN/THEN scenarios:
- WHEN scene frozen and tile's expires_at_wall_us passes, THEN NOT expired during freeze; expired in first post-unfreeze Stage 4 (spec lines 331-333)
- WHEN tile idle 4800ms and 2s freeze occurs, THEN not stale until 200ms after unfreeze (spec lines 335-337)
- WHEN scene unfreezes and mutations have present_at_wall_us in past, THEN all applied in first post-unfreeze Stage 3/4 (spec lines 339-341)

### Requirement: Safe Mode Timing Behavior (spec lines 343-358)
During safe mode: pending queue drain runs normally, expiry runs normally, staleness suppressed for suspended sessions, clock-skew estimation frozen. On exit: estimation window reset, staleness timers reset to 0.

WHEN/THEN scenarios:
- WHEN safe mode active and tile's expires_at_wall_us reached, THEN tile expired on schedule (spec lines 349-351)
- WHEN safe mode active and suspended session's tile has no mutations, THEN staleness NOT shown (spec lines 353-355)
- WHEN safe mode exits, THEN clock-skew estimation window reset to empty (spec lines 357-358)

### Requirement: Headless Virtual Clock (spec lines 360-367)
In headless mode, configurable frame rate (default 60fps) driven by synthetic clock. Same pipeline and timing semantics.

WHEN/THEN scenarios:
- WHEN compositor runs headless, THEN all timing behavior identical to windowed mode (spec lines 365-367)

### Requirement: Timing Configuration (spec lines 391-402)
All timing parameters configurable via TimingConfig with defaults and validation ranges: target_fps (60, 1-240), max_agent_clock_drift_ms (100, 1-10000), max_vsync_jitter_ms (2, 0-100), max_future_schedule_us (300000000, 1000000-3600000000), sync_group_max_defer_frames (3, 1-60), pending_queue_depth_per_agent (256, 16-4096), sync_drift_budget_us (500, 1-100000), tile_stale_threshold_ms (5000, 500-300000), clock_jump_detection_ms (50, 10-10000).

WHEN/THEN scenarios:
- WHEN config sets target_fps = 0, THEN runtime MUST reject (outside range 1-240) (spec lines 396-398)
- WHEN no [timing] section in config, THEN use all documented defaults (spec lines 400-402)

## Crate/file location
- crates/tze_hud_scene/src/timing/ (new module: hints.rs, scheduling.rs, pending_queue.rs, expiration.rs, staleness.rs, config.rs)
- crates/tze_hud_scene/src/clock.rs (extend for vsync sync point, clock drift)
- Proto definitions for TimingHints, MessageClass enum, ClockSync RPC

## Relevant test scenes
- lease_expiry — expiration behavior
- coalesced_dashboard — state-stream coalescing with coalesce_key
- chatty_dashboard_touch — ephemeral realtime with DROP_IF_LATE

## Cross-epic contracts
- Session-protocol (Epic 6) consumes ClockSync RPC, session clock sync point, session close queue flush
- Input-model (Epic 5) uses TimingHints for input event timestamps
- Scene types (Epic 1) carry TimingHints on MutationBatch and tile payloads
- Policy-arbitration (Epic 9) uses freeze/safe-mode timing behavior

## Acceptance criteria
- TimingHints struct with all spec fields, oneof schedule enforcement
- Presentation scheduling: future mutations held, immediate mutations applied
- Timestamp validation: TIMESTAMP_TOO_OLD, TIMESTAMP_TOO_FUTURE, TIMESTAMP_EXPIRY_BEFORE_PRESENT, CLOCK_SKEW_EXCESSIVE all produce structured errors
- Frame quantization: no-earlier-than guarantee verified with TestClock
- Pending queue: sorted, max depth 256, PENDING_QUEUE_FULL rejection
- Expiration: min-heap, O(expired_items), non-negotiable under degradation
- Staleness indicators: 5s threshold, cleared on mutation
- Clock drift: sliding window, median estimation, jump detection, enforcement tiers
- Freeze/safe-mode: correct suspension/resumption semantics
- Headless mode: synthetic clock drives same pipeline
- TimingConfig: all defaults, all validation ranges, rejection of out-of-range values
- All tested with SimulatedClock for deterministic behavior

## v1-mandatory
All requirements listed above.

## v1-reserved exclusions
- None

## post-v1 exclusions
- Media Clock Integration (spec lines 404-411)
- ClockedMediaCue active processing (spec lines 413-420) — enum value defined but not processed

## Scope boundary (what this bead does NOT include)
- Clock domain newtypes (sub-bead #1)
- Sync groups (sub-bead #3)
- Relative scheduling primitives (sub-bead #4)
- ClockedMediaCue active processing (post-v1)
title: TimingHints and presentation scheduling

description:
# Per-Event and Per-Frame Evaluation Pipelines

Implement event and frame evaluation pipelines per policy-arbitration/spec.md.

## Spec Requirements

### Requirement: Per-Frame Evaluation Pipeline (spec lines 194-205)
The runtime MUST evaluate the arbitration stack per-frame at the start of each frame cycle, before mutation intake.
- Per-frame evaluation order: Level 1 (Safety) -> Level 2 (Privacy) -> Level 5 (Resource) -> Level 6 (Content)
- If Level 1 triggers safe mode, Levels 2/5/6 MUST NOT be evaluated for that frame
- Total per-frame evaluation MUST complete in < 200us

### Requirement: Per-Event Evaluation Pipeline (spec lines 207-214)
The runtime MUST evaluate the arbitration stack per-event during input drain (Stage 1) and local feedback (Stage 2).
- Per-event evaluation order: Level 0 (Human Override) -> Level 4 (Attention) -> Level 3 (Security)
- If Level 0 triggers safe mode, Levels 4 and 3 MUST stop

### Requirement: Level 0 Human Override (spec lines 48-59)
- Human override actions (dismiss, safe mode, freeze, mute) MUST be local, instant, MUST NOT be intercepted/delayed/vetoed
- Override commands placed in dedicated OverrideCommandQueue (bounded, capacity 16, SPSC)
- Read by compositor thread before any MutationBatch intake
- Override MUST complete within one frame (16.6ms)

### Requirement: Level 1 Safety Response (spec lines 61-72)
- Monitor GPU device health and scene graph integrity
- On wgpu::DeviceError::Lost: enter safe mode (Tier 2)
- On scene graph corruption: enter safe mode
- Frame-time guardian emergency threshold (frame_time_p95 > 14ms sustained): trigger Level 5 emergency degradation
- Multiple simultaneous triggers: most severe response wins (catastrophic exit > safe mode > GPU reconfiguration)

### Requirement: GPU Failure Three-Tier Response (spec lines 74-89)
- Tier 1 (recoverable GPU): attempt surface.configure(), continue on success
- Tier 2 (non-recoverable GPU, CPU intact): enter safe mode, suspend leases, render chrome in software fallback, wait up to 2 seconds for overlay
- Tier 3 (catastrophic -- overlay cannot render): flush telemetry (200ms grace), graceful shutdown with non-zero exit

### Requirement: Policy Evaluation Latency Budgets (spec lines 294-312)
- Full per-frame evaluation < 200us
- Human override response < 1 frame (16.6ms)
- Privacy transition < 2 frames (33.2ms)

## WHEN/THEN Scenarios (from spec)

### Per-Frame
- WHEN a frame cycle begins THEN GPU health and scene integrity are checked first; if safe mode triggers, no further per-frame evaluation occurs (spec lines 199-201)
- WHEN a frame with typical tile count is evaluated THEN the full per-frame evaluation (Levels 1, 2, 5, 6) completes in < 200us (spec lines 203-205)
- WHEN the runtime operates under normal load THEN the full per-frame evaluation completes in < 200us (spec lines 299-301)

### Per-Event
- WHEN a Ctrl+Shift+Escape input and a tab-switch event arrive in the same frame THEN the override (safe mode) is processed first and the tab-switch event is discarded (spec lines 212-214)
- WHEN the viewer presses Ctrl+Shift+Escape while mutation intake is in progress THEN override command processed before any pending mutations in the current batch (spec lines 53-55)
- WHEN an agent has a valid lease with high priority and the viewer dismisses its tile THEN the tile is dismissed regardless of priority or capability (spec lines 57-59)

### Safety
- WHEN wgpu::DeviceError::Lost is detected THEN runtime enters safe mode (Tier 2) with SafeModeEntryReason=CRITICAL_ERROR (spec lines 67-68)
- WHEN GPU failure and scene corruption detected in same frame THEN most severe response applied (spec lines 71-72)
- WHEN SurfaceError::Lost occurs and surface.configure() succeeds THEN rendering continues transparently with no agent notification (spec lines 80-81)
- WHEN GPU device lost and reconfiguration fails THEN safe mode entered first (up to 2s for overlay) before shutdown (spec lines 83-85)
- WHEN safe mode overlay cannot render THEN telemetry flushed (200ms) and process exits with non-zero exit code (spec lines 87-89)

### Latency
- WHEN a human override command is issued THEN override response completes within 1 frame (16.6ms) (spec lines 307-309)
- WHEN viewer class changes require privacy re-evaluation THEN all privacy transitions complete within 2 frames (33.2ms) (spec lines 311-312)

## Test Scenes
- policy_matrix_basic: per-frame evaluation ordering verified
- policy_arbitration_collision: per-event + per-frame collision under multi-level conflict

## Acceptance Criteria
- Per-frame evaluation order verified: L1 -> L2 -> L5 -> L6
- Safe mode short-circuit verified: L1 triggers -> L2/L5/L6 skipped
- Per-event evaluation order verified: L0 -> L4 -> L3
- Per-event short-circuit verified: L0 safe mode -> L4/L3 stop
- Full-frame evaluation < 200us
- Human override < 1 frame (16.6ms)
- Privacy transition < 2 frames (33.2ms)
- OverrideCommandQueue SPSC with capacity 16
- Override commands read before MutationBatch intake
- GPU three-tier response implemented (Tier 1/2/3)
- Safety severity ordering: catastrophic exit > safe mode > GPU reconfiguration

## Purity Constraint
Policy evaluation MUST be a pure function over a typed PolicyContext — no side effects. The per-frame and per-event pipelines read device health, scene state, override queue, and viewer context as inputs. They produce ArbitrationOutcomes and safety signals. State transitions (entering safe mode, suspending leases) are signaled to the shell but not executed by the policy layer.

## Crate/File Location
crates/tze_hud_policy/src/frame.rs — per-frame evaluation pipeline
crates/tze_hud_policy/src/event.rs — per-event evaluation pipeline
crates/tze_hud_policy/src/safety.rs — Level 1 safety evaluation, GPU tier response logic
crates/tze_hud_policy/src/override_queue.rs — OverrideCommandQueue (SPSC, capacity 16)

## v1-mandatory requirements
Per-Frame Evaluation Pipeline, Per-Event Evaluation Pipeline, Level 0 Human Override, Level 1 Safety Response, GPU Failure Three-Tier Response, Policy Evaluation Latency Budgets (per-frame, override, privacy transition)

## v1-reserved exclusions
- Per-Agent Arbitration Diagnostics RPC (spec lines 376-383)

## post-v1 exclusions
- Dynamic Policy Rules (spec lines 367-374)
- Freeze/safe-mode state machine transitions (owned by system-shell)

## What this does NOT include
- Arbitration stack data structures (bead #1)
- Per-mutation evaluation pipeline (bead #2)
- Attention budget / quiet hours (bead #4)
- System-shell state machine for freeze/safe-mode (Epic 11)
- Compositor frame loop integration (compositor/runtime epic)
- GPU device management (renderer epic)
title: Per-event and per-frame evaluation pipelines

description:
Implement remaining config sections: privacy, zone registry, agent registration, dynamic agent policy, authentication secret indirection, and hot-reload per configuration/spec.md.

## Spec References
- Requirement: Privacy Configuration Defaults (spec.md lines 215-225, v1-mandatory)
- Requirement: Quiet Hours Configuration (spec.md lines 228-239, v1-mandatory)
- Requirement: Redaction Style Ownership (spec.md lines 254-261, v1-mandatory)
- Requirement: Zone Registry Configuration (spec.md lines 123-134, v1-mandatory)
- Requirement: Agent Registration with Per-Agent Budget Overrides (spec.md lines 136-147, v1-mandatory)
- Requirement: Dynamic Agent Policy (spec.md lines 302-309, v1-mandatory)
- Requirement: Authentication Secret Indirection (spec.md lines 311-322, v1-mandatory)
- Requirement: Configuration Reload (spec.md lines 263-274, v1-mandatory)
- Requirement: Viewer Identification Pipeline (spec.md lines 333-340, post-v1)

## WHEN/THEN Acceptance Scenarios

### Privacy
- WHEN default_classification = "top_secret" THEN startup fails with CONFIG_UNKNOWN_CLASSIFICATION (lines 221-222)
- WHEN default_viewer_class = "admin" THEN startup fails with CONFIG_UNKNOWN_VIEWER_CLASS (lines 225-226)
- WHEN [privacy.quiet_hours] enabled = true and pass_through_class = "HIGH" THEN during quiet hours: CRITICAL and HIGH pass through; NORMAL queued; LOW discarded; SILENT unaffected (lines 234-235)
- WHEN pass_through_class = "urgent" (doctrine name, not canonical enum) THEN startup fails with CONFIG_UNKNOWN_INTERRUPTION_CLASS with hint suggesting "HIGH" (lines 238-239)
- WHEN [privacy].redaction_style = "pattern" and [chrome] does not contain redaction_style THEN accepted, redaction uses privacy section value (lines 260-261)

### Zone Registry
- WHEN a tab defines subtitle = { policy = "bottom_strip", layer = "content" } without custom [zones.subtitle] THEN built-in subtitle zone type used (lines 129-130)
- WHEN a tab references zone type "news_ticker" not defined in [zones] and not built-in THEN startup fails with CONFIG_UNKNOWN_ZONE_TYPE (lines 133-134)

### Agent Registration
- WHEN pre-registered agent sets max_tiles = 4 and active profile has max_tiles = 1024 THEN accepted (lines 142-143)
- WHEN pre-registered agent sets max_tiles = 2048 and active profile has max_tiles = 1024 THEN startup fails with CONFIG_AGENT_BUDGET_EXCEEDS_PROFILE identifying agent, field, and ceiling (lines 146-147)

### Dynamic Agent Policy
- WHEN no [agents.dynamic_policy] section present THEN connections from unregistered agents rejected (lines 308-309)

### Authentication
- WHEN agent sets auth_psk_env = "AGENT_KEY" and env var AGENT_KEY is set THEN agent can authenticate using PSK from that variable (lines 317-318)
- WHEN agent sets auth_psk_env = "AGENT_KEY" and env var AGENT_KEY is not set THEN warning logged and agent cannot authenticate (lines 321-322)

### Hot-Reload
- WHEN runtime receives SIGHUP and updated config changes [privacy].redaction_style from "pattern" to "blank" THEN new redaction style takes effect without restart (lines 269-270)
- WHEN runtime receives SIGHUP and updated config has validation errors THEN errors returned and running config unchanged (lines 273-274)

## Implementation Location
crates/tze_hud_config/src/privacy.rs — privacy config, quiet hours, redaction style
crates/tze_hud_config/src/zones.rs — zone registry, built-in zone types
crates/tze_hud_config/src/agents.rs — agent registration, budget validation, dynamic policy, auth indirection
crates/tze_hud_config/src/reload.rs — SIGHUP handler, hot-reload field classification

## Immutability Contract
- Frozen at startup (require restart): [runtime], [[tabs]], [agents.registered], profile, capability grants
- Hot-reloadable: [privacy], [degradation], [chrome], [agents.dynamic_policy]
- On reload: entire config re-validated; validation errors returned without applying new config

## v1-mandatory requirements
Privacy Configuration Defaults, Quiet Hours Configuration, Redaction Style Ownership, Zone Registry Configuration, Agent Registration with Per-Agent Budget Overrides, Dynamic Agent Policy, Authentication Secret Indirection, Configuration Reload

## v1-reserved exclusions
- Quiet hours pass_through_class uses canonical InterruptionClass enum names (CRITICAL, HIGH, NORMAL, LOW, SILENT) not RFC 0006 doctrine names (urgent, gentle)

## post-v1 exclusions
- Viewer Identification Pipeline (privacy.viewer_detectors) — v1 uses single viewer_id_method string only
- Face recognition detector chains
- Multi-viewer spatial detection

## Does NOT include
- TOML parsing/loading (bead #1)
- Display profile resolution (bead #2)
- Capability vocabulary definition (bead #3 — this bead validates agent grants AGAINST the vocabulary from bead #3)

## Built-in Zone Types
subtitle, notification, status_bar, pip, ambient_background, alert_banner

description:
Two related issues in session-protocol/spec.md:

**1. Heartbeat naming contradiction WITHIN the spec:**
- Lines 67, 224, 229-230: Use 'Heartbeat' as a single bidirectional message (Heartbeat.timestamp_mono_us, ClientMessage.heartbeat=25, ServerMessage.heartbeat=33). This matches the current session.proto code.
- Lines 265, 270-274: Use 'HeartbeatPing' and 'HeartbeatPong' as two separate messages with different fields (HeartbeatPing has client_timestamp_mono_us; HeartbeatPong has client_timestamp_mono_us + server_timestamp_wall_us).

Pick one. The single bidirectional Heartbeat message (matching current proto) is simpler and already works. If the spec wants HeartbeatPing/Pong, update the proto and all earlier references. If it wants single Heartbeat, update lines 265-275 to match lines 224/229-230.

**2. Messages used in requirements but missing from outline (line 58-69):**
- InputFocusRequest / InputFocusResponse (line 559, 564)
- InputCaptureRelease / CaptureReleasedEvent (lines 568-569)
- CapabilityRequest / CapabilityNotice (lines 588, 594-595)
- SessionSuspended / SessionResumed (line 543)
- RuntimeError (line 602)
- EventBatch (lines 250, 259, 472, 569)

Add these to the outline inventory so the message list is complete. Workers reading the outline to understand the protocol surface will miss these otherwise.
title: Fix session-protocol Heartbeat contradiction and complete message inventory

close_reason: Resolved by RFC 0009 (PR #40) — GPU failure path unified between RFC 0002/0007
description:
## Problem

RFC 0002 contains an internal contradiction about GPU failure handling, and a cross-RFC conflict with RFC 0007:

**RFC 0002 §1.4 (Graceful Shutdown):** 'Fatal GPU errors (device lost, out of memory) trigger an emergency path: flush telemetry, log structured error, exit immediately without waiting for agents.'

**RFC 0002 §7.3 (Surface Lifecycle):** GPU device lost → attempt surface reconfiguration → if fails, trigger graceful shutdown with non-zero exit code. This is more nuanced (try recovery first) but still ends in process exit on failure.

**RFC 0007 §5.1 (Safe Mode Entry):** 'Automatic entry on critical runtime error: If the compositor detects a condition that would otherwise produce a blank or unresponsive screen — scene graph corruption, GPU device loss, unrecoverable render failure — it enters safe mode rather than crashing.'

The contradiction: RFC 0002 says GPU device loss → exit. RFC 0007 says GPU device loss → safe mode. These cannot both be true.

## Affected Sections

- RFC 0002 §1.4 final paragraph — emergency exit path
- RFC 0002 §7.3 — GPU device lost handling
- RFC 0007 §5.1 — safe mode automatic entry triggers
- RFC 0007 §5.4 — safe mode independence from scene graph

## Doctrine Reference

- failure.md §Core principle — 'the runtime must always be usable, even when agents are not'
- failure.md §What the user always sees — screen is responsive, dismiss/safe mode works
- architecture.md §System shell — 'shell exists, it is local, it is always available'

## Required Change

Establish a tiered GPU failure response:

1. **Recoverable GPU error (SurfaceError::Lost, SurfaceError::Outdated):** Attempt surface reconfiguration per §7.3. If recovery succeeds, resume. If recovery fails, escalate to tier 2.

2. **Non-recoverable GPU error with intact CPU state:** Enter safe mode (RFC 0007 §5). Chrome rendering uses a minimal fallback path (solid color fills only, no GPU-intensive compositing). Sessions are suspended. The user sees the safe mode overlay and can choose to resume or exit. This honors doctrine: screen is always usable.

3. **Catastrophic GPU error (device creation impossible, driver crash, OOM during safe mode rendering):** Flush telemetry and exit with structured error. This is the only case where immediate exit is justified — when even safe mode cannot render.

Update RFC 0002 §1.4 to reference safe mode entry instead of immediate exit for GPU loss. Update §7.3 to define the escalation path. Add cross-reference to RFC 0007 §5.
title: RFC 0002: resolve safe-mode vs fatal-GPU-error conflict with RFC 0007

description: A broadcast::Sender<SceneEvent> exists in SharedState but nothing publishes onto it. RFC 0002 §2.4 specifies event fan-out from compositor to subscribed agent sessions. RFC 0004 §6.4 requires input events to be dispatched to the owning agent asynchronously. Implement: (1) scene mutation events published on event_tx after each apply_mutations call, (2) a streaming EventSubscribe RPC that forwards events to subscribed clients, (3) input event dispatch from InputResult to owning agent session via the event stream, (4) exercise subscription in the vertical slice (connect, subscribe, trigger input, receive event).
title: Wire gRPC event subscription stream and input dispatch to agents

description: The session-protocol spec has a widespread inconsistency: 9 locations (lines 12, 18, 22, 192-203, 223, 249, 558, 659, 670) still reference SessionMessage as the envelope type while the authoritative Proto File Layout (line 59) defines the wire API as rpc Session(stream ClientMessage) returns (stream ServerMessage). The SessionMessage Envelope requirement (line 192) describes field allocation for a single envelope type but should describe ClientMessage (fields 10-29, client-to-server) and ServerMessage (fields 30-46, server-to-client) separately. This inconsistency was pre-existing before PR #97 but that PR makes the gap more visible. Discovered during review of PR #97 (rig-x6g6).
title: Fix SessionMessage vs ClientMessage/ServerMessage inconsistency in session-protocol spec

close_reason: Resolved by PR #50 (rig-5vq.17 R3) and RFC 0009 §5 — GPU failure path unified as safe-mode-then-shutdown
description:
## Problem

RFC 0002 has an internal contradiction about GPU device lost handling within its own text (separate from the cross-RFC conflict with RFC 0007 §5.1, which is tracked by a sibling bead):

**§1.4 (Graceful Shutdown), final paragraph:** 'Fatal GPU errors (device lost, out of memory) trigger an emergency path: flush telemetry, log structured error, exit immediately without waiting for agents.'

**§7.3 (Surface Lifecycle), GPU device lost:** '1. Compositor thread detects SurfaceError::Lost or SurfaceError::Outdated. 2. Flush telemetry with error event. 3. Attempt surface reconfiguration. If successful, resume normally. 4. If reconfiguration fails (device truly lost): trigger graceful shutdown (§1.4) with non-zero exit code.'

§1.4 says device lost → exit immediately. §7.3 says device lost → try recovery first, only exit if recovery fails. These are contradictory within the same document.

## Affected Sections

- RFC 0002 §1.4 final paragraph
- RFC 0002 §7.3 GPU device lost subsection

## Required Change

Make §1.4's emergency path consistent with §7.3's tiered approach:

1. Remove the blanket 'exit immediately' for device lost from §1.4.
2. §1.4 should reference §7.3 for GPU failure handling: 'GPU errors are handled per §7.3. Only catastrophic, unrecoverable GPU failure (device creation impossible after reconfiguration attempt) triggers the emergency exit path.'
3. Keep OOM as a separate case: GPU OOM may be recoverable (free resources, retry) or unrecoverable (driver state corrupt). Define which OOM scenarios are fatal vs recoverable.
title: RFC 0002: internal contradiction in GPU device lost handling (§1.4 vs §7.3)

description:
policy-arbitration/spec.md lines 364-377 encode 'safe mode cancels freeze' as a precedence note inside the 7-level arbitration stack, explaining why Level 1 (Safety) overrides Level 0 (Human Override/Freeze). This has been flagged across four review rounds as architecturally unclean.

**Current encoding (policy-arbitration lines 364-369):**
The behavior is correct but framed as 'why Level 1 beats Level 0' with a lengthy explanatory note about automatic vs manual triggers. This makes the arbitration stack feel like it has exceptions.

**Proposed change:**
1. In system-shell/spec.md: add a shell state-transition rule that freeze is cancelled whenever safe mode activates, regardless of trigger. This is a shell-state invariant: safe_mode=true implies freeze_active=false. Shell owns override state.
2. In policy-arbitration/spec.md: remove the freeze/safe-mode interaction requirement and the explanatory note. Replace with a cross-reference: 'See system-shell spec for freeze/safe-mode state transitions.'
3. The 7-level stack remains fixed and exception-free. Policy evaluates; shell and lease-governance own state transitions.

**Why this matters:**
- Keeps policy evaluation as a pure function (no state-transition side effects)
- Shell already owns OverrideState (freeze_active, safe_mode_active) — this just makes it the single author
- Removes the only 'exception note' from the arbitration law
- Makes the codebase cleaner: one state machine owner per concern
title: Move freeze/safe-mode interaction from policy-arbitration exception to shell state semantics

description:
Three cross-spec contract bugs that must be reconciled before freeze:

**1. ResourceId encoding contradiction**
- scene-graph/spec.md line 24: 'BLAKE3 content hash (32 bytes, hex-encoded)' — implies 64 ASCII chars
- resource-store/spec.md line 6/16: 'stored as 32 bytes' / 'exactly 32 bytes (256-bit BLAKE3 digest)' — raw binary
- Resolution: Unify on raw 32-byte binary for wire/storage. Hex encoding is a display concern. Update scene-graph spec.

**2. Timestamp suffix self-contradiction**
- timing-model/spec.md lines 23-30 mandates '_wall_us' / '_mono_us' clock-domain suffixes
- Same spec then uses bare '_us' ~22 times: present_at_us, expires_at_us, created_at_us, session_open_at_us (undefined)
- session-protocol/spec.md does it correctly (present_at_wall_us, expires_at_wall_us, etc.)
- scene-graph/spec.md line 277 also uses bare 'timestamp_us'
- Resolution: Normalize all bare '_us' fields to their correct clock domain across all specs.

**3. Interruption taxonomy mismatch**
- scene-events/spec.md lines 50-66: CRITICAL / URGENT / NORMAL / GENTLE / SILENT
- policy-arbitration/spec.md lines 131-150: CRITICAL / HIGH / NORMAL / LOW / SILENT
- Same semantics, different names at levels 2 and 4
- Resolution: Pick one canonical enum. scene-events' names (URGENT/GENTLE) are more descriptive than HIGH/LOW. Update policy-arbitration to match.

All three are cross-spec contract bugs that would cause protobuf schema conflicts at implementation time.
title: Fix cross-spec vocabulary: ResourceId encoding, timestamp suffixes, interruption taxonomy

description:
## Problem

All timing in RFC 0003 is expressed as absolute UTC wall-clock timestamps (present_at_us, expires_at_us). For local low-latency agents running on the same machine as the compositor, absolute UTC timestamps are unnecessarily fragile:

1. A local agent that wants to schedule content 'in 500ms' must query the current time, add 500ms, and submit that as present_at_us. This introduces a race: the clock may advance between the query and the submission.

2. An agent that wants 'show this on the next frame' must know the compositor's frame rate and current frame position. Getting this wrong by a few microseconds can cause a one-frame delay.

3. The pattern 'show this N frames from now' has no direct expression. Agents must compute present_at_us = current_time + N * frame_budget_us, which requires knowing the frame rate and introduces floating-point accumulation errors over multiple such calls.

These patterns are extremely common for local agents producing real-time UI updates (hover effects, typing indicators, animation keyframes). Forcing them through absolute UTC timestamps adds unnecessary complexity and fragility.

## Affected Sections

- Section 3.2 (Timestamp and Ordering Fields) — add new timing fields
- Section 7.1 (timing.proto) — add proto fields
- Section 5.3 (Presentation Deadline) — define evaluation semantics for relative timestamps

## Doctrine Reference

architecture.md 'Time is a first-class API concept': payloads carry timing semantics. The doctrine lists present_at and expires_at but does not prohibit relative forms. Making the timing API easier for the most common case (local agents) aligns with the core directive of LLM interactivity.

## Concrete Change

Add three relative scheduling primitives to MutationBatch / TimestampedPayload as an alternative to present_at_us:

1. **after_us (uint64):** 'Apply this mutation N microseconds from compositor's current monotonic clock at intake.' The compositor converts to absolute present_at_us at Stage 3 intake. Mutually exclusive with present_at_us — setting both is a validation error.

2. **frames_from_now (uint32):** 'Apply this mutation N display frames from the current frame.' The compositor converts to a target frame number at Stage 3. 0 = this frame (same as present_at_us = 0). Mutually exclusive with present_at_us and after_us.

3. **next_frame (bool):** Sugar for frames_from_now = 1. 'Apply on the very next frame after intake.' Useful for agents that want to guarantee their mutation is not applied in the current frame (e.g., to avoid visual glitches when combined with other mutations in the same batch that should apply this frame).

These are convenience sugar. The compositor converts them to absolute timestamps or frame numbers at intake time. They never appear in the scene graph, telemetry, or stored state — only in the wire protocol.

## Proto addition

In TimestampedPayload or MutationBatch:
  oneof schedule {
    uint64 present_at_us = 2;
    uint64 after_us = 20;
    uint32 frames_from_now = 21;
  }
  // next_frame is frames_from_now = 1

## Rationale

Reduces clock-related fragility for the most common local-agent scheduling patterns. Aligns with the extension idea in the external review. Does not change internal timing model — only adds wire-protocol convenience.
title: RFC 0003: Add relative scheduling primitives (after_us, frames_from_now, next_frame)

description:
# Keyboard Events and Command Input

Implement keyboard and abstract command model per input-model/spec.md.

## Spec Requirements (with line numbers from spec.md)
- **Requirement: Keyboard Event Types** (lines 289-291, spec.md) — KeyDownEvent, KeyUpEvent, CharacterEvent for focused nodes. KeyDownEvent carries physical key_code (DOM KeyboardEvent.code), logical key value (DOM KeyboardEvent.key), modifiers, repeat flag, timestamp_mono_us. CharacterEvent carries post-IME committed Unicode characters. Keyboard events target focused node first, then bubble to tile if node does not consume them.
- **Requirement: Command Input Model** (lines 378-380, spec.md) — Seven abstract command actions: NAVIGATE_NEXT, NAVIGATE_PREV, ACTIVATE, CANCEL, CONTEXT, SCROLL_UP, SCROLL_DOWN. Delivered via CommandInputEvent in EventBatch pipeline. CommandInputEvent carries tile_id, node_id, interaction_id, timestamp_mono_us, device_id, action, source (KEYBOARD, DPAD, VOICE, REMOTE_CLICKER, ROTARY_DIAL, PROGRAMMATIC). CommandInputEvent is transactional (never coalesced or dropped).
- **Requirement: ACTIVATE Local Feedback** (lines 389-391, spec.md) — ACTIVATE triggers same local feedback as PointerDownEvent on focused HitRegionNode: pressed state via SceneLocalPatch in Stage 2. Latency budget: input_to_local_ack p99 < 4ms. Rollback path on agent rejection applies.
- **Requirement: Pointer-Free Navigation** (lines 411-413, spec.md) — All pointer interactions achievable without pointer using only command input: click (ACTIVATE), context menu (CONTEXT), scroll (SCROLL_UP/SCROLL_DOWN), tab close (focus + CANCEL), focus cycling (NAVIGATE_NEXT/NAVIGATE_PREV). Agents handling ACTIVATE and CANCEL work on all input profiles without modification.
- **Requirement: IME V1 Fallback** (lines 466-468, spec.md) — v1 ships without active IME composition support. Direct ASCII and basic Unicode keyboard input via KeyDownEvent and CharacterEvent is v1-mandatory. Full IME protocol is v1-reserved with stable schema in events.proto.

## WHEN/THEN Acceptance Scenarios
- WHEN node N1 in tile T1 has focus and user presses 'A' key THEN agent receives KeyDownEvent(tile_id=T1, node_id=N1, key_code="KeyA", key="a") and CharacterEvent(character="a") (line 295)
- WHEN focused node does not consume key event THEN key event bubbles to tile-level handler for owning agent (line 300)
- WHEN glasses temple D-pad down button pressed with focus on HitRegionNode THEN runtime dispatches CommandInputEvent(action=NAVIGATE_NEXT, source=DPAD) and executes focus cycling (line 384)
- WHEN CommandInputEvent(action=ACTIVATE) arrives for focused HitRegionNode THEN runtime sets pressed=true on node in same frame and dispatches CommandInputEvent to owning agent (line 395)
- WHEN display node has InputCapabilitySet(has_pointer=false, has_dpad=true) THEN every interactive HitRegionNode with accepts_focus=true reachable via NAVIGATE_NEXT/NAVIGATE_PREV, and ACTIVATE produces same agent-facing effect as click (line 417)
- WHEN user types 'a' on keyboard with no IME active and node has focus THEN agent receives CharacterEvent(character="a") (line 472)

## Latency budgets
- ACTIVATE local feedback: < 4ms p99 input_to_local_ack (same as pointer)
- CommandInputEvent is transactional: never coalesced or dropped under backpressure

## Test scenes
- input_highlight — keyboard focus and command input validation
- chatty_dashboard_touch — command input on pointer-free profile

## Crate/file location
crates/tze_hud_input/src/ (keyboard.rs, command.rs)

## Depends on
- Sub-bead #1 (focus tree — keyboard events target focused node, command input routes to focused session)

## v1-mandatory
Keyboard Event Types, Command Input Model, ACTIVATE Local Feedback, Pointer-Free Navigation, IME V1 Fallback (basic CharacterEvent only)

## v1-reserved exclusions
- Full IME Composition Protocol (ImeCompositionStarted, Updated, Committed, Cancelled) — schema defined in events.proto but implementation deferred
- IME composition window positioning (SetImePositionRequest)
- IME cancelled on focus loss integration

## post-v1 exclusions
- None specific to this bead (IME is v1-reserved not post-v1)

## Does NOT include
- Focus tree implementation (bead #1)
- Pointer event dispatch (bead #2)
- Event batching and coalescing (bead #4 — though CommandInputEvent is transactional, batching logic is there)
- Pointer capture protocol (bead #5)
- Local feedback rendering pipeline (bead #6 — though ACTIVATE triggers local feedback)
title: Input: Keyboard events and command input

description:
Implement the transactional mutation pipeline: atomic batches, five-stage validation, concurrency control, and structured error responses per scene-graph/spec.md.

## Spec References

### Requirement: Atomic Batch Mutations (scene-graph/spec.md line 142)
Mutations submitted as atomic batches (MutationBatch). batch_id is a SceneId. Max batch size: 1000 mutations. Entire batch validated and committed atomically — all-or-nothing. agent_namespace derived from authenticated session context, never from client-supplied field.
Source: RFC 0001 §3.1, §3.2 | Scope: v1-mandatory

### Requirement: Transaction Validation Pipeline (scene-graph/spec.md line 159)
Five ordered validation checks: (1) Lease check, (2) Budget check, (3) Bounds check, (4) Type check, (5) Invariant check (post-mutation simulation). Must verify no TileId/NodeId collision, no acyclic-tree violation, no exclusive z-order conflict, all internal references valid.
Source: RFC 0001 §3.2, §3.3 | Scope: v1-mandatory

### Requirement: Transaction Concurrency (scene-graph/spec.md line 176)
Single writer lock. One batch commits at a time. Same-agent batches commit in submission order. Mutations within a batch are contiguous (no interleaving). Monotonically increasing u64 sequence_number assigned to each commit.
Source: RFC 0001 §3.5 | Scope: v1-mandatory

### Requirement: Transaction Validation Performance (scene-graph/spec.md line 289)
Validation < 200us per batch of 10 mutations. Commit (lock-acquire through lock-release) < 50us for 10 mutations. Full path (parse, validate, commit) < 300us p99 for 10 mutations on reference hardware.
Source: RFC 0001 §3.2, §10 | Scope: v1-mandatory

### Requirement: Batch Rejection Response (scene-graph/spec.md line 315)
BatchRejected response must contain batch_id and structured error. Validation errors include: mutation_index (0-based), mutation_type, ValidationErrorCode, human-readable message, machine-readable context (JSON), optional correction_hint (JSON). Error codes stable across minor versions.
Source: RFC 0001 §3.4 | Scope: v1-mandatory

### Requirement: MutationBatch Processing (session-protocol/spec.md line 294)
MutationBatch carries: batch_id (SceneId UUIDv7), lease_id (SceneId), repeated MutationProto (ordered, atomic), optional TimingHints (present_at_wall_us, expires_at_wall_us). Runtime responds with MutationResult.
Source: RFC 0005 | Scope: v1-mandatory

## WHEN/THEN Scenarios from Spec

- Scenario: All-or-nothing batch rejection (line 147)
  WHEN an agent submits a batch of 5 mutations and mutation 3 has invalid bounds
  THEN the runtime MUST reject the entire batch (all 5 mutations) and report the error at mutation_index=2

- Scenario: Batch size exceeded (line 151)
  WHEN an agent submits a batch with 1001 mutations
  THEN the runtime MUST reject with BatchSizeExceeded { max: 1000, got: 1001 }

- Scenario: Agent namespace not trusted from client (line 155)
  WHEN a batch is received over gRPC
  THEN the runtime MUST derive the agent namespace from the authenticated session context, never from a client-supplied field

- Scenario: Lease check before budget check (line 164)
  WHEN an agent submits a mutation targeting a tile with an expired lease
  THEN the runtime MUST reject with LeaseExpired BEFORE evaluating budget or bounds checks

- Scenario: Post-mutation invariant simulation (line 168)
  WHEN a batch of mutations would introduce an acyclic-tree violation in a tile's node tree
  THEN the runtime MUST reject the batch with CycleDetected

- Scenario: Exclusive z-order conflict (line 172)
  WHEN two non-passthrough tiles on the same tab share z_order with overlapping bounds after batch
  THEN reject with ZOrderConflict

- Scenario: Sequential batch ordering (line 181)
  WHEN agent A submits batches B1 and B2 in order
  THEN B1 committed before B2, B1.sequence_number < B2.sequence_number

- Scenario: Validation latency (line 294)
  WHEN a batch of 10 mutations is submitted for validation
  THEN validation MUST complete in < 200us on reference hardware

- Scenario: Commit latency (line 298)
  WHEN a validated batch of 10 mutations is committed
  THEN commit (lock-acquire through lock-release) MUST complete in < 50us

- Scenario: Structured validation error (line 320)
  WHEN a mutation fails bounds validation
  THEN the rejection MUST include mutation_index, code=BoundsOutOfRange, human-readable message, and context JSON with field, value, and constraint

## Implementation Details

Mutation types per types.proto: CreateTile, SetTileRoot, PublishToZone, ClearZone (v1 set). The pipeline processes MutationBatch through the five-stage validation, applies atomically on success, or produces BatchRejected with full diagnostic information. Single writer lock (Mutex or similar) ensures concurrency safety. Sequence numbers are monotonically increasing u64 counters.

## Acceptance Criteria

- Partial-failure rollback test passes: batch with N mutations where mutation K fails results in zero applied mutations
- Batch atomicity property tests pass: proptest generates random mutation batches, verifies all-or-nothing
- All mutation WHEN/THEN scenarios from spec pass as unit tests
- Five-stage validation ordering verified: expired lease caught before budget check
- Structured error responses contain all required fields (mutation_index, code, message, context, correction_hint)
- Performance: validation < 200us, commit < 50us, full path < 300us p99 for 10-mutation batches (hardware-normalized per validation-framework/spec.md calibration harness)
- Sequence numbers strictly monotonically increasing across commits

## Epic 0 Test Gates

- Layer 0 partial-failure rollback test from assert_layer0_invariants()
- Batch atomicity proptest suite
- Test scenes most relevant: three_agents_contention (concurrent mutation batches), max_tiles_stress (budget validation), overlapping_tiles_zorder (z-order conflict detection)

## Crate/File Location

- Primary: crates/tze_hud_scene/src/mutation.rs (MutationBatch, mutation pipeline)
- Validation: crates/tze_hud_scene/src/validation.rs (five-stage validation pipeline)
- Graph: crates/tze_hud_scene/src/graph.rs (commit application, sequence numbering)
- Tests: crates/tze_hud_scene/tests/batch_atomicity.rs (integration tests)

## v1 Scope Tags

- v1-mandatory: Atomic Batch Mutations (line 142), Transaction Validation Pipeline (line 159), Transaction Concurrency (line 176), Transaction Validation Performance (line 289), Batch Rejection Response (line 315)
- v1-reserved: None for this bead
- post-v1: Batch pipelining / speculative execution, conflict resolution beyond reject-on-conflict

## What This Does NOT Include

- Scene hierarchy types (bead #2) — consumed, not defined here
- Zone publishing mutations (bead #4) — PublishToZone and ClearZone mutation types are validated here but zone resolution logic is in bead #4
- Hit-test updates after mutation (bead #5)
- Snapshot generation after commit (bead #6)
- gRPC transport layer (session protocol epic) — this bead operates on in-process MutationBatch structs
- Lease state machine implementation (lease governance epic) — lease validity is checked, not managed
title: Atomic batch mutations and transaction validation pipeline

description:
Implement interruption classification, zone ceiling enforcement, quiet hours enforcement, quiet hours queue semantics, attention budget enforcement, and earned urgency tracking per scene-events/spec.md.

## Spec Requirements

### Requirement: Interruption Classification (spec lines 50-66)
5 wire-level classes (lower numeric value = higher urgency):
- CRITICAL: overrides everything, bypasses quiet hours and budget. Only runtime can emit CRITICAL; agent-requested CRITICAL downgraded to HIGH.
- HIGH: may override quiet hours (subject to pass_through_class config), subject to attention budget. Maps to "Urgent" in privacy.md doctrine.
- NORMAL: standard, filtered by attention budget.
- LOW: batched/deferred, blocked during quiet hours, subtle indicators only, counted. Maps to "Gentle" in privacy.md doctrine.
- SILENT: never interrupts, always passes quiet hours.

Zone ceiling: runtime applies more restrictive of agent-declared class and zone's ceiling_interruption_class. E.g., agent declares HIGH on zone with ceiling NORMAL -> effective is NORMAL.

### Requirement: Quiet Hours Enforcement (spec lines 69-89)
During quiet hours, the interruption gate evaluates each event's class:
- CRITICAL: always delivered immediately, never queued (bypasses quiet hours and budget)
- HIGH: passes through when pass_through_class <= HIGH (default config); otherwise deferred to queue
- NORMAL: queued in per-zone FIFO, delivered when quiet hours end in original order
- LOW: discarded (not queued — too stale by quiet hours exit to be useful)
- SILENT: unaffected (always passes, never interrupts)

Quiet hours affect delivery, not generation. Events still created, logged, counted for telemetry.

### Requirement: Quiet Hours Queue Semantics (spec lines 92-104)
Per-zone FIFO queue. On quiet hours exit, dequeue and deliver in FIFO order. Queued zone publishes follow zone's contention policy (e.g., LatestWins delivers only the last). Configurable maximum depth per zone: default 100. Overflow drops oldest-first.

### Requirement: Attention Budget Enforcement (spec lines 137-153)
Per-agent budget: default 20 interruptions/minute (rolling count of non-silent interruptions).
Per-zone budget: default 10/minute (30 for Stack-policy zones).
CRITICAL exempt from budget. SILENT carries zero interruption cost.
At 80% of limit (e.g., 16 for per-agent): emit AttentionBudgetWarningEvent to agents subscribed to ATTENTION_EVENTS.
When exhausted: mutations proceed but visual presentation coalesced (latest-wins within coalesce buffer).
HIGH passes through quiet hours but remains subject to budget.

### Requirement: Earned Urgency Tracking (spec lines 317-324)
Track per-agent HIGH-class event rates. When rate exceeds configurable threshold (default: 4 HIGH per agent per minute), log a warning. "An agent that escalates everything is an agent that means nothing." Per-HIGH-class budget enforcement deferred to post-v1.

## WHEN/THEN Scenarios (from spec)

- WHEN an agent declares InterruptionClass HIGH on a zone whose ceiling_interruption_class is NORMAL THEN the effective interruption class MUST be NORMAL (the more restrictive of the two) (spec line 57)
- WHEN an agent requests CRITICAL interruption class on an emitted event THEN the runtime MUST downgrade the effective class to HIGH, since only the runtime can emit CRITICAL events (spec line 61)
- WHEN quiet hours are active and an event has InterruptionClass SILENT THEN the event MUST be delivered immediately (silent events are invisible by definition) (spec line 65)
- WHEN quiet hours are active and a CRITICAL event (e.g., safe mode) is generated THEN the event MUST be delivered immediately without queuing (spec line 76)
- WHEN quiet hours are active with default pass_through_class=HIGH, and a HIGH event (e.g., agent.doorbell_agent.doorbell.ring) is generated THEN the event MUST be delivered immediately (HIGH passes through when pass_through_class <= HIGH) (spec line 80)
- WHEN quiet hours are active and a NORMAL zone update event (e.g., scene.zone.occupancy_changed) occurs THEN the event's visual change MUST be queued and delivered when quiet hours end (spec line 84)
- WHEN quiet hours are active and a LOW-class event is generated THEN the event MUST be discarded (not queued); LOW events are too stale by quiet hours exit to be useful (spec line 88)
- WHEN quiet hours are active and a LatestWins zone receives 10 queued publishes THEN on quiet hours exit, only the last publish MUST be delivered (spec line 99)
- WHEN the quiet hours queue for a zone reaches 100 entries and a new event arrives THEN the oldest entry MUST be dropped to make room for the new event (spec line 103)
- WHEN an agent's rolling interruption count reaches 16 (80% of default 20 per minute) THEN the runtime MUST emit AttentionBudgetWarningEvent to agents subscribed to ATTENTION_EVENTS (spec line 144)
- WHEN an agent exceeds its per-agent interruption budget and submits another NORMAL mutation THEN the mutation MUST commit normally but its visual presentation MUST be coalesced (spec line 148)
- WHEN a CRITICAL-class event is generated while the budget is exhausted THEN the event MUST be delivered immediately without coalescing (spec line 152)
- WHEN an agent emits 5 HIGH-class events within one minute (exceeding default threshold of 4) THEN the runtime MUST log a warning about the agent's disproportionate HIGH-class usage (spec line 324)

## Acceptance Criteria

- 5 InterruptionClass variants with correct ordering (CRITICAL < HIGH < NORMAL < LOW < SILENT numerically)
- Agent CRITICAL downgraded to HIGH
- Zone ceiling applied: min(agent_declared, zone_ceiling) = effective class
- During quiet hours: CRITICAL delivered, HIGH passes through (default config), NORMAL queued in per-zone FIFO, LOW discarded, SILENT passes
- LatestWins zones coalesce queued publishes on quiet hours exit
- Queue depth limit (default 100) drops oldest on overflow
- Quiet hours affect delivery not generation (events still logged/counted)
- Per-agent attention budget (default 20/min) with 80% warning (AttentionBudgetWarningEvent)
- Per-zone attention budget (default 10/min, 30 for Stack-policy)
- Budget exhaustion: mutations proceed, visuals coalesced
- CRITICAL exempt from budget
- SILENT has zero interruption cost
- Earned urgency: warning logged when agent exceeds 4 HIGH/min

## Relevant test scenes
- policy_arbitration_collision (quiet hours + degradation + attention budget interaction)
- chatty_dashboard_touch (attention budget exhaustion, coalescing)
- lease_expiry (CRITICAL lease events bypass everything)
- zone_publish_subtitle (quiet hours queuing of zone events)

## Crate/file location
- InterruptionClass enum: crates/tze_hud_scene/src/events/interruption.rs
- Quiet hours gate: crates/tze_hud_runtime/src/quiet_hours.rs
- Quiet hours queue: crates/tze_hud_runtime/src/quiet_hours/queue.rs
- Attention budget: crates/tze_hud_runtime/src/attention_budget.rs
- Earned urgency tracker: crates/tze_hud_runtime/src/attention_budget/urgency.rs

## v1-mandatory
- Interruption Classification (spec lines 50-66)
- Quiet Hours Enforcement (spec lines 69-89)
- Quiet Hours Queue Semantics (spec lines 92-104)
- Attention Budget Enforcement (spec lines 137-153)
- Earned Urgency Tracking (spec lines 317-324)

## v1-reserved exclusions
None for this bead.

## post-v1 exclusions
- Per-HIGH-class budget enforcement (spec line 319: "deferred to post-v1")
- Inter-Agent Event Bus (spec lines 328-335)
- Advanced Event Routing (spec lines 339-346)

## What this bead does NOT include
- Event envelope structure (bead #1)
- Naming convention validation (bead #1)
- Subscription filtering and delivery mechanics (bead #2)
- Self-event suppression (bead #2)
- Event coalescing under backpressure (bead #2 — this bead does attention budget coalescing only)
- Agent event emission protocol and rate limiting (bead #4)
- tab_switch_on_event (bead #4)
title: Interruption classification and quiet hours

close_reason: PR #133 merged after PR review. Privacy redaction implementation (Shell #4) reviewed and landed on main.
description:
# Privacy Redaction

Implement redaction placeholder rendering that replaces tile content with a neutral pattern when the viewer context does not permit visibility, while preserving layout dimensions and disabling interactive affordances.

## Ownership rule
The shell is the SOLE owner of redaction rendering decisions within the chrome/overlay layer. Redaction is driven by viewer context (owner/household_member/known_guest/unknown/nobody) intersecting with content classification. The agent is never notified that its tile is redacted. Policy-arbitration (Level 2 Privacy Evaluation) determines whether redaction applies; the shell renders the redaction.

## Spec references (system-shell/spec.md)

### Requirement: Redaction Placeholder (line 184)
When a tile's content is redacted due to viewer context, the tile's content area MUST be filled with a neutral placeholder (configurable via [privacy].redaction_style; allowed values: pattern, blank). No agent name, content hint, or icon shown. Interactive affordances (hit regions) disabled while redacted. Agent NOT notified of redaction. When viewer context changes to permit content, placeholder removed and tile content composited normally.
- Scenario: Tile redacted for guest viewer (line 189) — WHEN tile with private classification displayed to viewer with unknown class THEN content replaced with neutral placeholder, hit regions disabled, agent not notified
- Scenario: Redaction removed on viewer change (line 193) — WHEN viewer context changes from unknown to owner THEN redaction placeholder removed and tile's actual content displayed

### Requirement: Agent Isolation for Viewer State (line 232)
(Cross-reference with bead #1) Redaction is silent to agents. Agent cannot detect redaction state.
- Scenario: Agent cannot detect redaction (line 241) — WHEN agent's tile is redacted THEN agent receives no notification and cannot distinguish redacted from non-redacted state

### Requirement: Capture-Safe Redaction Architecture (line 294)
Content and chrome render passes separable. V1 ships overlay-only redaction (capture_surface_active always false). Separation preserved for future render-skip redaction.
- Scenario: Separable render passes (line 299) — WHEN compositor renders frame THEN content and chrome are separate passes

## Secondary spec references

### configuration/spec.md
- Requirement: Privacy Configuration Defaults (line 215) — [privacy] section defines redaction_style (pattern|blank), viewer_detectors (v1-reserved), content_classification defaults

## Redaction behavior details (from prompt)
- Viewer context determines content visibility (owner/household_member/known_guest/unknown/nobody)
- Content exceeding viewer access replaced with neutral pattern (redaction_style: pattern|blank only)
- No agent name, content hint, or icon shown during redaction
- Layout preserved (prevents information leak about content shape)
- Interactive affordances (hit regions) disabled while redacted
- Agent not notified of redaction

## Acceptance criteria
- privacy_redaction_mode test scene passes
- Redacted tiles show pattern or blank (per redaction_style config), no agent content
- No agent name, icon, or content hint visible during redaction
- Layout dimensions preserved (no information leak about content shape)
- Hit regions disabled while redacted
- Agent receives no notification of redaction
- When viewer context changes to permit content, redaction removed and content displayed
- Content and chrome render passes remain separable (overlay-only redaction in v1)
- capture_surface_active is always false in v1

## v1-mandatory requirements
- Redaction Placeholder (line 184)
- Agent Isolation for Viewer State (line 232) — redaction-specific aspect
- Capture-Safe Redaction Architecture (line 294)

## v1-reserved exclusions
- Viewer Identification Pipeline (privacy.viewer_detectors) — schema defined but implementation deferred
- Render-skip redaction — architecturally preserved but not implemented in v1

## post-v1 exclusions
- Render-skip redaction (capture_surface_active support)
- Persistent resource store
- Advanced content classification beyond private/public

## Crate/file location
crates/tze_hud_runtime/src/shell/redaction.rs — redaction placeholder rendering, viewer-context-to-visibility evaluation, hit region disabling, redaction_style configuration

## What this does NOT include
- Chrome layer rendering (bead #1)
- Safe mode state machine (bead #2)
- Freeze semantics (bead #3)
- Disconnection badges (bead #5)
- Viewer identification pipeline (v1-reserved, Epic 7 privacy config)
- Policy arbitration Level 2 Privacy Evaluation logic (Epic 8)
- Configuration loading of [privacy] section (Epic 7, rig-nolr)
title: Shell #4: Privacy redaction

close_reason: Merged via PR #73 — replaced <1KB ambiguity with two independent budgets (< 200 bytes per-tile struct, < 150 bytes per-node struct)
description:
## Problem

The ChatGPT 5.4 Pro external review caught an internal inconsistency in §8 (Scene Graph Capacity Limits). The RFC states:

> Memory budget (target): < 1KB per tile excluding texture data.

But the math immediately below shows:
- Tile struct: ~200 bytes
- Per node avg: ~150 bytes
- 64 nodes/tile: ~9.6 KB

The clarifying note says 'The 1KB/tile target excludes node content (markdown text, texture references) which is accounted via ResourceBudget.texture_bytes. Structural overhead (IDs, bounds, metadata) is the bounded quantity.' But this clarification does not resolve the inconsistency — even structural node overhead (IDs, bounds, enum fields) at 150 bytes times 64 nodes is 9.6KB, not <1KB.

## Why This Matters

- Implementors using this RFC as a specification will not know which number to trust
- Resource budgets feed into degradation policy, telemetry assertions, and performance testing — an order-of-magnitude error propagates
- The 1KB claim appeared in the §10 summary table as a formal requirement

## Sections Affected

- §8 Scene Graph Capacity Limits — the '<1KB per tile' claim and the 9.6KB calculation
- §10 Quantitative Requirements Summary — 'Memory per tile: < 1KB structural overhead'

## Concrete Changes Needed

Either:
(a) Correct the target to reflect reality: ~10KB per tile at max node count (64 nodes), or ~200 bytes per tile with 0 nodes. State that the budget scales linearly with node count.
(b) Redefine what '<1KB' means: if it is truly per-tile excluding all nodes, say '< 1KB per tile struct excluding node tree' and add a separate per-node budget (e.g., '< 200 bytes per node struct excluding content payloads').
(c) Reduce the max_nodes default from 64 to a value where the target holds (e.g., 5 nodes at 150 bytes = 750 bytes + 200 = 950 bytes < 1KB).

Recommendation: option (b) — separate tile-struct budget from node-tree budget, and update §10 to reflect both. This is the most honest and useful for implementors.
title: RFC 0001: Fix memory-budget inconsistency — <1KB claim vs 9.6KB math

description:
validation-framework/spec.md Requirement: Split Latency Budgets (lines 88-99, v1-mandatory).

The validation system SHALL measure and assert three distinct latency contracts independently:
1. input_to_local_ack (p99 < 4ms) — purely local visual feedback, no network
2. input_to_scene_commit (p99 < 50ms) — agent response reflected in scene graph
3. input_to_next_present (p99 < 33ms @ 60Hz) — rendered frame with agent content visible

**What must be built:**
1. Three independent LatencyBucket instances with distinct measurement points
2. Instrumentation hooks at each measurement boundary (Stage 2 completion, scene commit, GPU present)
3. Budget assertion tests for all three independently
4. Telemetry emission distinguishing the three latency types in FrameTelemetry

**Scenarios from spec:**
- WHEN a pointer event arrives THEN local_ack_us is measured at Stage 2 completion (< 4ms p99)
- WHEN an agent responds to an input event with a mutation THEN scene_commit_us is measured at Stage 4 (< 50ms p99)
- WHEN the compositor renders the frame containing the agent's response THEN next_present_us is measured at Stage 7 (< 33ms p99)

**Note:** input_to_local_ack is partially instrumented (existing test_input_to_local_ack_p99_within_budget). The other two measurement points need explicit instrumentation.

**v1-mandatory. Crate: crates/tze_hud_telemetry/, crates/tze_hud_runtime/, examples/vertical_slice/tests/**

title: Implement split latency instrumentation for three input latency contracts

**What must change:**

description:
validation-framework/spec.md Requirement: Soak and Leak Tests (lines 298-310, v1-mandatory).

The validation system SHALL include soak tests that run the runtime under sustained load for extended periods (minimum 1 hour CI, recommended 6 hours nightly) and verify zero resource growth. Post-disconnect cleanup SHALL leave zero resource footprint.

**What must be built:**
1. Soak test harness — sustained multi-agent load generation (3+ agents, continuous mutations, zone publishes, lease cycles)
2. Resource growth assertion — memory/handle tracking over time, assert no monotonic growth
3. Post-disconnect cleanup validation — verify resource footprint returns to zero after agent disconnect
4. CI integration — 1-hour minimum in CI, 6-hour nightly recommended

**Scenarios from spec:**
- WHEN the runtime runs under sustained load for N hours THEN resource usage (memory, handles, textures) shows no monotonic growth
- WHEN all agents disconnect THEN resource footprint returns to baseline within cleanup grace period
- WHEN a lease expires during soak THEN all associated resources are freed completely

**v1-mandatory. Crate: tests/ (soak test binary), crates/tze_hud_telemetry/ (resource tracking)**
